$$APF = \frac{V_{\text{atoms inside unit cell}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3}$$

where $n$ is the number of atoms per unit cell.

## 2.4 Theoretical Density Calculation

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_C \cdot N_A}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of atoms per unit cell
- $A$ = atomic weight (g/mol)
- $V_C$ = volume of the unit cell ($a^3$ for cubic) in $\text{cm}^3$
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = $6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms/mol}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is the bridge between the **microscopic world** (atomic radius, lattice parameter) and the **macroscopic world** (mass, weight, density). It is used to verify if a manufactured alloy has the correct composition through simple density measurements.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| BCC: $R$ vs $a$ | $R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}$ | $a$ in nm, $R$ in nm |
| FCC: $R$ vs $a$ | $R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$ | $a$ in nm, $R$ in nm |
| HCP ideal ratio | $\dfrac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3}} \approx 1.633$ | Dimensionless |
| Atomic Packing Factor | $APF = \dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3}$ | Max value = 0.7405 (FCC/HCP) |
| Theoretical Density | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_C \cdot N_A}$ | $\rho$ in $\text{g/cm}^3$ |
| Interplanar Spacing (cubic) | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$ | $d$ in nm |
| Planar Density | $PD = \dfrac{\text{No. of atoms centered on plane}}{\text{Area of plane}}$ | atoms / $\text{nm}^2$ |
| Linear Density | $LD = \dfrac{\text{No. of atoms centered on direction vector}}{\text{Length of direction vector}}$ | atoms / nm |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Steel Manufacturing:** Understanding that iron transitions from BCC ($\alpha$-Fe, ferrite) to FCC ($\gamma$-Fe, austenite) at **912 °C** allows metallurgists to control grain size, hardness, and ductility through heat treatment.
2. **Semiconductor Industry:** Silicon and Germanium have the **Diamond Cubic** structure — knowing this helps in wafer slicing along specific crystallographic planes.
3. **Aerospace Alloys:** Titanium's HCP structure limits its slip systems, making alloying elements (like Al and V) necessary to improve formability.
4. **Precious Metal Catalysis:** Pt and Pd (FCC) are preferred as catalysts because their close-packed $\{111\}$ planes offer optimal atomic spacing for chemical reactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Atomic Radius to Lattice Parameter for FCC

**Step 1 — Identify the atom contact direction in FCC.**
In an FCC structure, atoms touch each other along the **face diagonal**. The face diagonal of length $a\sqrt{2}$ contains: one corner atom ($R$), one face-center atom ($2R$), and the other corner atom ($R$).

**Step 2 — Set up the geometric relation.**
The total length of the face diagonal equals the sum of radii along the diagonal:

$$a\sqrt{2} = R + 2R + R = 4R$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R$.**

$$\boxed{R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Atomic Packing Factor for FCC

**Step 1 — Number of atoms per FCC unit cell.**
- 8 corner atoms $\times \frac{1}{8}$ each $= 1$
- 6 face-centered atoms $\times \frac{1}{2}$ each $= 3$
- **Total $n = 4$ atoms**

**Step 2 — Volume of 4 atoms (spheres).**

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = 4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi R^3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$ into $V_{\text{atoms}}$.**

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{16}{3}\pi \left(\frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^3$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{atoms}} &= \frac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{a^3 \cdot (\sqrt{2})^3}{64} \\
&= \frac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{a^3 \cdot 2\sqrt{2}}{64} \\
&= \frac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Volume of the cubic unit cell.**

$$V_C = a^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute APF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APF &= \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_C} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}}{a^3} \\
&= \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{6} \\
APF &\approx 0.74
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: APF for BCC

**Step 1 — Atom contact direction.**
In BCC, atoms touch along the **body diagonal** of length $a\sqrt{3}$: corner ($R$) + center ($2R$) + corner ($R$) = $4R$.

$$a\sqrt{3} = 4R \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Atoms per BCC cell.** $n = 2$ (8 corners $\times \frac{1}{8}$ + 1 center).

**Step 3 — Compute APF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APF &= \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3} = \frac{\dfrac{8}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $R^3 = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^3}{64}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
APF &= \frac{8}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^3}{64 \cdot a^3} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{8} \\
APF &\approx 0.68
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of BCC, FCC, HCP

| Parameter | BCC | FCC | HCP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atoms / unit cell | 2 | 4 | 6 (or 2 primitive) |
| APF | 0.68 | 0.74 | 0.74 |
| Coordination No. | 8 | 12 | 12 |
| Slip planes | Many | Many | Limited (basal) |
| Typical behavior | Less ductile | Very ductile | Moderately ductile |
| Common metals | $\alpha$-Fe, Cr, W | Cu, Al, Ni, Au | Mg, Zn, Ti |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Theoretical Density of Copper

**Given:** FCC structure, $R = 0.1278 \; \text{nm}$, $A = 63.5 \; \text{g/mol}$.

**Step 1 — Find lattice parameter $a$.**

$$a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.1278}{1.414} = 0.3615 \; \text{nm} = 3.615 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Unit cell volume.**

$$V_C = a^3 = (3.615 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 4.725 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Number of atoms per FCC cell** $n = 4$.

**Step 4 — Apply density formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{n \cdot A}{V_C \cdot N_A} = \frac{4 \times 63.5}{4.725 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\
&= \frac{254}{28.45} \\
\rho &\approx 8.93 \; \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the **measured** density of copper ($\approx 8.94 \; \text{g/cm}^3$), validating the formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Crystal Structure Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRYSTALLOGRAPHY] --> B[Seven Crystal Systems]
    A --> C[14 Bravais Lattices]
    A --> D[Common Metallic Structures]
    
    B --> B1[Cubic]
    B --> B2[Tetragonal]
    B --> B3[Hexagonal]
    B --> B4[Others]
    
    C --> C1[Simple Primitive P]
    C --> C2[Body Centered I]
    C --> C3[Face Centered F]
    C --> C4[Base Centered C]
    
    D --> D1[BCC - alpha Fe, W, Cr]
    D --> D2[FCC - Cu, Al, Au, Ni]
    D --> D3[HCP - Mg, Zn, Ti]
    
    D1 --> P1[APF 0.68 CN 8]
    D2 --> P2[APF 0.74 CN 12]
    D3 --> P3[APF 0.74 CN 12]
    
    P1 --> E[Engineering Properties]
    P2 --> E
    P3 --> E
    
    E --> E1[Ductility and Strength]
    E --> E2[Electrical Conductivity]
    E --> E3[Slip Systems and Formability]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Calculating the Atomic Packing Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify Structure Type] --> S2{Is it BCC, FCC, or HCP?}
    S2 -- BCC --> S3[Use R = a x sqrt 3 over 4]
    S2 -- FCC --> S4[Use R = a x sqrt 2 over 4]
    S2 -- HCP --> S5[Use R = a over 2 and c over a = 1.633]
    S3 --> S6[Count atoms n]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Compute V_atoms = n x 4/3 x pi x R cubed]
    S7 --> S8[Compute V_cell = a cubed]
    S8 --> S9[APF = V_atoms divided by V_cell]
    S9 --> S10[Report APF as decimal 0 to 1]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map: From Atoms to Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atom Type Fe Cu Al] --> A2[Crystal Structure FCC BCC HCP]
    A2 --> A3[Lattice Parameter a]
    A2 --> A4[Atomic Packing Factor]
    A2 --> A5[Slip Systems]
    A3 --> B1[Theoretical Density]
    A4 --> B2[Mechanical Strength]
    A5 --> B3[Ductility and Formability]
    B1 --> C1[Material Selection in Design]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on diagrams:** Physical atomic arrangement diagrams (showing corner/face/body atoms as spheres) are most accurately drawn using 3D CAD software or rendered directly in textbooks. The Mermaid block-level schematics above map the *logical interactions* between crystal structure concepts, which is the analytical equivalent required for KTU 2024 examination answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Coordination Number and Atomic Packing Factor. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The **coordination number (CN)** is the number of nearest-neighbor atoms surrounding a particular atom in a crystal structure. For example, in FCC, each atom is in direct contact with 12 neighbors, hence CN = 12.

The **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)** is defined as the ratio of the volume of atoms contained within a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell itself. Mathematically:

$$APF = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}}$$

APF is a dimensionless quantity, with a theoretical maximum of **0.74** for FCC and HCP, and **0.68** for BCC. It quantifies the efficiency of atomic packing and directly influences material density and mechanical behavior.

### Question 2: Differentiate between BCC and FCC structures with suitable examples. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Property | BCC | FCC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atoms / unit cell | 2 | 4 |
| Coordination No. | 8 | 12 |
| APF | 0.68 | 0.74 |
| Relation $R$-$a$ | $4R = a\sqrt{3}$ | $4R = a\sqrt{2}$ |
| Examples | $\alpha$-Fe, Cr, W, Mo | Cu, Al, Au, Ag, $\gamma$-Fe |
| Ductility | Less ductile | Highly ductile |

BCC structures have a lower packing efficiency and fewer slip systems, making them generally stronger but less formable. FCC structures are close-packed, more ductile, and ideal for applications requiring extensive plastic deformation (e.g., copper wiring, aluminum sheets).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the expression for the Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of an FCC crystal structure. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State structure and atom count.**
FCC has $n = 4$ atoms per unit cell (8 corner atoms $\times \frac{1}{8}$ + 6 face atoms $\times \frac{1}{2}$).

**Step 2 — Establish geometric relationship.**
Atoms touch along the face diagonal:

$$4R = a\sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute volume of atoms.**

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi R^3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R$.**

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{16}{3}\pi \left(\frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Compute APF.**

$$APF = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_C} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}}{a^3} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 0.74$$

**(b) Calculate the theoretical density of aluminum (FCC), given $R = 0.143 \; \text{nm}$ and atomic weight $A = 26.98 \; \text{g/mol}$. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute lattice parameter $a$.**

$$a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.143}{1.414} = 0.4046 \; \text{nm} = 4.046 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute unit cell volume.**

$$V_C = a^3 = (4.046 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 6.625 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Identify atoms per cell.**
FCC: $n = 4$.

**Step 4 — Apply density formula.**

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_C \cdot N_A}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{4 \times 26.98}{6.625 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\
&= \frac{107.92}{39.9} \\
\rho &\approx 2.704 \; \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

The calculated value closely matches the experimental density of aluminum ($\approx 2.70 \; \text{g/cm}^3}$), confirming the accuracy of the FCC model.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the HCP crystal structure. Derive the ideal $c/a$ ratio for HCP. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Describe HCP arrangement.**
The HCP structure consists of three layers of atoms: the bottom layer (A), middle layer (B), and top layer (A again), creating an ABABAB stacking sequence. In the conventional cell:
- 12 corner atoms (each shared by 6 cells) = 3
- 2 face-center atoms (top and bottom) = 1
- 3 mid-plane atoms = 3
- **Total = 6 atoms** (or 2 in the primitive cell)

**Step 2 — Establish geometric relations.**
The base of the hexagonal cell is a rhombus with side $a$. The mid-plane atoms sit in tetrahedral voids. The center-to-corner atom distance in the base is simply $a$, which equals $2R$, so $a = 2R$.

**Step 3 — Compute vertical height.**
The vertical height between the two close-packed layers (centers of touching atoms) in the HCP arrangement is:

$$h = \sqrt{\left(\frac{2R}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 - R^2} = \sqrt{\frac{4R^2}{3} - R^2} = R\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}$$

Wait, the more rigorous derivation uses the tetrahedral geometry. The center-to-center distance between the atom at the base and the atom above the void is $2R$. The horizontal offset is $\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$.

$$(2R)^2 = \left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 + h^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $c$.**
The total cell height is $c = 2h$ (two tetrahedral layers):

$$4R^2 = \frac{a^2}{3} + \frac{c^2}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Derive $c/a$ ratio.**
Substituting $a = 2R$ (so $a^2 = 4R^2$):

$$4R^2 = \frac{4R^2}{3} + \frac{c^2}{4}$$

$$\frac{c^2}{4} = 4R^2 - \frac{4R^2}{3} = \frac{8R^2}{3}$$

$$c^2 = \frac{32R^2}{3} = \frac{32 \cdot (a/2)^2}{3} = \frac{8a^2}{3}$$

$$\frac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} \approx 1.633$$

**(b) Copper has an FCC structure with atomic radius $R = 0.1278 \; \text{nm}$ and atomic weight $A = 63.54 \; \text{g/mol}$. Calculate its theoretical density and the planar density on the (111) plane. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Theoretical Density:**

**Step 1 — Lattice parameter.**

$$a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.1278}{1.414} = 0.3615 \; \text{nm} = 3.615 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of cell.**

$$V_C = a^3 = 4.726 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply density formula with $n = 4$ for FCC.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{4 \times 63.54}{4.726 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\
&= \frac{254.16}{28.46} \\
\rho &\approx 8.93 \; \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — Planar Density on (111):**

**Step 1 — Identify atoms on the (111) plane in FCC.**
The (111) plane in FCC is a close-packed plane containing a triangular arrangement of atoms. Within the planar equilateral triangle:
- 3 corner atoms $\times \frac{1}{6}$ each = $\frac{1}{2}$
- 3 edge-center atoms $\times \frac{1}{2}$ each = $\frac{3}{2}$
- **Total = 2 atoms**

**Step 2 — Area of the equilateral triangle.**
The triangle has side length $a\sqrt{2}/2$ (since atoms touch along face diagonals):

$$\text{Side} = a\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$$

$$\text{Area} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \frac{2a^2}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{3} a^2}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Planar density.**

$$PD_{(111)} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3} a^2}{8}} = \frac{16}{\sqrt{3}\, a^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $a = 0.3615 \; \text{nm}$.**

$$PD_{(111)} = \frac{16}{1.732 \times (0.3615)^2} = \frac{16}{1.732 \times 0.1307} = \frac{16}{0.2264} \approx 70.7 \; \text{atoms/nm}^2$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** $R$ in nm must be converted to cm when calculating density. A common error is using $R$ in nm and $N_A$ in $\text{mol}^{-1}$ without converting, leading to a density off by a factor of $10^{21}$!
> 2. **Wrong atom count:** Students frequently write $n = 4$ for BCC (correct is 2) or $n = 2$ for FCC (correct is 4). Memorize: BCC = 2, FCC = 4, HCP = 6.
> 3. **Using $a$ instead of $4R$ in face diagonal:** For FCC, the diagonal contains $R + 2R + R = 4R$, not $2R$. This is a favorite trick question.
> 4. **Mixing up Miller Indices and Miller-Bravais:** HCP uses 4-axis (hkil) notation; cubic systems use 3-axis (hkl). KTU 2024 expects both as per Module 2 syllabus.
> 5. **No units in final answer:** Always write $R$ in nm, $a$ in nm, $\rho$ in $\text{g/cm}^3$, and APF as a dimensionless decimal. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Seven Crystal Systems** are: Cubic, Tetragonal, Orthorhombic, Hexagonal, Rhombohedral, Monoclinic, Triclinic.
- **The 14 Bravais Lattices** are formed by adding centering conditions (P, I, F, C) to the 7 systems.
- **BCC:** $n = 2$, CN = 8, APF = 0.68, $4R = a\sqrt{3}$.
- **FCC:** $n = 4$, CN = 12, APF = 0.74, $4R = a\sqrt{2}$.
- **HCP:** $n = 6$, CN = 12, APF = 0.74, ideal $c/a = 1.633$.
- **Density formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_C \cdot N_A}$ — always convert nm → cm for $V_C$.
- **Miller Indices** for planes: take reciprocals of intercepts, reduce to smallest integers, enclose in parentheses $(hkl)$.
- **Miller Indices** for directions: use coordinates of a vector, reduce, enclose in square brackets $[uvw]$.
- **APF is dimensionless** and bounded between 0 and 1; **0.74 is the theoretical maximum** for identical spheres in 3D.
- **Iron polymorphism:** $\alpha$-Fe (BCC, stable below 912 °C) → $\gamma$-Fe (FCC, 912–1394 °C) → $\delta$-Fe (BCC, above 1394 °C). This is the basis of all steel heat treatment.
- **APF determines slip system availability** — higher APF (FCC/HCP) → more slip planes → better ductility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel and Its Alloys: Composition and Heat Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Steel and Its Alloys: Composition and Heat Treatment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Steel? **Steel** is an alloy of **iron (Fe)** and **carbon (C)** where the carbon content is restricted to less than **2.0 wt%**, with manganese (up to ~1.65%), silicon, phosphorus, sulfur, and other residual/trace elements. It is the most widely used engineering metallic material on the planet, primarily because its mechanical properties can be dramatically altered by controlling its **carbon content**, **alloying additions**, and **heat treatment cycles**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Steel is an iron-carbon alloy containing typically **0.02% to 2.0% C**, together with unavoidable impurities (Mn, Si, P, S) and intentional alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo, V, W, etc.) added to tailor mechanical, physical, and chemical properties. ## 1.2 Why Carbon Content Matters — The Conceptual Analogy Think of steel like a **reinforced concrete structure**: - **Iron (Fe)** is the concrete — soft, ductile, but inherently weak. - **Carbon (C)** atoms are the steel rebar — they slip into the spaces between iron atoms (interstitially) and act as **pinning points** that block dislocation motion. - More carbon = more pinning = **stronger but more brittle** material. > **The sweet spot for engineering steel: 0.1% to 1.0% C.** > Below 0.1% C → too soft (rivets, panels). > Above 1.0% C → too brittle for most structural uses (cutting tools, files only). ## 1.3 Classification of Steels (KTU 2024 Mandate) | **Classification Basis** | **Categories** | **Typical Application** | |--------------------------|----------------|--------------------------| | By **Carbon Content** | Low-C (≤ 0.25%), Medium-C (0.25–0.6%), High-C (0.6–1.0%), Ultra-High-C (>1.0%) | Structural, machinery, tools, dies | | By **Alloying** | Carbon Steel, Low-Alloy Steel (<5% alloying), High-Alloy Steel (≥5% alloying) | Bridges, gears, stainless parts | | By **Quality / Deoxidation** | Rimmed, Killed, Semi-killed, Capped | Sheet metal, forging stock | | By **Application** | Structural, Tool, Stainless, Heat-Resistant, Maraging | Bridges, dies, cutlery, turbines | > [!NOTE] > **Stainless Steel** is essentially a high-alloy steel containing ≥ 10.5% **Chromium (Cr)**, which forms a passive Cr₂O₃ layer preventing rust. ## 1.4 Heat Treatment — The Intuition Heat treatment is essentially **time-temperature choreography** for the atomic structure. We control: 1. **How fast** we heat the steel (austenitization). 2. **How long** we hold it at temperature (soaking). 3. **How fast** we cool it (quenching rate). 4. **What happens afterwards** (tempering, aging). By orchestrating these, we manipulate the internal crystal structure (BCC ↔ FCC ↔ BCT martensite) to obtain the desired **hardness, toughness, strength, and ductility**. > **Analogy:** Heat treatment is like **baking a cake** — the same ingredients (steel composition) can produce vastly different products (chocolate cake, sponge, bread) depending on the **temperature profile and cooling schedule** you apply. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Iron–Iron Carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) Phase Diagram — Core Knowledge The Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram is the **Rosetta Stone** of steel metallurgy. Every heat treatment decision flows from understanding it. ### Key Invariant Reactions | **Reaction** | **Temperature (°C)** | **Phases** | **Name** | |--------------|----------------------|------------|----------| | Peritectic | 1493 | L + δ → γ (Austenite) | Peritectic | | Eutectic | 1147 | L → γ + Fe₃C | Ledeburite formation | | Eutectoid | **727** | γ → α + Fe₃C | **Pearlite formation** | | Curie | 770 | Magnetic transition (no phase change) | Magnetic transformation | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **eutectoid point at 727 °C and 0.77% C** is the most critical reference. Steels with **< 0.77% C** are called **hypoeutectoid**; those with **> 0.77% C** are **hypereutectoid**. ## 2.2 Effects of Common Alloying Elements | **Element** | **Symbol** | **Effect on Steel** | |-------------|------------|---------------------| | Manganese | Mn | Increases hardenability, deoxidizer, forms MnS (reduces brittleness from S) | | Silicon | Si | Deoxidizer, increases strength and elastic limit (spring steel) | | Chromium | Cr | Increases hardness, wear, corrosion resistance (≥10.5% → stainless) | | Nickel | Ni | Promotes toughness, impact resistance, austenite stabilizer | | Molybdenum | Mo | Reduces temper brittleness, increases hot hardness, creep resistance | | Vanadium | V | Forms V₄C₃ — refines grain, increases strength | | Tungsten | W | Hot hardness, high-speed tool steels | | Cobalt | Co | High-speed steels, magnetic alloys | | Sulfur | S | Free-machining (↑Mn counteracts embrittlement) | | Phosphorus | P | Increases strength, but causes cold shortness (avoid!) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Heat Treatment & Hardenability | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Use** | |------------------------|----------------|---------| | Martensite fraction (Koistinen–Marburger) | $f_{m} = 1 - \exp\bigl[-\alpha(M_{s} - T_{q})\bigr]$ | Predict % martensite at quench temperature $T_q$ | | Constant α (carbon steels) | $\alpha \approx 0.011$ (for $M_s$ in °C) | Empirical coefficient | | Critical cooling rate | $V_{CR} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{700 \to 500}}{t_{nose}}$ | Minimum rate to avoid pearlite nose | | Ideal diameter (Grossmann) | $D_{I} = $ diameter fully hardening at infinite quench | Hardenability measure | | Jominy distance | $J = f(D_I, \text{severity of quench})$ | Empirical correlation for hardness vs distance | | Tempering parameter (Hollomon–Jaffe) | $H = T(\log t + C)$ | Predicts hardness after tempering | | Carbon → Hardness rule | $\text{HRC} \approx 50 \cdot (\%C) \text{ (approx., martensite)}$ | Quick estimate (within limits) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb:** Each additional alloying element typically shifts the **TTT diagram "nose"** to the right (longer times), **increasing hardenability**. Only Co shifts it to the left. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive crankshafts** → through-hardened low-alloy steels (AISI 4140) using oil quenching. - **Gears** → carburizing grade steels (AISI 8620) — soft core, hard surface. - **Cutting tools** → high-speed steels (M2, T1) with W, Mo, V, Co — retain hardness at 600 °C. - **Railroad rails** → high-carbon pearlitic steel (eutectoid 0.77% C) for wear resistance. - **Pressure vessels** → normalized C-Mn steel for uniform toughness. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Koistinen–Marburger Martensite Transformation Equation **Problem Setup:** A steel specimen with a martensite start temperature $M_s$ is quenched to a temperature $T_q < M_s$. Determine the **volume fraction of martensite** $f_m$ formed. ### Step 1 — Empirical Foundation Koistinen and Marburger (1956) established experimentally that the transformation follows first-order kinetics driven by **undercooling** $\Delta T = M_s - T_q$. ### Step 2 — Differential Equation Form The rate of martensite formation is proportional to the *remaining untransformed austenite*: $$\frac{df_m}{dT} = -\alpha (1 - f_m)$$ Here, $\alpha$ is a kinetic constant that depends on alloy composition, and the negative sign indicates austenite is being consumed as temperature decreases. ### Step 3 — Separation of Variables Rearrange to separate $f_m$ and $T$: $$\frac{df_m}{1 - f_m} = -\alpha \, dT$$ ### Step 4 — Integration Integrate from the start condition (at $T = M_s$, $f_m = 0$) to a general quench temperature $T$: $$\int_{0}^{f_m} \frac{df}{1 - f} = -\alpha \int_{M_s}^{T} dT$$ The left integral yields $-\ln(1 - f_m)$, and the right integral yields $-\alpha(T - M_s)$. $$-\ln(1 - f_m) = -\alpha(T - M_s)$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $f_m$ Multiply both sides by $-1$ and exponentiate: $$\ln(1 - f_m) = -\alpha(M_s - T)$$ $$1 - f_m = \exp\bigl[-\alpha(M_s - T)\bigr]$$ $$\boxed{f_m = 1 - \exp\bigl[-\alpha(M_s - T)\bigr]}$$ ### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example A **plain carbon steel** has $M_s = 200\ ^\circ C$ and is quenched to room temperature $T_q = 25\ ^\circ C$ in water. Use $\alpha = 0.011\ ^\circ C^{-1}$. **Step 6a:** Compute undercooling: $$\Delta T = M_s - T_q = 200 - 25 = 175\ ^\circ C$$ **Step 6b:** Substitute into the Koistinen–Marburger equation: $$f_m = 1 - \exp[-(0.011)(175)]$$ $$f_m = 1 - \exp(-1.925)$$ $$f_m = 1 - 0.1459$$ $$\boxed{f_m \approx 0.854 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 85.4\% \text{ martensite}}$$ > The remaining 14.6% is **retained austenite** — a common issue addressed by **cryogenic treatment** (sub-zero cooling) or **tempering**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Heat Treatment Process Comparison | **Process** | **Austenitization Temp (°C)** | **Cooling Medium** | **Resulting Microstructure** | **Hardness (HRC)** | **Primary Purpose** | |-------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------| | Full Anneal | Ac₃ + 30–50 (≈ 840) | Furnace (slow) | Coarse Pearlite + Ferrite | 15–25 | Softening, machinability | | Normalizing | Ac₃ + 50 (≈ 880) | Air (moderate) | Fine Pearlite + Ferrite | 20–30 | Refine grain, uniform structure | | Spheroidizing | Just below Ac₁ (≈ 700) | Very slow cool | Spheroidite (globular Fe₃C) | 10–20 | Maximum softness, deep drawing | | Quench Hardening | Ac₃ + 30–50 (≈ 850) | Water / Oil / Brine | **Martensite** (+ Retained γ) | 55–65 (max) | Maximum hardness | | Tempering (low) | 150–250 | Air | Tempered martensite | 55–60 | Reduce brittleness, retain hardness | | Tempering (high) | 400–650 | Air | Tempered martensite (coarse Fe₃C) | 25–35 | Toughness, ductility | | Austempering | Ac₃ + 30 | Salt bath (260–370) | **Bainite** | 40–55 | Toughness + hardness, no temper needed | | Martempering | Ac₃ + 30 | Salt bath (just above M_s) | Martensite (low residual stress) | 55–60 | Distortion control | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Surface Hardening Techniques | **Process** | **Mechanism** | **Case Depth** | **Core Hardness** | **Typical Steel** | |-------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Carburizing** | Diffuse C into surface (gas/pack/liquid) | 0.5–4 mm | Soft (low C core) | Low-C steels (AISI 1018, 8620) | | **Nitriding** | Diffuse N at 500–550 °C (no quench) | 0.1–0.7 mm | Unchanged | Nitralloy (Al, Cr, Mo steels) | | **Cyaniding** | Cyanide salt bath (C + N) | 0.1–0.5 mm | Soft | Low-C steels | | **Induction** | Rapid eddy-current heating + quench | 1–5 mm | Soft | Medium-C steels | | **Flame** | Oxy-acetylene flame + quench | 2–6 mm | Soft | Large parts | | **Laser / Electron Beam** | Localized surface melting | 0.5–2 mm | Soft | Selective hardening zones | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Steels (Tree Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A[STEEL ALLOYS] --> B[Carbon Steel] A --> C[Alloy Steel] A --> D[Stainless Steel] A --> E[Tool Steel] A --> F[Special Steels] B --> B1[Low Carbon below 0.25 percent] B --> B2[Medium Carbon 0.25 to 0.6 percent] B --> B3[High Carbon 0.6 to 1.0 percent] B --> B4[Ultra High Carbon above 1.0 percent] C --> C1[Low Alloy below 5 percent] C --> C2[High Alloy above 5 percent] D --> D1[Austenitic 300 series] D --> D2[Ferritic 400 series] D --> D3[Martensitic 400 series] D --> D4[Duplex] E --> E1[Water Hardening W] E --> E2[Shock Resistant S] E --> E3[High Speed HSS] E --> E4[Hot Work H] E --> E5[Cold Work O A D] F --> F1[Maraging Steels] F --> F2[Hadfield Manganese Steel] F --> F3[TRIP Steels] F --> F4[Dual Phase Steels] ``` ## 4.2 Heat Treatment Process Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR P1[Raw Steel] --> P2[Austenitization
Heat to 800 to 900 C] P2 --> P3{Hold at
soaking temp} P3 --> P4[Cooling Stage] P4 --> Q1[Furnace Cooling
ANNEALING] P4 --> Q2[Air Cooling
NORMALIZING] P4 --> Q3[Water or Oil Quench
HARDENING] P4 --> Q4[Brine Quench
SHARP QUENCH] P4 --> Q5[Salt Bath Hold
AUSTEMPERING] Q1 --> R1[Soft Pearlite] Q2 --> R2[Fine Pearlite] Q3 --> R5[Brittle Martensite] Q5 --> R6[Bainite] R5 --> R7[Reheat 150 to 650 C] R7 --> R3[Tempered Martensite] R7 --> R4[Tempering Cycle] R3 --> R4 R1 --> Z1[Final Component] R2 --> Z1 R3 --> Z1 R4 --> Z1 R6 --> Z1 ``` ## 4.3 TTT Diagram Schematic (Block Representation) ```mermaid graph TD S1[Austenite at T above A1] --> S2[Quench to Nose
approx 550 C] S2 --> S3{Time at Nose} S3 -- short -- S4[Avoid Nose] S3 -- long -- S5[Cross Nose] S4 --> S6[Continue Cooling] S6 --> S7[Hit Mf Line
form Martensite] S5 --> S8[Transform to
Fine Pearlite] S7 --> S9[Reheat 200 to 600 C] S9 --> S10[Tempered Martensite] S8 --> S11[Cool to Room Temp] S11 --> S12[Coarse Pearlite] ``` ## 4.4 Surface Hardening Selection Architecture ```mermaid graph TD SH[Surface Hardening Required] --> SH1{Desired Case
Depth} SH1 -- shallow below 1 mm -- SH2[Nitriding or Induction] SH1 -- medium 1 to 3 mm -- SH3[Carburizing or Laser] SH1 -- deep above 3 mm -- SH4[Flame Hardening] SH2 --> SH2A{Operating
Temp} SH2A -- low below 500 C -- SH2B[Induction Quench] SH2A -- high above 500 C -- SH2C[Nitriding with Nitralloy] SH3 --> SH3A{Carbon Source} SH3A -- gas -- SH3B[Gas Carburizing] SH3A -- liquid -- SH3C[Liquid Carburizing] SH3A -- pack -- SH3D[Pack Carburizing] SH4 --> SH4A[Flame Hardening
then Quench] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between Annealing and Normalizing. Why is normalizing preferred for medium carbon steels in engineering practice?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Annealing** | **Normalizing** | |------------|---------------|------------------| | Cooling medium | Furnace (very slow, ~30 °C/hr) | Air (moderate, ~150 °C/min) | | Microstructure | Coarse pearlite | Fine pearlite | | Hardness | Lower | Higher (~10–20% greater) | | Grain size | Coarse | Refined | **Why preferred for medium-carbon steels (1 mark):** Normalizing refines the grain and produces a **more uniform, fine-grained structure** that combines **higher strength with adequate ductility**, ideal for components like shafts, axles, and gears used as-machined. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is Retained Austenite? How does it affect the properties of quenched steel, and how is it controlled?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Retained austenite is the fraction of **face-centered cubic (FCC) γ-iron that fails to transform into martensite** during quenching because the quench was halted above the M_f temperature, leaving it metastable at room temperature. **Effects (1 mark):** Retained austenite lowers hardness, causes **dimensional instability** (expands on transformation during service), and may lead to **delayed cracking**. **Control (1 mark):** - Perform **cryogenic treatment** (sub-zero cooling in liquid nitrogen, ~ −196 °C) to push transformation past M_f. - Use **tempering** (reheating 150–200 °C) to allow some austenite to transform to bainite/martensite. - Reduce carbon content (lower $M_s$ → less retained austenite, but at cost of hardness). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a labeled sketch, explain the **Iron–Iron Carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) phase diagram**. Discuss the **eutectoid reaction** and its significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Hardenability**? Explain the **Jominy End-Quench Test** in detail and discuss how it is used to compare the hardenability of different steels. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)** **Iron–Iron Carbide Phase Diagram (3 marks):** The Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram is plotted with **temperature (°C)** on the Y-axis and **carbon content (wt %)** on the X-axis, ranging from pure iron (0% C) to iron carbide (6.67% C, Fe₃C). **Key regions and lines (sketch description, 2 marks):** - **δ-ferrite (BCC)** — high-temperature stable phase, exists from 1394 °C to 1538 °C. - **γ-austenite (FCC)** — stable between 727 °C and 1394 °C; non-magnetic; can dissolve up to 2.0% C. - **α-ferrite (BCC)** — stable below 912 °C; soft, magnetic; dissolves max 0.022% C at 727 °C. - **Fe₃C (cementite)** — hard, brittle intermetallic compound (6.67% C). - **A₁ (727 °C)** — eutectoid temperature. - **A₃** — upper critical (γ ↔ α + γ). - **A_cm** — solubility limit of C in austenite. - **Eutectic point C (4.3% C, 1147 °C)** — forms ledeburite. - **Eutectoid point S (0.77% C, 727 °C)** — forms pearlite. **Eutectoid Reaction & Significance (2 marks):** $$\gamma_{(0.77\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{727\,^\circ C} \alpha_{(0.022\%\,C)} + Fe_3C$$ On slow cooling through 727 °C, the single-phase austenite transforms simultaneously into **two distinct phases**: soft α-ferrite and hard Fe₃C cementite, forming the lamellar aggregate called **pearlite**. **Significance:** - Defines the **boundary between hypoeutectoid (< 0.77% C) and hypereutectoid (> 0.77% C) steels**. - Forms the basis for **pearlitic structures** that govern the strength of most structural steels. - The cooling rate relative to 727 °C determines whether pearlite, bainite, or martensite forms. --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)** **Hardenability (2 marks):** **Hardenability** is the ability of a steel to form **martensite** when quenched, measured by the **depth of hardening** obtained under specified cooling conditions. It is **NOT** the same as hardness — it indicates the **rate of hardness penetration**. > **Distinction:** A steel can be very hard at the surface but only shallow-hardenable (e.g., plain carbon 1040) or moderately hard but deeply hardenable (e.g., alloyed 4340). **Jominy End-Quench Test (3 marks):** **Procedure:** 1. A standard cylindrical specimen (Ø 25.4 mm × 100 mm long) is **austenitized** at the appropriate Ac₃ + 30 °C. 2. It is quickly transferred to a **fixture** where a **jet of water at 24 °C** impinges on the **bottom face** only. 3. After full quenching to room temperature, **parallel flats are ground** along the length. 4. **Rockwell C hardness is measured** at intervals of 1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 mm from the quenched end. 5. A **Jominy curve** (hardness vs. distance from quenched end, called *Jominy distance*) is plotted. **Interpretation (2 marks):** - **Quenched end (J = 0 mm):** Highest hardness, corresponds to the **critical cooling rate**. - **As J increases:** Cooling rate decreases; hardness drops as **martensite gives way to bainite, then pearlite**. - The shape of the Jominy curve is a **fingerprint of the steel's hardenability**. - **Alloy steels** maintain hardness to greater J distances → **higher hardenability** than plain carbon steels. > **Hardenability comparison:** AISI 4340 (Ni-Cr-Mo) maintains ~50 HRC up to 30 mm, whereas AISI 1040 drops to ~30 HRC within 10 mm. Therefore 4340 is far more hardenable. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Alternative]** **(a)** Explain **Tempering** of quenched steel. Discuss the **structural changes and property variations** during tempering stages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **Austempering and Martempering** processes. State their advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)** **Tempering (3 marks):** **Tempering** is a **sub-critical heat treatment** in which a quenched (martensitic) steel is reheated to a temperature **below the eutectoid temperature (A₁ = 727 °C)**, held for a specified time, then cooled at a suitable rate. The primary aim is to **reduce brittleness**, relieve residual stresses, and obtain a **favorable combination of strength, hardness, and toughness**. **Structural Changes During Tempering Stages (3 marks):** | **Stage** | **Temperature (°C)** | **Microstructural Change** | |-----------|----------------------|----------------------------| | Stage 1 (Precipitation) | 100–250 | Formation of **ε-carbide (Fe₂.₄C)**; transition carbides precipitate from supersaturated martensite. | | Stage 2 (Retained γ Decomposition) | 200–300 | Retained austenite decomposes to **bainite/ferrite + carbide**. | | Stage 3 (Carbide Replacement) | 250–400 | ε-carbide replaced by **cementite (Fe₃C)**; martensite tempered. | | Stage 4 (Grain Boundary Carbides) | 400–700 | Coarsening of Fe₃C, grain growth; **secondary hardening** in Mo/V/W steels. | **Property Variation (1 mark):** - As tempering temperature **increases**: hardness and strength **decrease**; ductility and toughness **increase**. - **Secondary hardening peak** observed in high-alloy tool steels due to precipitation of alloy carbides (V₄C₃, Mo₂C). --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B(b)** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Austempering** | **Martempering** | |------------|------------------|------------------| | Process | Quench into salt bath **260–370 °C**, hold until transformation complete | Quench into salt bath **just above M_s**, hold briefly, then air-cool through M_s–M_f | | Transformation product | **Bainite** | **Martensite** (tempered afterwards) | | Cooling sequence | Direct isothermal hold at bainite range | Interrupted quench, then final cool | | Hardness (HRC) | 40–55 | 55–60 | | Distortion | Very low | Low (vs. direct quench) | | Cracking risk | Negligible | Low | | Need for tempering | **No** (bainite is stable) | **Yes** (temper martensite afterwards) | | Salt bath medium | Mixture of nitrates / nitrites (KNO₃, NaNO₃) | Same (also chloride baths) | **Advantages, Limitations, Applications (2 marks):** **Austempering:** - *Advantages:* Excellent toughness + hardness; minimal distortion; no quench cracks. - *Limitations:* Limited to thin sections; expensive salt baths; requires long holding times. - *Applications:* Springs, clutch plates, agricultural tools, chains. **Martempering:** - *Advantages:* Reduced distortion vs. direct quench; uniform hardness; less cracking. - *Limitations:* Still requires tempering; salt bath must be precisely temperature-controlled; medium section thickness. - *Applications:* Gears, dies, cutting tools, firearm components. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Hardness ≠ Hardenability** — students often confuse these. Hardness is a *measured property at a point*; hardenability is a *depth-of-hardening capability* (often confused in numerical problems). > 2. **Pearlite forms at 727 °C, not room temperature** — a common mark-killing error in phase diagram questions. > 3. **Tempering reduces hardness but increases toughness** — never claim "tempering increases hardness" (it does only in *secondary hardening* of high-alloy tool steels, not plain-carbon steels). > 4. **Martempering still produces martensite** — it is *not* bainitic. A frequent error in short answers. > 5. **Forgetting to include the eutectoid reaction equation** $\gamma \to \alpha + Fe_3C$ in phase diagram answers — the board examiner almost always allocates marks for it explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - **Eutectoid point:** 0.77% C, 727 °C — pearlite forms here. - **Hypoeutectoid steels** (< 0.77% C): ferrite + pearlite. - **Hypereutectoid steels** (> 0.77% C): cementite network + pearlite. - **Martensite** is BCT (Body-Centered Tetragonal), hard and brittle. - **Retained austenite** → cryogenic treatment or tempering to control. - **Annealing** = slow cooling, soft, coarse pearlite. - **Normalizing** = air cooling, fine pearlite, refined grain. - **Quenching** = water/oil/brine, forms martensite. - **Tempering** = reheating below 727 °C, relieves stress, trades hardness for toughness. - **Austempering** = bainite, no tempering needed. - **Martempering** = martensite, tempered afterwards, less distortion. - **Hardenability ≠ Hardness** — hardenability refers to depth, hardness to surface value. - **Jominy test** is the standard KTU-referenced hardenability test. - **Alloying elements** (Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn, Si) shift the TTT nose rightward → improve hardenability (except Co). - **Surface hardening methods:** carburizing, nitriding, induction, flame, laser. - **Koistinen–Marburger equation** for martensite fraction is a high-yield KTU problem. - **TTT diagram nose** = critical cooling rate determinant. - **Tool steels** require W, Mo, V, Co for hot hardness. - **Stainless steels** need ≥ 10.5% Cr to form a passive oxide layer. - **Maraging steels** achieve ultra-high strength through precipitation hardening of Ni₃(Ti, Mo, Al) in a low-carbon martensitic matrix. - **Free-machining steels** use high S + Mn to form MnS inclusions (AISI 12L14). - **Austenitizing temperature** is typically **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C** for hypoeutectoid steels.

topics.title: Types of Steel: Carbon Steel, Alloy Steel, Stainless Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Steel: Carbon Steel, Alloy Steel, Stainless Steel # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Steel? **Steel** is an alloy of **iron (Fe)** and **carbon (C)** in which the carbon content typically ranges from **0.02% to 2.14%** by weight, with manganese (up to ~1.65%) and small amounts of other elements. Beyond 2.14% carbon, the alloy is classified as **cast iron**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Steel is a ferrous alloy whose primary interstitial alloying element is carbon, whose mechanical properties are predominantly derived from the allotropic transformation of iron (BCC austenite to BCC/BCT martensite) and the formation of iron carbide (Fe₃C / cementite). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Family" Think of steel as a **family of recipes** based on the same "dough" (iron + carbon). Just like a chef can add salt, sugar, or chocolate to change the taste: - **Adding only flour and water** = Plain carbon steel (basic, no frills). - **Adding chocolate chips, nuts, or raisins** = Alloy steel (special ingredients give special properties). - **Adding preservatives that resist spoilage** = Stainless steel (chromium makes it "rust-proof"). The base ingredients (iron + carbon) remain, but the **additions change behaviour drastically**. ## 1.3 The Three Principal Types of Steel (KTU Module 2 Focus) | Type | Defining Feature | Primary Distinguishing Element | |---|---|---| | Carbon Steel | Only Fe + C + Mn (no deliberate alloying) | Carbon (0.02 – 2.14 %) | | Alloy Steel | Deliberate addition of alloying elements | Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo, V, Si > 1.65 % total | | Stainless Steel | High chromium for passive oxide film | Cr ≥ 10.5 % | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 specifically mandates understanding the classification, composition, properties, and applications of these three families of steel, with explicit attention to the effect of alloying elements on the Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram and resulting mechanical behaviour. ## 1.4 Why Engineers Care - **Construction:** Reinforcement bars, structural beams (carbon steel). - **Automobile:** Crankshafts, gears, axles (alloy steel). - **Food/Medical:** Surgical instruments, kitchenware (stainless steel). - **Aerospace & Power:** Turbine shafts, boilers, pressure vessels (alloy + stainless). Without proper selection, components fail by **corrosion, fatigue, wear, or brittle fracture**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Classification Logic ### A. Carbon Steel (Plain Carbon Steel) Steel whose properties are determined almost entirely by its **carbon content**. Manganese and silicon are present only as residual deoxidizers (Mn ≤ 1.65 %; Si ≤ 0.6 %). **Sub-classification by carbon content:** | Sub-type | Carbon (%) | Typical Properties | Typical Uses | |---|---|---|---| | Low Carbon (Mild) Steel | 0.05 – 0.25 | Soft, ductile, weldable, machinable | Structural shapes, sheets, wire, nails | | Medium Carbon Steel | 0.25 – 0.60 | Stronger, harder, less ductile; responds to heat treatment (quench + temper) | Railway tracks, axles, gears, crankshafts | | High Carbon Steel | 0.60 – 1.0 | Very hard, strong, low ductility; used in hardened condition | Springs, cutting tools, chisels, blades | | Ultra-High Carbon Steel | 1.0 – 2.0 | Extremely hard and brittle | Punches, dies, knives, axles | **Effect of Carbon on Properties:** - ↑ Carbon ⇒ ↑ Hardness, ↑ Tensile strength, ↓ Ductility, ↓ Weldability - Each 0.1 % C raises tensile strength by roughly **70 – 100 MPa**. ### B. Alloy Steel Deliberate addition of alloying elements (total > 1.65 % or specific elements like Ni, Cr, Mo added in significant amounts) to **enhance mechanical, physical, or chemical properties**. **Role of Common Alloying Elements:** | Element | Effect on Steel | Industrial Reason for Addition | |---|---|---| | Mn (Manganese) | Increases hardenability, deoxidizes | Sulfur fixation (MnS), prevents hot shortness | | Ni (Nickel) | Promotes toughness, austenite stabilizer | Low-temperature service, impact resistance | | Cr (Chromium) | Increases hardness, wear & corrosion resistance | Cutting tools, ball bearings | | Mo (Molybdenum) | Reduces temper brittleness, high-temp strength | Boiler steels, turbine rotors | | V (Vanadium) | Forms hard carbides, grain refiner | Tool steels, springs | | W (Tungsten) | High red-hardness, abrasion resistance | High-speed tool steels (HSS) | | Co (Cobalt) | High red-hardness, magnetic properties | Permanent magnets, HSS | | Si (Silicon) | Increases strength, magnetic permeability | Electrical steels (transformer cores) | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the effect of alloying elements on Fe lattice:** > **"NiSTi CAFe"** — **Ni** (opens γ-austenite), **C** (opens γ), **N** (opens γ), **M**n (opens γ slightly), **Cr**, **Al**, **Si**, **W**, **Mo**, **V**, **Ti**, **P**, **Sn**, **Sb** (close γ-austenite, favour α-ferrite). **Sub-classification of Alloy Steel:** 1. **Low Alloy Steel:** Total alloying < 5 % (e.g., 4140, 4340) 2. **High Alloy Steel:** Total alloying > 5 % (e.g., tool steels, stainless steels) ### C. Stainless Steel A high-alloy steel containing a **minimum of 10.5 % chromium** (by mass) that forms a passive, self-healing Cr₂O₃ film on the surface, providing exceptional corrosion resistance. **Why Chromium is Magical:** $$3\text{Fe} + 4\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3^{\text{passive}} + 3\text{FeO}$$ This **inert oxide layer** is only a few nanometres thick, yet stops oxygen and water from reaching the underlying iron — preventing rust. **The Four Major Families of Stainless Steel:** | Family | Microstructure | Typical Cr / Ni Content | Properties | Typical Uses | |---|---|---|---|---| | Austenitic (300-series) | FCC γ-austenite | Cr 18 %, Ni 8 % (e.g., SS 304) | Non-magnetic, ductile, corrosion-resistant, not hardenable by heat treatment | Kitchen sinks, chemical tanks, food processing | | Ferritic (400-series) | BCC α-ferrite | Cr 11 – 18 %, no Ni (e.g., SS 430) | Magnetic, moderate corrosion resistance, formable | Automotive trim, exhaust systems | | Martensitic (400-series) | BCT martensite | Cr 12 – 18 %, low/no Ni (e.g., SS 410) | Hardenable by heat treatment, magnetic, hard, strong | Cutlery, surgical tools, turbine blades | | Duplex (Austenitic + Ferritic) | Mixed γ + α | Cr 22 – 25 %, Ni 5 – 7 %, Mo 3 % | Strength + corrosion resistance combined | Offshore, chemical, marine applications | > [!IMPORTANT] > **AISI Naming Convention (often asked in KTU):** > - **3xx** = Austenitic (Cr + Ni), e.g., **304** (18/8), **316** (with Mo) > - **4xx** = Ferritic or Martensitic (Cr only) > - **2xx** = Austenitic with Mn + N replacing Ni ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the **key quantitative relationships** frequently asked in KTU ESE questions on types of steel. No vertical pipes have been used inside cell content to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\%C = \dfrac{\text{Mass of C}}{\text{Total Mass of Steel}} \times 100$ | Carbon content definition | wt. %, governs classification | | 2 | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 300 + 700 \times \%C$ | Approx. ultimate tensile strength vs. carbon (for plain C-steel) | MPa, valid 0.1 % \leq C \leq 0.8 % | | 3 | $\%EL \approx 50 - 40 \times \%C$ | Approx. elongation vs. carbon | % elongation, decreases with C | | 4 | $T_{eutectoid} = 727\,^\circ C$ | Eutectoid temperature in Fe–Fe₃C | $C_{eutectoid} = 0.76\%$ C | | 5 | $C_{max\ soluble\ in\ \gamma} = 2.14\%$ | Max. C in austenite (Eutectic point) | Defines steel vs. cast iron boundary | | 6 | $C_{max\ soluble\ in\ \alpha} = 0.022\%$ | Max. C in ferrite at $727\,^\circ C$ | At RT: $\approx 0.008\%$ C | | 7 | $n_{FCC\ austenite} = 4\ atoms / cell$, $a \approx 3.56\ \text{Å}$ | Austenite crystal data | FCC, dense | | 8 | $n_{BCC\ ferrite} = 2\ atoms / cell$, $a \approx 2.86\ \text{Å}$ | Ferrite crystal data | BCC, less dense | | 9 | $\%Cr_{stainless} \geq 10.5\%$ | Minimum Cr for "stainless" classification | wt. %, AISI standard | | 10 | $\text{Pitting Resistance Equivalent} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | PRE number for stainless steels | Dimensionless; higher = better pitting resistance | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Carbon steel** dominates by volume (bridges, buildings, rails) due to cost. - **Alloy steel** is selected when **performance** is critical (jet engines, gears, dies). - **Stainless steel** is mandatory when **corrosion, hygiene, or aesthetics** matter (implants, food processing, marine). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Effect of Carbon on Mechanical Properties **Step 1:** The Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram shows that increasing carbon content progressively increases the **hardness-contributing phase**, which is **cementite (Fe₃C)**. **Step 2:** The weight fraction of cementite in a fully annealed (100 % pearlite) steel at the eutectoid composition can be derived using the **lever rule**: $$W_{Fe_3C} = \frac{C_{0} - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022}$$ where $C_0$ = carbon content of the steel, and 6.70 % = carbon content of pure cementite. **Step 3:** For a typical medium-carbon steel with $C_0 = 0.40\%$ (e.g., AISI 1040): $$W_{Fe_3C} = \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022} = \frac{0.378}{6.678} \approx 0.0566 = 5.66\%$$ **Step 4:** Interpretation: **~5.66 % of the microstructure is the hard, brittle Fe₃C phase**, while the remaining 94.34 % is soft, ductile α-ferrite. The composite hardness $H_{alloy}$ lies between $H_{ferrite} \approx 80\ \text{HV}$ and $H_{Fe_3C} \approx 1000\ \text{HV}$, given by a **rule of mixtures** (approximation): $$H_{alloy} \approx W_{\alpha} \cdot H_{\alpha} + W_{Fe_3C} \cdot H_{Fe_3C}$$ $$H_{alloy} \approx 0.9434 \times 80 + 0.0566 \times 1000 \approx 132\ \text{HV}$$ **Step 5:** Compare with mild steel ($C_0 = 0.10\%$): $$W_{Fe_3C} = \frac{0.10 - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022} = \frac{0.078}{6.678} \approx 0.0117 = 1.17\%$$ $$H_{alloy} \approx 0.9883 \times 80 + 0.0117 \times 1000 \approx 91\ \text{HV}$$ **Conclusion:** Doubling carbon from 0.10 % to 0.40 % almost **1.45× the hardness** by increasing the volume fraction of cementite. ## 3.2 Derivation — Effect of Chromium on Corrosion Resistance (Stainless Steel) **Step 1:** The minimum Cr content for passivation is 10.5 %. Above this threshold, oxygen exposure forms a continuous **Cr₂O₃** film of thickness $\sim 2\ \text{nm}$. **Step 2:** Using the **Pilling–Bedworth (P-B) ratio** for the protective oxide: $$PB = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{Cr_2O_3} \cdot \rho_{Cr}}{2 \cdot M_{Cr} \cdot \rho_{Cr_2O_3}}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting: - $M_{Cr_2O_3} = 152\ \text{g/mol}$, $\rho_{Cr_2O_3} = 5.22\ \text{g/cm}^3$ - $M_{Cr} = 52\ \text{g/mol}$, $\rho_{Cr} = 7.19\ \text{g/cm}^3$ $$PB = \frac{152 \times 7.19}{2 \times 52 \times 5.22} = \frac{1092.88}{542.88} \approx 2.01$$ **Step 4:** A P-B ratio between **1 and 2** indicates a **protective, adherent oxide layer**. Cr₂O₃ satisfies this criterion perfectly — the film is compressive and self-healing. **Step 5:** Thus, $\%Cr \geq 10.5\%$ guarantees the formation of a **passive Cr₂O₃ film** that blocks further oxidation, making the steel "stainless". ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Parameter | Low Carbon Steel | Medium Carbon Steel | High Carbon Steel | Alloy Steel | Austenitic SS (304) | Martensitic SS (410) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | % Carbon | 0.05 – 0.25 | 0.25 – 0.60 | 0.60 – 1.0 | 0.20 – 0.60 | \leq 0.08 | 0.10 – 0.40 | | UTS (MPa) | 350 – 500 | 550 – 850 | 850 – 1200 | 700 – 1500 | 500 – 750 | 500 – 1700 | | Hardness (HB) | 100 – 150 | 150 – 250 | 250 – 400 | 200 – 600 | 150 – 200 | 150 – 600 (heat-treated) | | % Elongation | 30 – 40 | 15 – 25 | 5 – 15 | 10 – 30 | 40 – 60 | 10 – 25 | | Weldability | Excellent | Fair | Poor | Good (low alloy) / Fair (high) | Excellent | Poor | | Heat Treatable | No (case) | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | | Magnetic | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | | Corrosion Resistance | Low | Low | Low | Low – Medium | Excellent | Good | | Cost (Relative) | 1.0× | 1.1× | 1.2× | 1.5 – 3× | 4 – 6× | 3 – 4× | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Pitting Resistance Equivalent (PRE) **Problem:** Compare SS 304 (18 % Cr, 8 % Ni) with SS 316 (17 % Cr, 12 % Ni, 2 % Mo, 0.05 % N) for marine use. **Step 1:** Apply the PRE formula: $$PRE = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$$ **For SS 304:** $$PRE_{304} = 18 + 3.3(0) + 16(0) = 18$$ **For SS 316:** $$PRE_{316} = 17 + 3.3(2) + 16(0.05) = 17 + 6.6 + 0.8 = 24.4$$ **Step 2:** Interpretation: - SS 304: $PRE = 18$ (suitable for mild environments, not chloride-rich). - SS 316: $PRE = 24.4$ (recommended for marine, chemical, and chloride exposure). **Conclusion:** The **2 % Mo addition in SS 316** raises its pitting resistance by **~35 %**, justifying its use in aggressive environments at a modest cost premium. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Steels (Mermaid Tree Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[STEEL
Fe plus C 0.02 to 2.14 percent]:::root A --> B[CARBON STEEL
No deliberate alloying]:::cs A --> C[ALLOY STEEL
Deliberate alloying elements]:::as A --> D[STAINLESS STEEL
Cr greater than 10.5 percent]:::ss B --> B1[Low Carbon
0.05 to 0.25 percent C]:::cs B --> B2[Medium Carbon
0.25 to 0.60 percent C]:::cs B --> B3[High Carbon
0.60 to 1.0 percent C]:::cs B --> B4[Ultra High Carbon
1.0 to 2.0 percent C]:::cs C --> C1[Low Alloy
Total alloy less than 5 percent]:::as C --> C2[High Alloy
Total alloy greater than 5 percent]:::as C1 --> C1a[Ni Cr Mo steels]:::as C1 --> C1b[Spring steels]:::as C1 --> C1c[Bearing steels]:::as C2 --> C2a[Tool steels HSS]:::as C2 --> C2b[Die steels]:::as D --> D1[Austenitic 300 series
FCC non magnetic]:::ss D --> D2[Ferritic 400 series
BCC magnetic]:::ss D --> D3[Martensitic 400 series
BCT hardenable]:::ss D --> D4[Duplex
Mixed FCC plus BCC]:::ss D1 --> D1a[SS 304 18 by 8]:::ss D1 --> D1b[SS 316 with Mo]:::ss D2 --> D2a[SS 430]:::ss D3 --> D3a[SS 410]:::ss D3 --> D3b[SS 420 cutlery]:::ss classDef root fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef cs fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef as fill:#fff2e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef ss fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Selection of Steel Type ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Service Requirements
Input Parameters]:::in --> PROC{Decision Engine
Selection Logic}:::logic PROC --> C1[Strength Priority]:::cs PROC --> C2[Corrosion Priority]:::ss PROC --> C3[Special Property Priority]:::as C1 --> O1[Carbon Steel
Mild Medium or High C]:::csout C2 --> O2[Stainless Steel
Austenitic or Duplex]:::ssout C3 --> O3[Alloy Steel
Ni Cr Mo V etc]:::asout O1 --> APP1[Construction Rails
Nails Structural Beams]:::app O2 --> APP2[Marine Food Medical
Chemical Reactors]:::app O3 --> APP3[Aerospace Gears
Turbines Tooling]:::app APP1 --> OUT[Engineered Component]:::final APP2 --> OUT APP3 --> OUT classDef in fill:#fffacd,stroke:#8b7500,color:#000 classDef logic fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 classDef cs fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef ss fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef as fill:#fff2e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef csout fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef ssout fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef asout fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef app fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef final fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Cr₂O₃ Passive Film Formation ```mermaid flowchart TD ENV[Atmospheric Oxygen plus Moisture]:::env --> A[Cr in Steel Surface
greater than 10.5 percent]:::cr A --> B[Oxidation Reaction
4Cr plus 3O2 yields 2Cr2O3]:::rxn B --> C[Self Healing Cr2O3 Film
Thickness 1 to 3 nm]:::film C --> D{Is Film Damaged?}:::decision D -->|Yes| E[Underlying Cr Reacts
with Oxygen Restores Film]:::repair E --> C D -->|No| F[Steel Remains Protected
No Rust Formation]:::ok classDef env fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef cr fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef rxn fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef film fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 classDef repair fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef ok fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Differentiate between low carbon steel, medium carbon steel, and high carbon steel on the basis of carbon content and typical applications.** **Model Answer:** | Steel Type | Carbon Content | Key Properties | Typical Applications | |---|---|---|---| | Low Carbon (Mild) Steel | 0.05 – 0.25 % | Soft, ductile, excellent weldability, low hardness | Structural beams, sheets, wire, nails, automobile body panels | | Medium Carbon Steel | 0.25 – 0.60 % | Higher strength and hardness, moderate ductility, responds to heat treatment (Q&T) | Railway tracks, axles, gears, crankshafts, connecting rods | | High Carbon Steel | 0.60 – 1.0 % | Very high hardness and strength, low ductility, poor weldability | Springs, chisels, cutting tools, blades, punches | Increasing carbon content **↑ hardness, ↑ tensile strength, ↓ ductility, ↓ weldability**. *(3 marks: 1 mark for carbon ranges, 1 mark for properties, 1 mark for applications.)* --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Why is chromium essential for stainless steel? What is the minimum Cr content for a steel to be classified as stainless?** **Model Answer:** Chromium is the **key alloying element** in stainless steel because, when present in sufficient quantity, it forms a thin, **passive, self-healing oxide film of Cr₂O₃** on the steel surface. This film is only 1–3 nm thick but is **dense, adherent, and chemically inert**, preventing oxygen and moisture from reaching the underlying iron and thus protecting the steel from rusting and corrosion. If the film is mechanically scratched, the **exposed underlying Cr reacts immediately with atmospheric oxygen** to re-form the passive layer, making the protection **self-healing**. As per the **AISI standard**, a steel must contain a **minimum of 10.5 % chromium** by mass to be classified as "stainless". *(3 marks: 1 mark for Cr₂O₃ film formation, 1 mark for self-healing, 1 mark for 10.5 % minimum.)* --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):** > - Students often **omit comparison tables** — a single comparative table fetches **2–3 extra marks** effortlessly. > - **Never** mix up austenitic (non-magnetic) and martensitic (magnetic) stainless steels. > - **Effect of alloying elements** must be linked to specific **properties** (e.g., Ni → toughness, Cr → hardness & corrosion). Vague statements lose marks. > - Always mention **percentages and temperatures** with units. Numerical quantifiers (10.5 % Cr, 727 °C, 0.76 % C) score higher in KTU valuations. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEMET721 Module 2]** **(a)** Classify the different types of steels based on their carbon content and alloying additions. Explain the effect of carbon content on the mechanical properties of plain carbon steel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the classification, composition, microstructure, and properties of **stainless steel** in detail. Compare austenitic, ferritic, and martensitic stainless steels. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Classification of Steels and Effect of Carbon on Properties (7 marks):** Steels are primarily classified into three major families based on alloying additions: **1. Carbon Steel (Plain Carbon Steel):** Contains iron, carbon (0.02 – 2.14 %), manganese (≤ 1.65 %), and silicon (≤ 0.6 %) as residuals. No deliberate alloying elements. **Sub-classification:** | Type | Carbon (%) | Applications | |---|---|---| | Low carbon | 0.05 – 0.25 | Sheets, structural shapes, wire | | Medium carbon | 0.25 – 0.60 | Gears, rails, axles | | High carbon | 0.60 – 1.0 | Springs, cutting tools | | Ultra-high carbon | 1.0 – 2.0 | Dies, punches, blades | **2. Alloy Steel:** Deliberate addition of alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, V, W, etc.) to enhance properties like strength, toughness, hardness, wear resistance, and corrosion resistance. **3. Stainless Steel:** Contains ≥ 10.5 % Cr; corrosion-resistant due to passive Cr₂O₃ film. **Effect of Carbon on Properties of Plain Carbon Steel:** - **Hardness & Strength:** ↑ C ⇒ ↑ Hardness (HV), ↑ Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS). Each 0.1 % C raises UTS by ~70–100 MPa. - **Ductility:** ↑ C ⇒ ↓ % Elongation, ↓ % Reduction in Area. - **Weldability:** ↑ C ⇒ ↓ Weldability (more cracking risk in HAZ). - **Machinability:** Improves up to ~0.20 % C, then decreases. - **Heat Treatability:** Only steels with C > 0.30 % can be effectively hardened by quenching. - **Brittleness:** ↑ C ⇒ ↑ Brittleness. This monotonic relationship arises because increasing C increases the volume fraction of **hard, brittle cementite (Fe₃C)** in the microstructure (as derived in Section 3.1). *(7 marks: 2 for classification, 4 for effect of C, 1 for concluding statement.)* --- **(b) Stainless Steel — Classification, Composition, Microstructure, Properties (7 marks):** Stainless steel is a high-alloy steel containing **minimum 10.5 % chromium**, which forms a self-healing Cr₂O₃ passive film that provides corrosion resistance. **Four Major Families:** **1. Austenitic Stainless Steel (e.g., SS 304, 316):** - Composition: 16–26 % Cr, 6–22 % Ni, low C (≤ 0.08 %) - Microstructure: **FCC γ-austenite** (non-magnetic) - Properties: Excellent corrosion resistance, high ductility, excellent formability & weldability, not hardenable by heat treatment, non-magnetic - Uses: Kitchenware, chemical tanks, food processing, marine **2. Ferritic Stainless Steel (e.g., SS 430):** - Composition: 11–18 % Cr, no Ni or very low Ni - Microstructure: **BCC α-ferrite** (magnetic) - Properties: Magnetic, moderate corrosion resistance, good formability, not hardenable by heat treatment - Uses: Automotive trim, exhaust systems, architectural panels **3. Martensitic Stainless Steel (e.g., SS 410, 420):** - Composition: 12–18 % Cr, low/no Ni, higher C (0.1–0.4 %) - Microstructure: **BCT martensite** (magnetic, hardenable) - Properties: Magnetic, high strength & hardness (achievable by quenching & tempering), moderate corrosion resistance - Uses: Cutlery, surgical instruments, turbine blades, valves **4. Duplex Stainless Steel:** - Composition: 22–25 % Cr, 5–7 % Ni, 3 % Mo - Microstructure: **Mixed austenite + ferrite** (~50:50) - Properties: Excellent strength (twice that of austenitic), good corrosion resistance (especially to stress corrosion cracking) - Uses: Offshore platforms, chemical plants **Comparative Table:** | Property | Austenitic | Ferritic | Martensitic | |---|---|---|---| | Magnetic | No | Yes | Yes | | Hardenable | No | No | Yes | | Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Good | Moderate | | Ductility | Excellent | Good | Fair | | Weldability | Excellent | Good | Poor | | Cost | High | Moderate | Moderate | **Why Cr ≥ 10.5 %:** Below this, the passive film is discontinuous and the underlying Fe corrodes. At and above 10.5 %, a continuous Cr₂O₃ film forms, satisfying the Pilling–Bedworth criterion (PB ≈ 2.0) for a protective, adherent oxide layer (as derived in Section 3.2). *(7 marks: 1 for definition + Cr threshold, 2 for the four families, 2 for comparison, 2 for application/microstructure discussion.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEMET721 Module 2]** **(a)** What are alloy steels? Explain in detail the role of the following alloying elements: Manganese, Nickel, Chromium, Molybdenum, and Vanadium. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of the Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram, explain the **eutectoid reaction** and its significance in the heat treatment of steels. Discuss how alloying elements shift the eutectoid composition and temperature. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Alloy Steels and Role of Alloying Elements (7 marks):** **Definition:** Alloy steels are steels in which deliberate additions of alloying elements (other than carbon) are made in significant amounts (typically > 1.65 % total) to improve or develop specific mechanical, physical, or chemical properties. **Role of Each Element:** **1. Manganese (Mn):** - Acts as a **deoxidizer and desulfurizer** during steelmaking. - Combines with sulfur to form **MnS**, preventing hot shortness (cracking at high temperatures). - Slightly increases **hardenability** (depth of hardening). - Austenite stabilizer (opens γ-field). - Typical addition: 0.5 – 1.8 % in mild steel, up to 12 % in Hadfield steel. **2. Nickel (Ni):** - Strong **austenite stabilizer** (enlarges γ-field, may suppress α entirely). - Promotes **toughness and impact resistance**, especially at sub-zero temperatures. - Enhances **corrosion resistance** (used with Cr in austenitic SS). - Typical addition: 1 – 5 % in low alloy; 8 – 12 % in 300-series stainless. **3. Chromium (Cr):** - Strong **ferrite stabilizer** (closes γ-field). - Forms **hard, wear-resistant carbides** (Cr₂₃C₆, Cr₇C₃) → improves wear resistance. - Provides **corrosion and oxidation resistance** (Cr₂O₃ passive film at ≥ 10.5 %). - Improves hardenability. - Typical addition: 0.5 – 2 % in low alloy; 12 – 30 % in stainless. **4. Molybdenum (Mo):** - Strong **carbide former** (Mo₂C, MoC). - **Reduces temper brittleness** in Ni–Cr steels. - Improves **high-temperature strength and creep resistance**. - Refines grain size. - Typical addition: 0.2 – 0.5 % in low alloy; up to 3 – 5 % in tool and die steels. **5. Vanadium (V):** - Strong **carbide and nitride former** (V₄C₃, VN). - Very strong **grain refiner** — fine grains improve toughness. - Provides **secondary hardening** during tempering. - Typical addition: 0.1 – 0.3 % in tool and spring steels. **Mnemonic for γ-stabilizers (open γ-field):** Ni, Co, Mn, C, N, Cu. **Mnemonic for α-stabilizers (close γ-field):** Cr, Al, Si, W, Mo, V, Ti, P. *(7 marks: 1 for definition, 1 mark each for the 5 elements × 1 = 5, 1 for concluding summary.)* --- **(b) Eutectoid Reaction in Fe–Fe₃C Diagram and Effect of Alloying (7 marks):** **Eutectoid Reaction:** The eutectoid reaction in the Fe–Fe₃C system occurs at **T = 727 °C** and **C = 0.76 %**: $$\gamma\ (FCC\ austenite,\ 0.76\%\ C) \xrightarrow{cooling} \alpha\ (BCC\ ferrite,\ 0.022\%\ C) + Fe_3C\ (cementite,\ 6.70\%\ C)$$ This decomposition produces a **lamellar microstructure of alternating ferrite and cementite** known as **pearlite** (named for its mother-of-pearl appearance under a microscope). **Significance in Heat Treatment:** - The eutectoid temperature (727 °C, often called **A₁** or the lower critical temperature) is the **foundation of all heat treatment operations**: - **Annealing:** Heat above A₁ (typically 30–50 °C above 727 °C), hold, slow cool → soft, coarse pearlite. - **Normalizing:** Heat above A₃ (upper critical), air cool → fine pearlite, improved strength. - **Hardening (Quenching):** Heat above A₃, rapid quench → **martensite** (BCT, very hard, brittle). - **Tempering:** Reheat martensite below A₁ → tough, less brittle tempered martensite. - Plain carbon steels with **0.76 % C** are **eutectoid steels**; lower C → **hypoeutectoid**; higher C → **hypereutectoid**. **Effect of Alloying Elements on Eutectoid:** - **γ-stabilizers (Ni, Mn, Co):** Lower the eutectoid temperature and shift the eutectoid composition to **lower C** values. - **α-stabilizers (Cr, Mo, W, V, Si, Al):** Raise the eutectoid temperature and shift the eutectoid composition to **higher C** values. - Alloying elements **depress the eutectoid temperature** (or raise it) and alter the carbon content at which pearlite forms. - Alloying elements also **slow down the diffusion-controlled transformation** of austenite to pearlite, shifting the TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) curve to the right → **increased hardenability**. This is why alloy steels can be hardened in thick sections using milder quenchants (oil or air) instead of water. *(7 marks: 2 for eutectoid reaction, 2 for heat treatment significance, 2 for effect of alloying on eutectoid, 1 for hardenability.)* --- ## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Types of Steel** - **Steel** = Fe + C (0.02 to 2.14 %) + residual Mn, Si. - **Cast Iron** begins at **> 2.14 % C**. - **Carbon Steel sub-types:** Low (≤ 0.25 %), Medium (0.25 – 0.60 %), High (0.60 – 1.0 %), Ultra-high (1.0 – 2.0 %). - **Alloy Steel:** Deliberate alloying > 1.65 %; **Stainless Steel:** Cr ≥ **10.5 %** by AISI standard. - **Eutectoid reaction** at **727 °C**, **0.76 % C** → austenite → pearlite (α + Fe₃C). - **Eutectic reaction** at **1147 °C**, **4.30 % C** → L → γ + Fe₃C. - **Austenite (γ)** = FCC, dense, non-magnetic; **Ferrite (α)** = BCC; **Martensite (α')** = BCT, hard, brittle. - **γ-stabilizers (open γ-field):** Ni, Co, Mn, C, N, Cu. - **α-stabilizers (close γ-field):** Cr, Al, Si, W, Mo, V, Ti, P. - **Austenitic SS (300-series):** Non-magnetic, FCC, not hardenable, best corrosion resistance. - **Martensitic SS (400-series):** Magnetic, BCT, hardenable, best strength. - **Ferritic SS (400-series):** Magnetic, BCC, not hardenable, moderate corrosion resistance. - **Duplex SS:** Mixed γ + α, ~50:50, high strength + corrosion resistance. - **Cr forms Cr₂O₃ passive film** (Pilling–Bedworth ratio ≈ 2.0) — protective and self-healing. - **Hardenability ↑ with alloying** (TTT curve shifts right); hence alloy steels are preferred for thick sections. - **AISI naming:** 3xx = austenitic (Cr + Ni); 4xx = ferritic/martensitic (Cr only); 2xx = austenitic with Mn. - **PRE (Pitting Resistance Equivalent):** %Cr + 3.3 %Mo + 16 %N; higher PRE → better pitting resistance. - **Mnemonic for 300 vs 400 series:** "**3**o**0** = a**U**stenitic (3 sounds like 'U')", "**4**0**0** = F or M (Magnetic)." > **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation!** 🚀

topics.title: Non-Ferrous Metals and Alloys: Aluminum, Copper, Titanium, Nickel Alloys
Applications and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Domain: Applications and Properties of Engineering Materials

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Engineering Materials*, the topic **"Applications and Properties"** constitutes the foundational bridge between the microscopic structure of a material and its macroscopic engineering utility. Every engineering component — from a turbine blade in a jet engine to a microchip in a smartphone — is selected based on a strict quantitative analysis of its **mechanical, thermal, electrical, magnetic, optical, and chemical properties**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> **Material Properties** are the measurable, intrinsic characteristics that govern the response of a material to applied external stimuli (force, heat, electric field, magnetic field, light, or chemical environment). **Applications** refer to the engineered deployment of a material based on a direct, optimized mapping of these properties to a specific functional requirement.

In simpler terms: *Properties tell us what a material **CAN DO**, and applications tell us where we **SHOULD USE IT**.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Material Job Interview"

Imagine every engineering material is a candidate applying for a job.

- **The CV (Properties):** Lists the candidate's strengths — tensile strength (can lift heavy loads?), conductivity (can carry electricity?), hardness (can resist scratches?).
- **The Job Role (Application):** The company (engineer) reads the CV and places the candidate in the most suitable position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Mapping:**
> - A material with **high electrical conductivity (CV strength)** → gets the "job" of **electrical wiring** (application).
> - A material with **high hardness and wear resistance** → gets the "job" of **cutting tool** (application).
> - A material with **low density and high strength** → gets the "job" of **aerospace structural component** (application).

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Material Properties

KTU 2024 Scheme groups material properties into **five fundamental categories**, each tied directly to specific engineering applications:

| # | Property Category | Stimulus | Primary Engineering Use |
|---|------------------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | Force / Load | Structural components, machine parts |
| 2 | **Thermal** | Heat / Temperature | Heat exchangers, insulators, turbines |
| 3 | **Electrical** | Electric field / Current | Conductors, semiconductors, insulators |
| 4 | **Magnetic** | Magnetic field | Motors, transformers, data storage |
| 5 | **Chemical / Optical** | Chemical agents / Light | Corrosion-resistant parts, optical lenses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, students are frequently asked to *list* the categories of properties and give **one example application** of each. Memorize this 5-category framework.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants (Must Memorize)

The following constants form the **quantitative backbone** of property calculations:

- **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$
- **Density of Water (reference):** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Resistivity of Copper:** $\rho_{Cu} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8} \text{ }\Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- **Curie Temperature of Iron:** $T_C = 770 \text{ °C}$ (i.e., **1043 K**)
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4\text{)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties — The Structural Backbone

Mechanical properties define how a material responds to **applied forces**. These are the **most frequently tested** properties in KTU board exams.

### 2.1.1 Stress ($\sigma$)
The internal resisting force per unit area developed inside a material when an external load is applied.

### 2.1.2 Strain ($\varepsilon$)
The fractional change in dimension (length, volume, or shape) of the material due to applied stress.

### 2.1.3 The Master Equation: Hooke's Law

For the **linear elastic region** of the stress-strain curve, stress is directly proportional to strain. This is governed by **Young's Modulus (E)**, the slope of the elastic portion of the curve.

### 2.1.4 Toughness vs. Brittleness

- **Toughness:** Total energy absorbed per unit volume before fracture. Represented by the **total area under the stress-strain curve**.
- **Brittleness:** Opposite of toughness. Brittle materials (like ceramics and cast iron) fracture with little plastic deformation.

### 2.1.5 Hardness

Resistance of a material to **localized plastic deformation** (scratches, indentation, abrasion). Common scales: **Brinell, Rockwell, Vickers, Mohs**.

### 2.1.6 Fatigue

Failure of a material under **repeated cyclic loading**, even when the maximum stress is well below the ultimate tensile strength. Critical for **shafts, bridges, aircraft wings**.

## 2.2 Thermal Properties — The Heat Handlers

### 2.2.1 Thermal Conductivity ($k$)
Rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient. Metals like **copper ($k \approx 400 \text{ W/mK}$)** are excellent conductors; polymers and ceramics are insulators.

### 2.2.2 Coefficient of Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)
Fractional change in length per unit change in temperature. Critical for **bimetallic strips, railway tracks, glass-to-metal seals**.

### 2.2.3 Specific Heat Capacity ($c$)
Energy required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of material by **1 K** (or 1 °C). Critical in heat treatment and thermal storage.

## 2.3 Electrical Properties — The Charge Carriers

### 2.3.1 Electrical Conductivity ($\sigma_e$) and Resistivity ($\rho_e$)
Quantify how easily current flows through a material.

- **Conductors:** $\sigma_e \approx 10^7 \text{ S/m}$ (Copper, Aluminum, Silver)
- **Semiconductors:** $\sigma_e \approx 10^{-6} \text{ to } 10^3 \text{ S/m}$ (Silicon, Germanium)
- **Insulators:** $\sigma_e < 10^{-10} \text{ S/m}$ (Glass, Rubber, Teflon)

### 2.3.2 Dielectric Strength
Maximum electric field a dielectric material can withstand without breaking down. Critical for **capacitors, transformer insulation, high-voltage cables**.
