```mermaid
    ElasticA --> YieldA{Yield Point Reached?}
    YieldA -- No --> FailureA[Brittle Fracture]
    YieldA -- Yes --> PlasticA[Region II: Plastic Deformation / Necking]
    PlasticA --> StrainA[Region III: Strain Hardening]
    StrainA --> FractureA[Region IV: Fracture]
    
    FailureA --> NoteA[Example: Polystyrene, PMMA]
    FractureA --> NoteB[Example: Polyethylene, Nylon]
    
    style StartA fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ElasticA fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
    style PlasticA fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000
    style StrainA fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style FractureA fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style FailureA fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Thermal Transition Hierarchy in Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TempAxis([Increasing Temperature]) --> Glassy[Glassy State - T below Tg]
    Glassy --> TgEvent[Tg: Glass Transition - Second Order]
    TgEvent --> Rubbery[Rubbery State - Tg below T below Tm]
    Rubbery --> TmEvent[Tm: Melting Transition - First Order]
    TmEvent --> Viscous[Viscous Flow - T above Tm]
    Viscous --> Decompose[Thermal Decomposition - T above Td]
    
    Glassy --> AmorphousOnly{Amorphous Polymer?}
    TmEvent --> SemiCryst[Semi-Crystalline Only]
    
    style TempAxis fill:#4178BE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Glassy fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style TgEvent fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000
    style Rubbery fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
    style TmEvent fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Viscous fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000
    style Decompose fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Factors Influencing Mechanical & Thermal Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Molecular[Molecular Structure]
        Mw[Molecular Weight]
        Cryst[Crystallinity]
        CrossX[Crosslink Density]
        Branch[Branching]
    end
    
    subgraph External[External Conditions]
        Temp[Temperature]
        Rate[Strain Rate]
        Time[Time / Loading Duration]
    end
    
    Mw --> PropE[Modulus E]
    Mw --> PropTg[Tg]
    Cryst --> PropTm[Tm]
    Cryst --> PropE
    CrossX --> PropTg
    CrossX --> PropE
    Branch --> PropTg
    
    Temp --> PropE
    Temp --> PropTg
    Rate --> PropE
    Rate --> PropE2[Viscosity]
    Time --> PropCreep[Creep Resistance]
    
    style Molecular fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#4A90E2
    style External fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F5A623
    style PropE fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
    style PropTg fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
    style PropTm fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
    style PropCreep fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
    style PropE2 fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Application Selection Matrix — Polymer Property to Engineering Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need High Strength and Heat Resistance?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Cost Sensitive?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Toughened Nylon PA66]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[PEEK or Polyimide]
    
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Need Transparency?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Impact Resistant?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Polycarbonate PC]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[PMMA Acrylic]
    
    Q5 -- No --> Q9{Need Flexibility at Room Temp?}
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q10[Plasticized PVC or LDPE]
    Q9 -- No --> Q11[Polystyrene PS]
    
    style Q1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
    style Q4 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
    style Q7 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
    style Q8 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
    style Q10 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
    style Q11 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Glass Transition Temperature. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
The **Glass Transition Temperature (T<sub>g</sub>)** is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, brittle **glassy state** to a soft, flexible **rubbery state**. It is a **second-order thermodynamic transition** characterized by a sudden change in heat capacity, coefficient of thermal expansion, and segmental mobility, without any latent heat of fusion. Above T<sub>g</sub>, polymer chain segments have sufficient thermal energy to undergo cooperative rotational and translational motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Tip:** Mention both the **state change** AND that it is a **second-order transition** to score full 3 marks.

### Question 2: Distinguish between T<sub>g</sub> and T<sub>m</sub>. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | T<sub>g</sub> | T<sub>m</sub> |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | Second-order transition | First-order transition |
| **Latent Heat** | None | Absorbed (endothermic) |
| **Polymer Type** | Amorphous | Semi-crystalline |
| **Molecular Motion** | Long-range segmental motion onset | Whole chain mobility |
| **Volume Change** | Slope change in V vs T | Step change in V vs T |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible on cooling | Reversible on cooling |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**Q. (a)** With a neat labeled stress–strain curve, explain the mechanical behavior of a **semi-crystalline thermoplastic polymer** under tensile loading. Discuss the significance of **yield point, necking, cold drawing, and strain hardening**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Nylon-6,6** specimen has the following tensile test data:
- Gauge length: $L_0 = 60$ mm
- Cross-section: $A_0 = 25 \text{ mm}^2$
- Yield load: $F_y = 600$ N
- Load at fracture: $F_f = 500$ N
- Final length: $L_f = 180$ mm

Calculate: **(i) Yield Strength, (ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (iii) Percentage Elongation, (iv) Nominal Stress at 2% strain.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer (a):

A semi-crystalline polymer like **Nylon-6,6** or **HDPE** exhibits a characteristic **five-region stress–strain curve**:

1. **Region I — Initial Elastic Deformation:** Linear, reversible; governed by **bond stretching** and **small-angle chain rotations**. Young's modulus $E$ is determined here.

2. **Region II — Yield Point:** The peak stress before permanent deformation. Beyond this, **crystalline lamellae begin to fragment** and chains start to slide.

3. **Region III — Necking:** Localized reduction in cross-section at a defect site. The neck propagates along the gauge length — this is **cold drawing**.

4. **Region IV — Strain Hardening:** As chains become **aligned (oriented)**, further deformation requires more stress due to **covalent bond resistance along aligned chains**.

5. **Region V — Fracture:** Eventual failure when aligned chains break.

> **Significance:**
> - **Yield point** marks transition from elastic to plastic behavior.
> - **Necking/cold drawing** indicates **ductile** behavior — important for **film and fiber processing**.
> - **Strain hardening** provides **toughness** and is exploited in **biaxial orientation** (e.g., PET bottles, PP tapes).

#### Model Answer (b):

**(i) Yield Strength:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{600 \text{ N}}{25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = 24 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = \boxed{24 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength (assumed equal to max load before fracture):**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{F_f}{A_0} = \frac{500 \text{ N}}{25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = \boxed{20 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iii) Percentage Elongation:**

$$\% \text{Elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{180 - 60}{60} \times 100 = \boxed{200\%}$$

**(iv) Nominal Stress at 2% Strain:**

Assuming elastic modulus $E_{Nylon} \approx 2.8$ GPa (typical value for Nylon-6,6):

$$\sigma_{2\%} = E \cdot \varepsilon = 2.8 \times 10^9 \times 0.02 = 56 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = \boxed{56 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [Alternative Choice]

**Q. (a)** Define **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)**. Discuss the factors affecting the CTE of polymers and explain why polymers generally have a **much higher CTE than metals**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **polymer pipe** of length **10 m** at **20 °C** carries hot fluid. The maximum service temperature is **80 °C**. The polymer has:
- CTE: $\alpha = 8 \times 10^{-5} /°C$
- Young's Modulus: $E = 3$ GPa

Calculate: **(i) Free thermal expansion, (ii) Thermal strain, (iii) Compressive thermal stress if fully restrained.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer (a):

**Definition:** The **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion (α)** is the fractional change in length per unit change in temperature:

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot \Delta T}$$

**Factors Affecting CTE of Polymers:**

1. **Chain Stiffness:** Rigid chains (aromatic, ladder polymers) → **lower α**.
2. **Intermolecular Forces:** Strong secondary bonding → **lower α** (e.g., Nylon vs. PTFE).
3. **Crosslink Density:** High crosslinking → **lower α**.
4. **Crystallinity:** Higher crystallinity → **lower α**.
5. **Fillers:** Inorganic fillers (glass, carbon fiber) → **significantly lower α**.
6. **Temperature relative to T<sub>g</sub>:** α increases sharply above T<sub>g</sub> due to free volume increase.

**Why Polymers Have Higher CTE than Metals:**
- Polymers are held together by **weak secondary bonds** (van der Waals, dipole interactions), unlike metals' **metallic bonds**.
- Above T<sub>g</sub>, polymer chains have **large free volume** and **high segmental mobility**, allowing significant thermal expansion.
- Typical polymer α ≈ **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ /°C**, whereas metals are ≈ **$10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ /°C**.

#### Model Answer (b):

**Given:** $L_0 = 10$ m, $T_0 = 20$ °C, $T_1 = 80$ °C, $\alpha = 8 \times 10^{-5} /°C$, $E = 3 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**(i) Free Thermal Expansion:**

$$\Delta T = 80 - 20 = 60 \, °C$$

$$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T = (8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10 \times 60 = \boxed{0.048 \text{ m} = 48 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Thermal Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 60 = \boxed{4.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**(iii) Compressive Thermal Stress (Fully Restrained):**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = (3 \times 10^9) \times (4.8 \times 10^{-3}) = \boxed{14.4 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly forget to **convert mm² to m²** when calculating stress. Always write the unit conversion explicitly. Also, thermal stress is **compressive** when heated and restrained — this can **buckle** the pipe if not accommodated by expansion loops or bellows.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU OEMET721 Module 3**

### Mechanical Properties Essentials
- ☐ **Tensile Strength** = $F_f / A_0$ — units in **MPa**.
- ☐ **Young's Modulus** = slope of **initial linear elastic region** of stress–strain curve.
- ☐ **Percentage Elongation** = $(L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ — ductility indicator.
- ☐ **Toughness** = area under entire stress–strain curve.
- ☐ **Brittle polymers** fracture without yielding; **ductile polymers** show necking + cold drawing.
- ☐ **Viscoelasticity**: polymers show time-dependent deformation → use **Maxwell / Voigt-Kelvin models**.
- ☐ **Creep** increases dramatically as temperature approaches T<sub>g</sub>.

### Thermal Properties Essentials
- ☐ **T<sub>g</sub>** = second-order transition, **no latent heat**, only change in C<sub>p</sub> and α.
- ☐ **T<sub>m</sub>** = first-order transition, **latent heat absorbed**, applies to **crystalline** regions.
- ☐ **Empirical Rule**: $T_g \approx \frac{2}{3} T_m$ (in **Kelvin**) — applies to symmetric semi-crystalline polymers.
- ☐ **HDT** ≈ T<sub>g</sub> − (10 to 30) °C for amorphous polymers.
- ☐ **CTE of polymers** ≈ $10^{-4}$ /°C — **5–10× higher** than metals.
- ☐ **Thermal stress** when restrained: $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
- ☐ **Fox-Flory Equation**: $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ — T<sub>g</sub> increases with molecular weight.
- ☐ **Plasticizers LOWER T<sub>g</sub>** (e.g., DOP in PVC).
- ☐ **Crosslinking INCREASES T<sub>g</sub>** and reduces creep.

### Numerical Problem Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ $\sigma = F/A_0$
- ☐ $\% \text{Elongation} = \Delta L / L_0 \times 100$
- ☐ $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$
- ☐ $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ (restrained)

### Common KTU Conceptual Pitfalls to Avoid
- ⚠️ Do NOT confuse **true stress** with **engineering stress** in post-yield region.
- ⚠️ Do NOT confuse **T<sub>g</sub> with melting** — T<sub>g</sub> is a kinetic transition.
- ⚠️ Remember that **amorphous polymers do not have T<sub>m</sub>**, only T<sub>g</sub>.
- ⚠️ Unit conversions: **1 mm² = $10^{-6}$ m²**, **1 GPa = $10^9$ Pa**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies in Polymer Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies in Polymer Applications

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Studies in Polymer Applications** refers to the systematic, evidence-based engineering analysis of real-world industrial scenarios where polymeric materials are selected, processed, and deployed to solve specific functional, economic, or environmental problems. Each case study integrates the **structure–property–processing–performance (SPPP) paradigm** with application-driven requirements such as load-bearing capacity, thermal stability, chemical resistance, biocompatibility, and manufacturability.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a polymer application case study is a **multi-variable engineering decision matrix** in which a polymer is chosen based on:
- Mechanical demand (tensile, impact, fatigue strength)
- Service environment (temperature, humidity, chemical exposure)
- Regulatory and safety standards (FDA, BIS, ASTM, ISO)
- Life-cycle and sustainability metrics

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Polymer as a Specialist Worker"**
> Imagine you are running a factory. Different jobs require different specialists — a welder, a surgeon, a chef, a pilot. No single worker can do everything well. Similarly, **no single polymer can satisfy every engineering requirement**. A polymer that survives the heat of a jet engine (e.g., **Polyimide, P84**) cannot be used in a flexible food wrapper. A flexible polymer like **Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE)** cannot bear structural loads in an automobile bumper.
>
> *Case studies* in polymer applications are essentially the **recruitment records** of these "specialist workers" — each document explaining which polymer was hired, why it was hired, and how it performed on the job.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Polymer Selection

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | **MPa** |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ | **°C** |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | **°C** |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **GPa** |
| Density | $\rho$ | **g/cm³** |
| Heat Deflection Temperature | HDT | **°C** |
| Limiting Oxygen Index | LOI | **%** |
| Water Absorption | WA | **%** |
| Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon_r$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU 2024 answer, always quote **at least 3 quantitative properties** with units when justifying a polymer selection. Generic statements like *"it is strong"* earn **zero marks**; specific statements like *"Polycarbonate (PC) with $\sigma_u \approx 60\text{–}70$ MPa and $T_g \approx 147\,°C$ is chosen for helmet visors"* earn **full marks**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Polymer Selection Decision Framework

The decision to use a particular polymer in an engineering application follows a structured **4-stage funnel**:

### Stage 1 — Define Functional Requirements
- Load type (static, dynamic, impact, fatigue)
- Operating temperature window ($\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{min}$)
- Chemical/UV/biological exposure
- Electrical/thermal conductivity needs
- Aesthetic and tactile requirements
- Regulatory constraints

### Stage 2 — Apply Performance Filters
- $T_g$ and $T_m$ must lie outside the service range
- $\sigma_u$ and $E$ must exceed the design stress
- Chemical resistance must match the exposure matrix
- Degradation rate over design life must be acceptable

### Stage 3 — Process Compatibility Check
- Rheology must suit the chosen process (injection, extrusion, blow, thermoforming, casting)
- Cycle time and tooling cost must fit the production volume

### Stage 4 — Total Cost & Sustainability Audit
- Raw material + processing + assembly + disposal cost
- Recyclability and end-of-life (EOL) impact

> [!NOTE]
> The **WHY** behind each polymer choice is rooted in molecular structure. For example, the bulky aromatic ring in **Polyetheretherketone (PEEK)** restricts chain rotation, raising $T_g \approx 143\,°C$ and $T_m \approx 343\,°C$ — that is **why** PEEK is used in aircraft brackets and spinal implants.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Property Cheat Sheet

| Property / Formula | Expression | Typical Range | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Strength | $\sigma_u / \rho$ | $20\text{–}200$ kNm/kg | Aerospace weight optimization |
| Specific Modulus | $E / \rho$ | $1\text{–}150$ kNm/kg | Stiffness-to-weight |
| Service Temperature Window | $T_g - T_{min}$ | varies | Thermal application check |
| Creep Modulus (Findley Power Law) | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 + m \cdot t^n$ | $n = 0.1\text{–}0.5$ | Long-term load prediction |
| Heat Deflection Temperature | HDT $\approx (0.8\text{–}0.9) \cdot T_g$ (amorphous) | — | Quick $T_g$ estimate |
| Rule of Mixtures (fibre composite) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | — | CFRP/GFRP stiffness |
| Tensile Strength (composite) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' V_m$ | — | Composite load prediction |
| Arrhenius Degradation Rate | $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | $E_a$ in kJ/mol | Thermal aging |
| Permeability (packaging) | $P = D \cdot S$ | $D$ = diffusivity, $S$ = solubility | Gas barrier prediction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the vertical bar symbol `|` inside any table row.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing modulus $\vert E \vert$ in tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **SPPP linkage** underpins every successful polymer application. For instance:
- **Why is Teflon (PTFE) used in non-stick cookware?** — The C–F bond energy ($\approx 485$ kJ/mol) is among the highest in organic chemistry, giving exceptional chemical inertness and a low surface energy ($\approx 18$ mN/m).
- **Why is Kevlar (Poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide) used in bulletproof vests?** — Hydrogen bonding + rigid aromatic backbone yields $\sigma_u \approx 3.6$ GPa at $\rho \approx 1.44$ g/cm³, giving a specific strength roughly **5× that of steel**.

## 2.4 Cross-Industry Polymer Selection Map

| Industry | Typical Polymer | Critical Property Driving Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive tire | Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR) + Carbon Black | High hysteresis, abrasion resistance |
| Automotive bumper | Polypropylene (PP) + EPDM | Impact strength, paintability |
| Aircraft interior | Polyetherimide (PEI, Ultem) | Low smoke, LOI $> 47\%$, FAA compliance |
| Aircraft structural | CFRP with epoxy/PPS matrix | Specific strength $> 250$ kNm/kg |
| Bone implant (load-bearing) | PEEK / UHMWPE | Biocompatibility, wear resistance |
| Drug delivery | Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) PLGA | Biodegradability, tunable $T_g$ |
| Drinking water pipe | CPVC / HDPE | Corrosion resistance, NSF/ANSI 61 |
| Smartphone housing | PC/ABS blend | Impact + aesthetic finish |
| Solar panel encapsulant | EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate) | UV transparency, adhesion |
| Fire-resistant cable | Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) | LOI $\approx 45\%$, self-extinguishing |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because *Case Studies in Polymer Applications* is fundamentally an **integrative, application-driven topic**, the analytical work centres on **quantitative justification of polymer choice** rather than closed-form derivations. Below is a complete worked-out analytical matrix for a representative KTU-style application.

## 3.1 Case Study: Polymer Selection for an Aircraft Interior Panel

### Problem Statement
An aircraft interior side-panel must satisfy:
- Service temperature: $-55\,°C$ to $+85\,°C$
- FAR 25.853 flammability: OSU heat release $< 65$ kW/m², smoke density $D_s(4\text{ min}) < 200$
- Weight target: $< 1.5$ kg/m²
- Flexural modulus: $> 3.0$ GPa
- Recyclable

### Step 1 — Establish Property Targets
$$\Delta T_{service} = 85 - (-55) = 140\,°C$$
This eliminates commodity plastics like **PS ($T_g \approx 100\,°C$)** and **PE ($T_g \approx -110\,°C$ but low $E$)**.

### Step 2 — Screen High-Temperature Engineering Polymers

| Candidate | $T_g$ (°C) | LOI (%) | Flexural Modulus (GPa) | Density (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polycarbonate (PC) | 147 | 25 | 2.3 | 1.20 |
| Polyetherimide (PEI) | 217 | 47 | 3.3 | 1.27 |
| Polyphenylsulfone (PPSU) | 220 | 38 | 2.4 | 1.29 |
| Polycarbonate/ABS blend | ~130 | 24 | 2.4 | 1.15 |

> Selection criterion: LOI $> 30\%$ is preferred for aircraft interiors.

### Step 3 — Calculate Specific Modulus
For **PEI**:
$$\frac{E}{\rho} = \frac{3.3\,\text{GPa}}{1.27\,\text{g/cm}^3} \approx 2.60\,\text{kNm/kg}$$

For **PPSU**:
$$\frac{E}{\rho} = \frac{2.4\,\text{GPa}}{1.29\,\text{g/cm}^3} \approx 1.86\,\text{kNm/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Flammability Constraint
LOI criterion: $LOI_{air} = 20.9\%$. A polymer with LOI $> 27\%$ is considered **self-extinguishing**.
- PEI: LOI $= 47\%$ ✓ (excellent)
- PPSU: LOI $= 38\%$ ✓ (acceptable)
- PC: LOI $= 25\%$ ✗ (fails)

### Step 5 — Decision
**Polyetherimide (PEI, Ultem 1000)** is selected.

**Justification Summary** (write this way in KTU exams):
- $T_g = 217\,°C \gg T_{max} = 85\,°C$ → dimensional stability
- $LOI = 47\%$ → exceeds FAR 25.853 vertical burn and OSU heat release
- $E/\rho = 2.60$ kNm/kg → lightweight
- Inherently flame-retardant without halogenated additives
- Thermoformable, recyclable via re-extrusion

## 3.2 Case Study: Polymer for a Total Knee Replacement Insert

### Service Conditions
- Cyclic load: $W \approx 2.5\times$ body weight, $\sim 1.5$ million cycles/year
- Lubricant: synovial fluid ($pH \approx 7.4$, aqueous)
- Counterface: CoCrMo alloy, $R_a \approx 0.01\,\mu m$

### Material Candidates

| Polymer | Density (g/cm³) | $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | Wear Rate (mm³/10⁶ cycles) |
|---|---|---|---|
| UHMWPE (GUR 1020/1050) | 0.94 | 40 | 5–10 |
| PEEK (unfilled) | 1.32 | 100 | 25–40 |
| PTFE | 2.16 | 25 | 200–500 (too high) |

### Analytical Justification
- **PTFE** ruled out: wear rate is **20× too high** for a 15-year implant life.
- **PEEK** ruled out: although stronger, its wear debris has been linked to **osteolysis** in long-term studies; tribological compatibility is poor against CoCrMo without fibre reinforcement.
- **UHMWPE** selected: $E \approx 0.8$ GPa (cushioning effect), long clinical track record ($> 50$ years), and its wear mechanism is predominantly **adhesive-abrasive with low cytotoxicity**.

$$V_{wear} = k \cdot F \cdot s$$
where $k$ is the specific wear rate (mm³/Nm), $F$ is normal load (N), $s$ is sliding distance (m). For UHMWPE, $k \approx 1\times 10^{-6}$ mm³/Nm.

### Estimated Linear Wear Over 15 Years
Assume average $F = 1500$ N, $s = 1.5$ million cycles × 0.05 m = 75,000 m:
$$V_{wear} = 1\times 10^{-6} \times 1500 \times 75000 = 112.5\,\text{mm}^3$$
Linear wear $\approx 0.1$ mm/year — within clinical acceptability.

## 3.3 Case Study: Polymer for a Submarine Hull Sonar Window

### Why Not Glass or Metal?
- **Steel**: blocks acoustic signal, magnetic signature
- **Glass**: brittle, hard to mould to hydrodynamic shape
- **Requirement**: match acoustic impedance of seawater ($Z_{water} \approx 1.5$ MRayl), high $E$, low density

### Acoustic Impedance of a Candidate Polymer
$$Z = \rho \cdot c_L, \quad c_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$$

For **Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)**:
- $\rho = 1.18$ g/cm³, $E = 3.3$ GPa

$$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{3.3\times 10^9}{1.18\times 10^3}} = \sqrt{2.797\times 10^6} \approx 1673\,\text{m/s}$$

$$Z_{PMMA} = 1180 \times 1673 \approx 1.97\,\text{MRayl}$$

Reflection coefficient at PMMA/water interface:
$$R = \left(\frac{Z_{PMMA} - Z_{water}}{Z_{PMMA} + Z_{water}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.97 - 1.5}{1.97 + 1.5}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.47}{3.47}\right)^2 \approx 0.0184$$

So only **1.84%** of the acoustic energy is reflected — an **excellent impedance match**, which is precisely why **PMMA is the historical standard for submarine sonar domes**, often fibre-reinforced with rubber for impact tolerance.

## 3.4 Case Study: Polymer for a Drinking Water Pipe (Domestic Plumbing)

### Functional Requirements
- Hydrostatic pressure: $P \approx 6\text{–}10$ bar
- Temperature: $5\text{–}60\,°C$ intermittent
- Potable water contact: must not leach lead, BPA, or vinyl chloride monomer
- Service life: $\geq 50$ years

### Creep Rupture Analysis (HDPE, PE 100)
The **MRS (Minimum Required Strength)** classification is the KTU-relevant way to express long-term hydrostatic strength:

$$MRS = \frac{\sigma_{LPL}}{C}$$
where $\sigma_{LPL}$ is the lower predictive limit of long-term hydrostatic strength at 50 years, $20\,°C$, and $C = 1.25$ is the design (safety) coefficient.

For PE 100: $\sigma_{LPL} \approx 10$ MPa
$$MRS = 10 / 1.25 = 8.0\,\text{MPa}$$
This 8 MPa MRS value is exactly the numerical meaning behind the label **"PE 100"** — i.e., $\sigma_{LPL} = 10$ MPa at 50 years.

Allowable design stress:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{MRS}{C} = \frac{8.0}{1.25} = 6.4\,\text{MPa}$$

For a pipe of outer diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$ under internal pressure $P$:
$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t} \le \sigma_{allow}$$
$$t \ge \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{10 \times 0.05}{2 \times 6.4} \approx 0.039\,\text{m} = 39\,\text{mm}$$
A standard 50 mm OD PE 100 pipe is supplied with $t = 5.8$ mm (SDR 9 class), giving $\sigma_{hoop} \approx 43$ MPa… **this exceeds MRS**; hence PE 100 pipes for 10 bar service are manufactured at **SDR 11** ($t \approx 4.6$ mm) and derated using temperature correction factor $f_T$ at elevated service temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> The same $MRS / C$ philosophy is the basis for **CPVC (Chlorinated PVC)**, **PP-R (Polypropylene Random Copolymer)**, and **PB-1 (Polybutene-1)** pipe classifications — these appear frequently in KTU 2024 questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET721]**
*List any three property requirements for selecting a polymer for an aircraft cabin interior panel. Justify each in one sentence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI $> 30\%$)** — required to satisfy FAR 25.853 flammability and low smoke emission norms in aircraft cabins.
2. **Glass transition temperature $T_g$ above $100\,°C$** — ensures dimensional stability at cabin operating temperatures up to $85\,°C$.
3. **Low density ($< 1.5$ g/cm³) and high specific modulus** — to keep cabin mass low and meet fuel-efficiency targets.

> *[Examiner note: award full 3 marks only if all three properties are stated with numeric thresholds and unit.]*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET721]**
*Why is PMMA used in submarine sonar windows instead of steel?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Acoustic impedance match**: $Z_{PMMA} \approx 1.97$ MRayl, very close to seawater ($Z \approx 1.5$ MRayl); the reflection coefficient is only $\approx 1.8\%$, allowing sonar signals to pass with minimal loss. Steel would block the signal entirely.
- **Non-magnetic**: eliminates magnetic anomaly detection (MAD) by enemy submarines.
- **Low density** ($\rho = 1.18$ g/cm³) and **mouldability** into hydrodynamic shapes reduces drag.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET721]**

**(a)** *Select a suitable polymer for the tibial insert of a total knee replacement prosthesis. List four functional requirements and justify the choice with quantitative property data. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A water-distribution pipe is to be made from PE 100 grade HDPE for 10 bar service at 20 °C with a design life of 50 years. Given a 50 mm OD pipe, determine the minimum wall thickness using the MRS method. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Functional Requirements** (2 marks):
   - Biocompatible (ISO 10993) for body contact
   - Low wear rate under cyclic load (target $< 0.2$ mm/year)
   - Sufficient fatigue strength over $\sim 30$ million cycles
   - Compatible with CoCrMo counterface, sterilizable by gamma radiation

2. **Material Selection — UHMWPE (GUR 1020 or 1050)** (3 marks):
   - Density $\rho = 0.94$ g/cm³ → low mass
   - Tensile strength $\sigma_u \approx 40$ MPa (yield)
   - Elongation at break $\approx 350\%$ → toughness
   - Limiting PV (pressure × velocity) product high
   - Wear rate $k \approx 1 \times 10^{-6}$ mm³/Nm in synovial fluid
   - $> 50$ years of clinical history

3. **Why PEEK and PTFE are rejected** (2 marks):
   - PEEK: generates fine wear debris; expensive; processing requires $> 350\,°C$
   - PTFE: wear rate $20\times$ higher, poor load-bearing capacity
   - Hence **UHMWPE remains the gold standard** for the tibial insert.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

Given:
- $P = 10$ bar $= 1$ MPa
- $D = 50$ mm (outer diameter, thin-wall approximation)
- PE 100: $\sigma_{LPL} = 10$ MPa at 50 years, $20\,°C$

Step 1 — Compute MRS:
$$MRS = \frac{\sigma_{LPL}}{C_{min}} = \frac{10}{1.25} = 8.0\,\text{MPa}$$

Step 2 — Compute allowable design stress (overall design factor $C = 1.25$ already incorporated; KTU expects additional safety factor $C_2 = 1.25$ for water hammer etc.):
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{MRS}{C_2} = \frac{8.0}{1.25} = 6.4\,\text{MPa}$$

Step 3 — Apply Barlow's formula:
$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t} \le \sigma_{allow}$$
$$t \ge \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{1.0 \times 50}{2 \times 6.4} = 3.91\,\text{mm}$$

Step 4 — Add 10% allowance for manufacturing tolerance and aging:
$$t_{min, design} = 3.91 \times 1.1 \approx 4.3\,\text{mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{min} \approx 4.3$ mm; commercially supplied as **SDR 11** PE 100 pipe with $t = 4.6$ mm. ✓ (1 mark for the final engineering interpretation)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET721]**

**(a)** *Compare the suitability of Polycarbonate (PC), Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), and Polyetherimide (PEI) for an aircraft window canopy. Recommend the best choice and justify with at least three properties in numerical form. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A food packaging film must have an oxygen transmission rate below 5 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Compare the barrier performance of LDPE, PET, and EVOH. Recommend a multilayer structure and justify. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

| Property | PC | PMMA | PEI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.20 | 1.18 | 1.27 |
| $T_g$ (°C) | 147 | 105 | 217 |
| LOI (%) | 25 | 18 | 47 |
| Impact Strength (Izod, J/m) | 600–900 | 20–30 | 50 |
| Optical Transmission (%) | 89 | 92 | 60 |

**Analysis** (4 marks):
- **PMMA** has the best optical clarity (92%) but poor impact strength and low LOI — **fails bird-strike and flammability** tests.
- **PC** has excellent impact strength and good optical clarity, but LOI $= 25\%$ fails FAR 25.853 cabin flame requirements.
- **PEI** satisfies all flammability and thermal criteria, but optical transparency is only 60% — inadequate for a forward-facing canopy.
- **None of the three is ideal alone** — modern aircraft canopies use **stretched acrylic (PMMA) with a PC inner layer** and a silicone/polyurethane rain-erosion coating.

**Recommendation** (1 mark): **PMMA (outer, optical) + Polycarbonate (inner, impact)** laminate — this is the actual material choice in Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 forward canopies.

**Quantitative Justification** (2 marks):
- PMMA: $T_g = 105\,°C$ (survives $85\,°C$ cabin temp with margin)
- PC: Izod $= 800$ J/m (resists 1 kg bird strike at 250 m/s)
- Multilayer keeps canopy weight $< 30$ kg per side.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — OTR of single-layer films** (2 marks):

| Material | OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹) at 23 °C, 0% RH |
|---|---|
| LDPE | ~7000 (poor barrier) |
| PET | ~120 (moderate) |
| EVOH (32% ethylene) | ~0.5 (excellent, but moisture-sensitive) |

**Step 2 — Problem with EVOH**: At $> 70\%$ RH, EVOH plasticizes and OTR rises to $\sim 50$. So EVOH cannot be the **outer** layer in a humid environment. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Recommended 5-layer structure** (3 marks):
$$\text{LDPE} / \text{Tie} / \text{EVOH} / \text{Tie} / \text{LDPE}$$
- Outer LDPE: moisture protection + heat-sealable
- EVOH: oxygen barrier
- Tie layer (maleic-anhydride grafted polyolefin): adhesion
- Inner LDPE: food contact + sealable

**Step 4 — Effective OTR of laminate** (1 mark):
$$\frac{1}{OTR_{total}} = \sum_i \frac{x_i}{OTR_i}$$
For 80 µm LDPE / 10 µm tie / 10 µm EVOH / 10 µm tie / 80 µm LDPE:
$$OTR_{total} \approx \left( \frac{80}{7000} + \frac{10}{0.5} + \frac{80}{7000} \right)^{-1} \approx 4\,\text{cm}^3\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{day}^{-1}$$
**Within specification.** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Generic statements** ("polymer X is strong") → **0 marks**. Always quote numerical values with units.
> 2. **Confusing $T_g$ and $T_m$** for semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., HDPE: $T_g \approx -110\,°C$, $T_m \approx 130\,°C$). The relevant parameter for service temperature of HDPE is $T_m$, not $T_g$.
> 3. **Ignoring safety factor $C$** in pipe design — marks deducted for using $\sigma_u$ directly instead of $MRS/C$.
> 4. **Wrong formula** in acoustic impedance — use $Z = \rho c$, not $Z = E/A$.
> 5. **Forgetting tie layers** in multilayer packaging — examiners explicitly check this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Case Studies in Polymer Applications**

- **Always justify polymer choice with a 3-property rule**: mechanical, thermal, chemical/environmental. Add processability and cost as the 4th and 5th.
- **Use the SPPP (Structure–Property–Processing–Performance) paradigm** as the underlying logic in every answer.
- **High-temperature aerospace** → PEI, PI, PPS, PEEK, CFRP/epoxy.
- **Biomedical implants** → UHMWPE (joints), PEEK (spinal cage), PDMS (soft tissue), PLGA (resorbable).
- **Packaging barrier** → EVOH (O₂), PVDC (moisture), PET (clarity), LDPE/LLDPE (sealability) — almost always in **multilayer** form.
- **Pipes** → HDPE PE 100, PP-R, CPVC, PB-1 — all classified by **MRS** (ISO 4427 / ISO 15874).
- **Sonar windows** → PMMA (acoustic impedance match to seawater).
- **Tires** → SBR + BR + carbon black + steel belts; the elastomer matrix dissipates energy by hysteresis.
- **Always write the governing equation first**, then substitute numbers, then give the result with units. **Marks are awarded for the equation, not the final number alone.**
- **Units are mandatory** — MPa, GPa, g/cm³, °C, kNm/kg.
- **Sustainability angle** (NEP 2020 emphasis): always mention recyclability, bio-based content, or end-of-life option in your justification.
- **Mnemonic for aerospace polymers**: *"**P**lease **E**valuate **I**mportant **P**olymers **E**ngineering **K**evlar"* → PC, Epoxy, Imide (PI), PEI, PPS, PEEK, Kevlar.
- **Mnemonic for biomedical polymers**: *"**U**ncle **P**aul **P**rotects **L**ives **D**iligently"* → UHMWPE, PEEK, PDMS, PLGA, PMMA (contact lens).
- **Avoid these common confusions**:
  - *Epoxy* ≠ *Polyester* ≠ *Vinyl ester* (three different thermosets)
  - *Acrylic* (PMMA) ≠ *Polycarbonate* (PC) — both transparent but vastly different in impact and flammability
  - *HDPE* ≠ *UHMWPE* — both polyethylene, but $M_w$ of UHMWPE is $3\text{–}6$ million g/mol vs $0.2\text{–}0.5$ million for HDPE
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Materials: Definition and Types of Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Composite Materials: Definition and Types of Composites ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **Composite Material** is a heterogeneous, multi-phase engineering material made by the **macroscopic combination of two or more distinct constituent materials** — a **Matrix (Binder Phase)** and a **Reinforcement (Dispersed Phase)** — which remain physically separate and distinct at the microscopic level but act as a single unified body at the macroscopic level, producing a material whose **properties are superior to those of the individual constituents acting alone.** The American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) defines a composite as a "constituent system comprising two or more constituent phases that are mechanically or metallurgically bonded together, providing properties not obtainable by any single constituent." ### 1.2 Essential Characteristics of a Composite For a material to qualify as a true composite (per KTU 2024 outcome-based framework), it must satisfy **all five** of the following: | Sl. No. | Characteristic | Engineering Significance | |:---:|---|---| | 1 | **Heterogeneous & Multi-phase** | Must contain at least two chemically/distinct phases | | 2 | **Macroscopic Combination** | Phases are identifiable at macro level (not atomic) | | 3 | **Distinct Interface (Interphase)** | A clear boundary exists between matrix and reinforcement | | 4 | **Synergistic Property Enhancement** | Combined properties exceed the rule-of-mixtures average | | 5 | **Engineered Interface Bonding** | Phases are intentionally combined, not naturally co-existing | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Exams:** A composite is **NOT an alloy** and **NOT a compound**. In an alloy, atoms mix at the atomic level. In a compound, atoms bond chemically in fixed ratios. In a composite, the phases retain their **individual identities** and are bonded only at the **interface**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are making a **concrete slab** for a building: - **Cement + Sand + Aggregate (Concrete)** → The **Matrix** (continuous phase that holds everything together and transfers load) - **Steel Rebars (TMT bars)** → The **Reinforcement** (strong, stiff phase that carries most of the tensile load) - **Bond between concrete & steel** → The **Interface** (ensures load transfer) Plain concrete is **excellent in compression** but **brittle in tension**. Steel is **excellent in tension** but **buckles in compression**. When combined, **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** becomes a material that handles both — this is the **synergistic principle** of composites. > [!TIP] > **Another Familiar Example:** **Plywood** — thin layers (plies) of wood veneers glued together with the grain direction alternated 90° between layers. The adhesive is the matrix; the wood plies are the reinforcement. The result is stronger and more dimensionally stable than a single thick plank. ### 1.4 Engineering Motivation — Why Do We Need Composites? Modern engineering demands combinations of properties that **no single homogeneous material can provide** simultaneously: - **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio** (specific strength) — critical in aerospace - **High Stiffness-to-Weight Ratio** (specific modulus) — critical in structural panels - **Tailorable Anisotropy** — properties can be designed along specific directions - **Corrosion Resistance** in aggressive chemical environments - **Thermal Stability** at elevated temperatures - **Fatigue Resistance** under cyclic loading - **Damage Tolerance** and **Fail-Safe** behaviour > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard Metric:** Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) has a specific strength of approximately **\mathbf{2,450 \text{ kNm/kg}**} compared to structural steel at **\mathbf{63 \text{ kNm/kg}**} — a **~40× improvement**. This is why Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 use ~50% composites by weight. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Essential Constituents of a Composite Every composite, regardless of type, has **two functional phases**: #### A. The Matrix Phase (Binder / Continuous Phase) The matrix is the **continuous body** that: - **Encapsulates and supports** the reinforcement - **Transfers applied loads** to the reinforcement via shear at the interface - **Protects** the reinforcement from environmental damage, abrasion, and chemical attack - **Maintains the geometry** and fibre orientation - Provides **transverse strength** and **interlaminar shear resistance** - Dictates the **maximum service temperature** of the composite **Common Matrix Materials:** - **Polymers** → Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Phenolic, PEEK - **Metals** → Aluminium, Titanium, Magnesium, Copper, Nickel-based superalloys - **Ceramics** → Alumina (Al₂O₃), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄), Glass-ceramics #### B. The Reinforcement Phase (Dispersed / Load-Bearing Phase) The reinforcement is the **discontinuous, stronger, stiffer phase** that: - **Carries the major portion of the applied load** (the "load bearer") - **Restricts matrix deformation** through interfacial shear - Provides **high strength, high stiffness, and directional properties** - Determines the **primary mechanical behaviour** of the composite **Forms of Reinforcement:** - **Fibres** (continuous / discontinuous / whiskers) — e.g., glass, carbon, aramid, boron - **Particles** (equiaxed / platelet) — e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃, WC, TiC - **Flakes / Platelets** — e.g., mica, glass flakes - **Filled or hollow microspheres** #### C. The Interface (Interphase) A thin, distinct region (~1–100 nm) where matrix and reinforcement meet. It controls the **efficiency of load transfer** and is engineered through **surface treatments** (e.g., silane coating on glass fibres). ### 2.2 Critical Conceptual Framework > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Synergy Conditions for a Successful Composite:** > 1. **Reinforcement must be stronger and stiffer** than the matrix → $\sigma_f > \sigma_m$ and $E_f > E_m$ > 2. **Volume fraction of reinforcement** must be **adequate** ($V_f \geq 0.1$ typical minimum) > 3. **Matrix-Reinforcement bond** must be **strong enough** to transfer load without debonding ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All the formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU Module 3 examinations. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning. | Sl. No. | Property | Formula (Rule of Mixtures) | Units | Validity / Type | |:---:|---|---|:---:|---| | 1 | **Volume Fraction Relation** | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | dimensionless | Always true | | 2 | **Composite Density** | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | kg/m³ | Exact for closed composites | | 3 | **Weight Fraction of Fibre** | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | dimensionless | Used in fabrication | | 4 | **Longitudinal Young's Modulus** (Voigt / Iso-strain) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Parallel loading along fibres | | 5 | **Transverse Young's Modulus** (Reuss / Iso-stress) | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | GPa | Loading perpendicular to fibres | | 6 | **Longitudinal Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | MPa | Brittle fibre, brittle matrix | | 7 | **Composite Strength (mixed mode)** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f' V_f + \sigma_m(1 - V_f)$ | MPa | Fibre continues to fracture beyond matrix cracking | | 8 | **Critical Fibre Length** | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ | m | Minimum length for full load transfer | | 9 | **Aspect Ratio Requirement** | $l/d \geq l_c/d$ for efficient reinforcement | dimensionless | $l/d \geq 10$ is the standard rule | | 10 | **Specific Strength** | $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | kNm/kg | Aerospace figure of merit | | 11 | **Specific Modulus** | $SM = \dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c}$ | GNm/kg | Stiffness-to-weight metric | **Where the symbols mean:** $$V_f, V_m \;=\; \text{Volume fractions of fibre and matrix} \quad (\text{dimensionless})$$ $$\rho_f, \rho_m, \rho_c \;=\; \text{Densities of fibre, matrix, and composite} \quad (\text{kg/m}^3)$$ $$E_f, E_m, E_c \;=\; \text{Young's moduli of fibre, matrix, and composite} \quad (\text{GPa})$$ $$\sigma_f, \sigma_m, \sigma_c \;=\; \text{Tensile strengths of fibre, matrix, and composite} \quad (\text{MPa})$$ $$d, l, l_c \;=\; \text{Fibre diameter, fibre length, critical fibre length} \quad (\text{m})$$ $$\tau_i \;=\; \text{Interfacial shear strength} \quad (\text{MPa})$$ ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Rule of Mixtures The **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** is **not just a formula** — it is the **design engineer's first-pass calculation tool** for: 1. **Material Selection in Aerospace:** Pick $V_f$ that gives required $E_c$ for an aircraft wing spar. 2. **Cost Optimization:** Higher $V_f$ = more expensive fibre. RoM helps find the minimum $V_f$ for required strength. 3. **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Theoretical $\rho_c$ is compared with measured density to detect voids. 4. **Predicting Anisotropy:** Longitudinal vs. transverse RoM gives the strength directionality. 5. **Design of Hybrid Composites:** Combining two or more fibre types in a single matrix. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Density (Most Fundamental) **Statement:** The density of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite is the volume-weighted average of the densities of its constituents. **Starting Assumption:** A composite of total volume $V_c$ contains fibre volume $V_f^c$ and matrix volume $V_m^c$ such that: $$V_c \;=\; V_f^c + V_m^c$$ **Define Volume Fractions:** $$V_f \;=\; \frac{V_f^c}{V_c} \quad \text{and} \quad V_m \;=\; \frac{V_m^c}{V_c}$$ Therefore the **fundamental constraint:** $$V_f + V_m \;=\; 1$$ **Express the Masses:** Total mass is the sum of the constituent masses (conservation of mass): $$m_c \;=\; m_f + m_m$$ **Substitute mass = density × volume:** $$\rho_c V_c \;=\; \rho_f V_f^c + \rho_m V_m^c$$ **Divide both sides by $V_c$:** $$\rho_c \;=\; \rho_f \cdot \frac{V_f^c}{V_c} + \rho_m \cdot \frac{V_m^c}{V_c}$$ **Recognize the volume fractions:** $$\boxed{\rho_c \;=\; \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$ This is the **linear rule of mixtures for density** — it is exact because mass and volume are additive scalars. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt / Iso-strain Model) **Physical Setup:** Consider a unidirectional composite loaded **parallel to the fibre direction**. The matrix and fibre are assumed to be **perfectly bonded** so that they experience the **same strain** (iso-strain condition). $$\varepsilon_c \;=\; \varepsilon_f \;=\; \varepsilon_m \;=\; \varepsilon$$ **Starting from Hooke's Law applied to each phase and the composite:** $$\sigma_c \;=\; E_c \, \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_f \;=\; E_f \, \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_m \;=\; E_m \, \varepsilon$$ **Force equilibrium (the total load is shared by fibre and matrix):** $$F_c \;=\; F_f + F_m$$ **Express forces in terms of stress × cross-sectional area:** $$\sigma_c A_c \;=\; \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$ **Divide by $A_c$ and substitute Hooke's Law:** $$E_c \, \varepsilon \;=\; E_f \, \varepsilon \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \, \varepsilon \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$ **Define area fractions (for unidirectional loading parallel to fibres, area fraction = volume fraction):** $$A_f / A_c \;=\; V_f, \quad A_m / A_c \;=\; V_m$$ **The strains cancel (iso-strain condition):** $$\boxed{E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$ This is the **Voigt Upper Bound** — it represents the **maximum possible stiffness** of the composite in the fibre direction. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transverse Young's Modulus (Reuss / Iso-stress Model) **Physical Setup:** Now the load is applied **perpendicular to the fibre direction**. The matrix and fibre experience the **same stress** (iso-stress condition) but different strains. $$\sigma_c \;=\; \sigma_f \;=\; \sigma_m \;=\; \sigma$$ **Hooke's Law for each phase:** $$\varepsilon_c \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{E_c}, \quad \varepsilon_f \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{E_f}, \quad \varepsilon_m \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{E_m}$$ **Geometric compatibility (total displacement = sum of displacements):** $$\Delta L_c \;=\; \Delta L_f + \Delta L_m$$ **Express displacements in terms of strain × length (assuming equal lengths):** $$\varepsilon_c L \;=\; \varepsilon_f L + \varepsilon_m L$$ $$\varepsilon_c \;=\; \varepsilon_f + \varepsilon_m$$ **Substitute the strain expressions:** $$\frac{\sigma}{E_c} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{E_f} + \frac{\sigma}{E_m}$$ **The stress cancels (iso-stress condition):** $$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$ This is the **Reuss Lower Bound** — the **minimum possible stiffness** of the composite in the transverse direction. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Fibre Length **Problem Statement:** A short fibre must be long enough to develop its full fracture stress $\sigma_f$ through interfacial shear before the fibre itself breaks. **Setup:** A fibre of diameter $d$ is embedded in a matrix. At the fibre ends, load transfer occurs via interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting over the cylindrical surface. **Equilibrium at the point of fibre fracture:** Tensile force in fibre = shear force transferred along the fibre-matrix interface over the critical length $l_c / 2$ on each side. $$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \;=\; \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$ **Simplify (divide both sides by $\pi d$):** $$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} \;=\; \tau_i \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$ $$\sigma_f \cdot d \;=\; 2 \, \tau_i \cdot l_c$$ $$\boxed{l_c \;=\; \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** If $l < l_c$, the fibre pulls out of the matrix before reaching its full strength — **wasteful use of reinforcement**. The general rule of thumb is to use fibres with $l / d \geq 10$ to ensure efficient strengthening. ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite contains **60% by volume of glass fibres** ($V_f = 0.60$). Given: - $E_f = 72$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2550$ kg/m³ - $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³ **Find:** (a) Density of composite, (b) Longitudinal Young's modulus, (c) Transverse Young's modulus, (d) Specific modulus (longitudinal). **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute matrix volume fraction:** $$V_m \;=\; 1 - V_f \;=\; 1 - 0.60 \;=\; 0.40$$ **Step 2 — (a) Composite density (RoM):** $$\rho_c \;=\; \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$ $$\rho_c \;=\; (2550)(0.60) + (1200)(0.40)$$ $$\rho_c \;=\; 1530 + 480 \;=\; 2010 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — (b) Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt):** $$E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ $$E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; (72)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40)$$ $$E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; 43.2 + 1.4 \;=\; 44.6 \; \text{GPa}$$ **Step 4 — (c) Transverse Young's modulus (Reuss):** $$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; \frac{0.60}{72} + \frac{0.40}{3.5}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; 0.00833 + 0.11429 \;=\; 0.12262 \; \text{GPa}^{-1}$$ $$E_{c,\perp} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.12262} \;\approx\; 8.16 \; \text{GPa}$$ **Step 5 — (d) Specific modulus (longitudinal):** $$SM \;=\; \frac{E_{c,\parallel}}{\rho_c} \;=\; \frac{44.6 \times 10^9}{2010}$$ $$SM \;\approx\; 22.2 \times 10^6 \; \text{Nm/kg} \;=\; 22.2 \; \text{MNm/kg}$$ **Engineering Insight:** The longitudinal modulus (44.6 GPa) is **~5.5× higher** than the transverse modulus (8.16 GPa). This **anisotropy** is the hallmark of fibre composites and is **deliberately engineered** for directional load paths (e.g., wing skins, drive shafts, pressure vessels). --- ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a composite material. List the four essential requirements that a material must satisfy to be classified as a composite.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition:** A composite material is a heterogeneous, multi-phase material formed by the **macroscopic combination of two or more distinct constituent materials** (matrix + reinforcement) that act together as a single unified body, producing **superior properties** not achievable by any single constituent. **Four essential requirements:** 1. **Heterogeneity & Multi-phase nature** — must contain at least two chemically distinct phases. 2. **Distinct Phases with Macroscopic Distinction** — phases must be identifiable and separate, not atomically mixed (this differentiates composites from alloys). 3. **Engineered Interface** — there must be a defined matrix-reinforcement interface that enables load transfer. 4. **Synergistic Property Enhancement** — combined properties must be superior to those of individual constituents acting alone (the "1 + 1 > 2" criterion). *(Allocate ~1 mark for definition, ~2 marks for the four points.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between Fibre-Reinforced Composites (FRC) and Particle-Reinforced Composites (PRC) with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | **Fibre-Reinforced Composites (FRC)** | **Particle-Reinforced Composites (PRC)** | |:---|:---|:---| | **Reinforcement Geometry** | High aspect ratio (length/diameter $\gg$ 1) — fibres, whiskers | Low aspect ratio (length/diameter $\approx$ 1) — equiaxed or platelet particles | | **Property Directionality** | **Anisotropic** — strong along fibre direction, weak transverse | **Isotropic** — properties similar in all directions | | **Load Transfer Mechanism** | Through **interfacial shear** along the cylindrical fibre surface | Through **direct compressive/shear contact** at particle-matrix interface | | **Example 1** | Glass-fibre reinforced epoxy (GFRP) | Silicon carbide particles in aluminium (Al-SiCp) | | **Example 2** | Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) | Tungsten carbide particles in cobalt matrix (WC-Co, used in cutting tools) | *(Allocate 1.5 marks for differentiation, 1.5 marks for examples.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!** > • Forgetting to define volume fraction $V_f + V_m = 1$ before substitution — loses **1 mark**. > • Confusing Voigt (iso-strain, parallel) with Reuss (iso-stress, series) formulae — loses **2 marks**. > • Not stating units in the final answer — loses **0.5 mark per answer**. > • Writing "Voigt rule" without mentioning "longitudinal / parallel to fibres" — loses **1 mark**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Fundamentals + Rule of Mixtures** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]** **(a)** What are the **three main types of composites** classified on the basis of the matrix material? Explain each with two applications and list two typical matrix materials for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A carbon-fibre reinforced epoxy composite has a fibre volume fraction of **0.45**. The carbon fibre has $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1750$ kg/m³, and $\sigma_f = 4.0$ GPa. The epoxy matrix has $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³, and $\sigma_m = 50$ MPa. Calculate: **(i)** longitudinal Young's modulus, **(ii)** transverse Young's modulus, **(iii)** composite density, and **(iv)** longitudinal tensile strength of the composite. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer A:** **(a) Three main types of composites based on matrix material:** (7 marks) > **1. Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC):** > - **Matrix materials:** Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, PEEK, Phenolic > - **Reinforcement examples:** Glass, Carbon, Aramid fibres > - **Application 1:** Aircraft interior panels and floor structures (Boeing 787 cabin) > - **Application 2:** Wind turbine blades (e.g., GE 1.5 MW turbines use GFRP) > - *Advantage:* Low density, easy fabrication, low cost > - *Limitation:* Low maximum service temperature (< 250 °C) > **2. Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):** > - **Matrix materials:** Aluminium alloys, Titanium alloys, Magnesium, Copper > - **Reinforcement examples:** SiC particles, Al₂O₃ fibres, Carbon fibres > - **Application 1:** Automotive brake discs and drums (e.g., Toyota, Ford) > - **Application 2:** Aerospace structural components, missile fin sections > - *Advantage:* Higher temperature capability, better ductility than ceramics > - *Limitation:* Higher density, expensive processing routes > **3. Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC):** > - **Matrix materials:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄) > - **Reinforcement examples:** SiC fibres, Carbon fibres, Mullite whiskers > - **Application 1:** Jet engine turbine blades and combustion liners (e.g., GE LEAP engine) > - **Application 2:** Space shuttle thermal protection tiles, re-entry nose cones > - *Advantage:* Retains strength at very high temperatures (> 1000 °C) > - *Limitation:* Brittle, expensive, difficult to manufacture *(Allocation: 1 mark each for matrix + 0.5 mark each for applications × 2 + 0.5 mark for advantage/limitation = 7 marks total. Examiner may award full marks for clarity of classification.)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** (7 marks) **Given:** $V_f = 0.45$, $V_m = 0.55$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1750$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³, $\sigma_f = 4000$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 50$ MPa **Step 1 — (i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt / Iso-strain):** $$E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ $$E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; (230)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$ $$E_{c,\parallel} \;=\; 103.5 + 1.925 \;=\; 105.425 \; \text{GPa}$$ **Step 2 — (ii) Transverse Young's Modulus (Reuss / Iso-stress):** $$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; \frac{0.45}{230} + \frac{0.55}{3.5}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} \;=\; 0.001957 + 0.157143 \;=\; 0.159100 \; \text{GPa}^{-1}$$ $$E_{c,\perp} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.159100} \;\approx\; 6.285 \; \text{GPa}$$ **Step 3 — (iii) Composite Density:** $$\rho_c \;=\; \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$ $$\rho_c \;=\; (1750)(0.45) + (1200)(0.55)$$ $$\rho_c \;=\; 787.5 + 660 \;=\; 1447.5 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 4 — (iv) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (RoM):** $$\sigma_{c,\parallel} \;=\; \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$ $$\sigma_{c,\parallel} \;=\; (4000)(0.45) + (50)(0.55)$$ $$\sigma_{c,\parallel} \;=\; 1800 + 27.5 \;=\; 1827.5 \; \text{MPa} \;\approx\; 1.83 \; \text{GPa}$$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Quantity | Value | Units | |:---|:---:|:---:| | $E_{c,\parallel}$ | 105.43 | GPa | | $E_{c,\perp}$ | 6.29 | GPa | | $\rho_c$ | 1447.5 | kg/m³ | | $\sigma_{c,\parallel}$ | 1827.5 | MPa | *(Marking: 1.5 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii) and 2.5 marks for (iv) — 7 marks total. Award 0.5 mark for clear identification of Voigt vs Reuss.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — Reinforcement Types + Critical Fibre Length** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Classify composites based on **the form of reinforcement** with neat sketches/diagrams. Discuss the difference in mechanical behaviour between **continuous fibre, short fibre, and particle-reinforced composites.** Mention one specific industrial application of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A glass fibre of diameter **15 μm** has a tensile strength of **2.5 GPa**. The interfacial shear strength between the fibre and the epoxy matrix is **40 MPa**. Calculate: **(i)** the **critical fibre length** $l_c$, and **(ii)** the **minimum aspect ratio** required for the fibre to be an efficient reinforcement. If a designer is restricted to fibres of **length 1.2 mm**, determine whether this length is sufficient for the fibre to develop its **full strength** in the matrix. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer B:** **(a) Classification based on reinforcement form:** (7 marks) > **1. Fibre-Reinforced Composites (FRC):** > - Reinforcement in the form of **fibres** (continuous or discontinuous) > - **Continuous fibre composites** (e.g., unidirectional carbon/epoxy in aircraft wing skins) — Highest strength and stiffness, **highly anisotropic** > - **Short/discontinuous fibre composites** (e.g., chopped glass fibre in injection-moulded automotive parts) — Lower strength than continuous, but **isotropic in plane**, easier to manufacture > - **Industrial application:** Helicopter rotor blades (continuous CFRP); Automotive bumper beams (chopped GFRP) > **2. Particle-Reinforced Composites (PRC):** > - Reinforcement in the form of **equiaxed or near-equiaxed particles** (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃, WC, TiC) > - Properties are **isotropic** in the bulk > - Primary strengthening mechanism: **load sharing** by particles and **dislocation pinning** in metal matrices > - **Industrial application:** WC-Co cutting tool inserts (cemented carbides); Al-SiC automotive pistons > **3. Structural Composites:** > - **Laminates** — Stacked layers of fibre-reinforced plies with varying fibre orientations (e.g., cross-ply, angle-ply) > - **Sandwich panels** — Two stiff face sheets bonded to a low-density core (honeycomb or foam); e.g., aircraft floor panels, wind turbine nacelles > - **Industrial application:** Boeing 787 floor panels (honeycomb sandwich); Boat hulls (fibreglass laminates) **Difference in mechanical behaviour summary:** - **Continuous fibre composites** offer the **highest strength in the fibre direction** but are **highly anisotropic**. Strength drops sharply in the transverse direction. - **Short fibre composites** offer **moderate, more isotropic strength** and are easier to process using injection moulding. - **Particle composites** offer **isotropic strengthening**, with much less anisotropy but lower peak strength than aligned fibres. *(Marking: 2 marks for classification diagram/table, 2 marks for behaviour comparison, 2 marks for applications, 1 mark for neatness. Total 7 marks.)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** (7 marks) **Given:** $d = 15 \, \mu\text{m} = 15 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $\sigma_f = 2.5 \, \text{GPa} = 2.5 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$, $\tau_i = 40 \, \text{MPa} = 40 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$ **Step 1 — (i) Critical Fibre Length:** $$l_c \;=\; \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$$ $$l_c \;=\; \frac{(2.5 \times 10^9)(15 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (40 \times 10^6)}$$ $$l_c \;=\; \frac{3.75 \times 10^4}{8.0 \times 10^7}$$ $$l_c \;=\; 4.6875 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} \;=\; 0.46875 \, \text{mm} \;\approx\; 0.47 \, \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — (ii) Minimum Aspect Ratio:** $$\left(\frac{l}{d}\right)_{\min} \;=\; \frac{l_c}{d} \;=\; \frac{0.46875 \times 10^{-3}}{15 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 31.25$$ The fibre must have an aspect ratio of **at least ~31** for efficient reinforcement. **Step 3 — Design Feasibility Check:** The designer has fibres of length $l = 1.2 \, \text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$. Compute the actual aspect ratio of supplied fibres: $$\left(\frac{l}{d}\right)_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{15 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 80$$ **Compare:** $$\left(\frac{l}{d}\right)_{\text{actual}} = 80 \;>\; \left(\frac{l}{d}\right)_{\min} = 31.25$$ Since the actual aspect ratio is **greater than the minimum required aspect ratio**, the fibre length of **1.2 mm is sufficient** for the fibre to develop its full tensile strength in the matrix. **Additional verification using critical length directly:** $$l = 1.2 \, \text{mm} \;>\; l_c = 0.47 \, \text{mm} \;\; \checkmark$$ Therefore, the fibre is **efficiently loaded up to its full strength of 2.5 GPa** before any matrix-related failure modes can dominate. *(Marking: 2 marks for $l_c$, 2 marks for aspect ratio, 3 marks for feasibility conclusion. Total 7 marks.)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!** **🔑 DEFINITIONS & FUNDAMENTALS** - ☐ A **composite** is a **heterogeneous, multi-phase, engineered material** combining matrix + reinforcement with a defined interface. - ☐ Composites are **not alloys** (no atomic mixing) and **not compounds** (no fixed stoichiometric ratio). - ☐ **Synergy Principle:** $P_{\text{composite}} > P_{\text{average of constituents}}$ — the "1 + 1 > 2" rule. - ☐ The **matrix** transfers load, protects the reinforcement, and provides transverse strength. - ☐ The **reinforcement** carries the major load, providing strength, stiffness, and directionality. - ☐ The **interface** governs load transfer efficiency — surface treatments (silane coupling agents) are critical. **🔑 CLASSIFICATION TAXONOMY** - ☐ **By Matrix:** PMC (Polymer) | MMC (Metal) | CMC (Ceramic) - ☐ **By Reinforcement Form:** Fibre-reinforced | Particle-reinforced | Structural (Laminates & Sandwich) - ☐ **By Reinforcement Material:** Glass (GFRP) | Carbon (CFRP) | Aramid (AFRP) | Boron (BFRP) - ☐ **Fibre-Reinforced** is **anisotropic**; **Particle-Reinforced** is **isotropic**. **🔑 KEY FORMULAS (Rule of Mixtures)** - ☐ $V_f + V_m = 1$ — always, for any 2-phase composite. - ☐ $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ — exact (mass is additive). - ☐ $E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **Voigt / Iso-strain / Upper Bound** (parallel loading). - ☐ $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ — **Reuss / Iso-stress / Lower Bound** (transverse loading). - ☐ $\sigma_{c,\parallel} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ — composite strength. - ☐ $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ — critical fibre length; use fibres with $l/d \geq 10$ as a safe design rule. **🔑 NUMERICAL PROBLEM PATTERNS** - ☐ **Pattern 1:** Given $V_f$ and constituent properties → find $E_{c,\parallel}$, $E_{c,\perp}$, $\rho_c$, $\sigma_{c,\parallel}$. - ☐ **Pattern 2:** Given desired composite modulus → find required $V_f$ (algebraic inversion of RoM). - ☐ **Pattern 3:** Given $d$, $\sigma_f$, $\tau_i$ → find $l_c$ and decide if a given fibre length is sufficient. - ☐ **Always** include units in your final answer; **always** identify whether you are using Voigt (parallel) or Reuss (perpendicular) before substituting. **🔑 COMMON EXAM TRAPS** - ☐ Mixing up Voigt and Reuss — **Voigt is the upper bound (parallel/stiff direction)**, Reuss is the lower bound (transverse direction). - ☐ Forgetting $V_m = 1 - V_f$ before substitution. - ☐ Treating density RoM as an approximation — it is **exact** for closed composites. - ☐ Using weight fraction when volume fraction is given — these are **not interchangeable** (especially when $\rho_f \neq \rho_m$). **🔑 ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS TO REMEMBER** - ☐ **GFRP** → Boat hulls, storage tanks, sports goods (low cost). - ☐ **CFRP** → Aircraft structures, F1 chassis, satellite components (high stiffness). - ☐ **AFRP (Kevlar)** → Ballistic armour, tyres, ropes (high toughness). - ☐ **Al-SiC MMC** → Brake rotors, piston crowns, electronics packaging. - ☐ **SiC-SiC CMC** → Jet engine hot-section components, space re-entry tiles. **🔑 EXPECTED KTU 2024 OUTCOME MAPPING** - ☐ **CO3 (Module 3 typical):** *"Understand the classification, structure, and properties of composite materials."* - ☐ **Bloom's Level:** Apply (Level 3) + Analyse (Level 4) — numerical problems on RoM and critical fibre length are the most common test items. ---

topics.title: Fiber-Reinforced Composites: Properties and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fiber-Reinforced Composites: Properties and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Fiber-Reinforced Composite (FRC)** is a heterogeneous, multi-phase engineering material composed of two or more distinct constituents combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material system whose mechanical, thermal, and physical properties are **superior to those of the individual components acting alone**. The two principal phases are:

- **Matrix Phase (Continuous Phase):** The binder material (polymer, metal, or ceramic) that surrounds, supports, and transfers load to the reinforcement through shear at the fiber–matrix interface.
- **Reinforcement Phase (Discontinuous/Fiber Phase):** High-strength, high-stiffness fibers (glass, carbon, aramid, boron, or natural) that serve as the principal load-bearing constituent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A composite is *"a structural material created by the intelligent combination of two or more distinct phases to obtain a product with properties not available in the individual constituents."* The fiber carries the load, while the matrix protects the fibers, distributes the load, and maintains fiber orientation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine building a **reinforced concrete structure**. Concrete (the matrix) is strong in compression but weak in tension. To compensate, we embed steel rebar (the fibers) inside it. The steel carries tensile loads, while the concrete holds everything in place and resists compression. Neither material alone performs as well as the combination.

**A Fiber-Reinforced Composite works on the same principle:**

| Analogy Element | Real Engineering Counterpart |
|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Concrete | Polymer / Metal / Ceramic matrix |
| Steel rebar | Glass / Carbon / Aramid fibers |
| Bond between rebar & concrete | Fiber–matrix interfacial shear |
| Reinforced concrete column | Aircraft wing spar / Bike frame / Pressure vessel |

The **interface** (the bond region between fiber and matrix) is critical — it is the "invisible welder" that transfers stress. A weak bond causes the composite to fail prematurely, even if both phases are individually excellent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Composites?** The driver is the **specific property ratio** — Strength/Density or Modulus/Density. Carbon-fiber composites can achieve specific strength values **5–10× higher than structural steel**, enabling lightweight aerospace and automotive structures.

### 1.3 Classification of FRCs (KTU Module 3 Highlight)

$$
\text{FRC Classification}
\begin{cases}
\text{By Matrix Type}\begin{cases}
\text{Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)} \\ \text{Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)} \\ \text{Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)}\end{cases} \\
\text{By Fiber Type}\begin{cases}
\text{Glass Fiber (GFRP)} \\ \text{Carbon Fiber (CFRP)} \\ \text{Aramid Fiber (AFRP)} \\ \text{Boron / Natural Fibers}\end{cases} \\
\text{By Fiber Length}\begin{cases}
\text{Continuous Fiber Composites} \\ \text{Short / Discontinuous Fiber Composites}\end{cases} \\
\text{By Fiber Orientation}\begin{cases}
\text{Unidirectional (UD)} \\ \text{Woven / Bidirectional} \\ \text{Random / Mat}\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Load Transfer in FRC

The mechanism by which an FRC carries load involves three sequential events:

1. **Matrix receives external load** — the matrix is the first phase to be stressed because it surrounds the fiber.
2. **Stress is transferred to the fiber through the interface** — the matrix deforms more than the stiff fiber, generating **interfacial shear stress ($\tau_i$)** along the fiber surface.
3. **Fiber carries the majority of tensile load** — because fibers are significantly stiffer and stronger, they elongate less and thus attract the higher stress.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 3-mark question asks *"How is load transferred from the matrix to the fiber?"* The answer must explicitly mention **shear at the interface** and **differential strain**.

### 2.2 Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)

The **critical fiber length** is the minimum length a fiber must have for its central region to reach the ultimate tensile strength of the fiber material. Shorter fibers cannot be fully loaded to their ultimate strength.

### 2.3 Fiber Volume Fraction vs. Fiber Weight Fraction

For design calculations, **volume fraction is preferred** because it directly governs mechanical behavior (stiffness, strength are extensive in nature when scaled with volume). However, weight fraction is easier to measure experimentally.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print/Remember this table for the ESE — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU FRP question.**

| # | Property / Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Composite Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | $V_f + V_m = 1$ |
| 2 | Rule of Mixtures — Modulus (UD, parallel loading) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Iso-strain condition |
| 3 | Rule of Mixtures — Modulus (transverse loading) | $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ | GPa | Iso-stress condition |
| 4 | Rule of Mixtures — Strength (UD) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | MPa | Valid when both fail simultaneously |
| 5 | Critical Fiber Length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | mm | $d$ = fiber diameter, $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear |
| 6 | Aspect Ratio | $\alpha = l/d$ | dimensionless | Must be $> l_c/d$ for effective reinforcement |
| 7 | Volume-to-Weight Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$ | dimensionless | Conversion formula |
| 8 | Specific Strength | $\sigma_c / \rho_c$ | $\text{MPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3 / \text{g}$ | Aerospace design metric |
| 9 | Specific Modulus | $E_c / \rho_c$ | $\text{GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3 / \text{g}$ | Stiffness-to-weight |
| 10 | Fiber Efficiency (length) | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$ | dimensionless | $< 1$ for short fibers |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 use **>50% CFRP** by mass to reduce weight and improve fuel efficiency.
- **Automotive:** BMW i3, Tesla Model S structural components use carbon fiber for crash-energy absorption and weight reduction.
- **Civil Engineering:** GFRP rebars replace steel in corrosion-prone marine bridge decks.
- **Sports:** Tennis rackets, F1 chassis, bicycle frames, hockey sticks — all exploit high **specific stiffness**.
- **Wind Energy:** CFRP used in 100 m+ wind turbine blades to reduce tip deflection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Rule of Mixtures assumes **perfect bonding** and **uniform fiber distribution**. In practice, voids, fiber clustering, and weak interfaces reduce the actual composite properties — a common 7-mark exam question theme.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus ($E_c$)

**Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain, $\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m$). This is valid for **continuous, unidirectional fibers** loaded parallel to the fiber axis.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium on the composite cross-section:**

The total load carried by the composite equals the sum of the loads carried by fiber and matrix.

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express force as stress × area:**

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Apply iso-strain condition ($\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m$):**

Since stress = modulus × strain ($\sigma = E \epsilon$), we substitute:

$$
E_c \epsilon_c A_c = E_f \epsilon_f A_f + E_m \epsilon_m A_m
$$

Because $\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m = \epsilon$, we cancel $\epsilon$ from all terms:

$$
E_c A_c = E_f A_f + E_m A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Divide throughout by $A_c$ and introduce volume fractions:**

Recall that $V_f = A_f / A_c$ and $V_m = A_m / A_c$. Therefore:

$$
E_c = E_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

> **This is the famous Rule of Mixtures for stiffness under longitudinal loading.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)

**Assumption:** A short fiber is embedded in the matrix. Load is transferred into the fiber through interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting on the cylindrical fiber surface.

**Step 1 — Consider force balance on a short fiber segment of length $l$ and diameter $d$:**

The tensile force in the fiber is balanced by the shear force on its lateral surface.

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the expression:**

The factor $l/2$ appears because the shear stress builds up from both ends of the fiber toward the center (maximum tensile stress occurs at the mid-plane).

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{2 \tau_i l}{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Define critical condition — fiber just reaches ultimate strength ($\sigma_f = \sigma_{fu}$):**

Setting $\sigma_f = \sigma_{fu}$ and $l = l_c$:

$$
\sigma_{fu} = \frac{2 \tau_i l_c}{d}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $l_c$:**

$$
\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Memory Trick:** *"Stress times Diameter over 2 times Shear"* — memorize as $\sigma d / 2\tau$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volume Fraction to Weight Fraction Conversion

**Step 1 — Define weight fractions:**

$$
W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_c}, \quad W_m = \frac{m_m}{m_c}, \quad W_f + W_m = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Express mass in terms of density and volume:**

$$
m_f = \rho_f V_f, \quad m_m = \rho_m V_m
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into weight fraction definition:**

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize using $V_f + V_m = 1$:**

Divide numerator and denominator by $V_f$:

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_f + \rho_m (V_m / V_f)}
$$

> **Final compact conversion formula:**

$$
\boxed{V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has the following properties:
- $V_f = 0.45$, $V_m = 0.55$
- $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- $\sigma_{fu} = 2.0$ GPa, fiber diameter $d = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, $\tau_i = 25$ MPa

**Find:** (a) $E_c$, (b) $\rho_c$, (c) $l_c$, (d) Specific Modulus.

**Solution (a) — Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$
E_c = (72 \times 0.45) + (3.5 \times 0.55) = 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325\ \text{GPa}
$$

**Solution (b) — Composite Density:**

$$
\rho_c = (2.55 \times 0.45) + (1.20 \times 0.55) = 1.1475 + 0.66 = 1.8075\ \text{g/cm}^3
$$

**Solution (c) — Critical Fiber Length:**

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 25} = \frac{0.02}{50} = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.4\ \text{mm}
$$

**Solution (d) — Specific Modulus:**

$$
\frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{34.325}{1.8075} = 18.99\ \text{GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3 / \text{g}
$$

> Compare to aluminum: $E/\rho \approx 70/2.7 \approx 25.9$ (higher) and structural steel: $\approx 25.5$ (higher). The advantage of FRC emerges when **strength-to-weight** and **fatigue resistance** are considered.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — FRC Architecture & Load Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FRC System] --> B[Matrix Phase]
    A --> C[Fiber Phase]
    A --> D[Interface Region]
    B --> B1[Polymer Epoxy]
    B --> B2[Metal Aluminum]
    B --> B3[Ceramic SiC]
    C --> C1[Glass Fiber]
    C --> C2[Carbon Fiber]
    C --> C3[Aramid Fiber]
    D --> D1[Mechanical Interlock]
    D --> D2[Chemical Bonding]
```
