However, if two letters are printed closer than the wavelength of visible light, they will blur into one — this is the **diffraction limit**. To "see" finer details (like atoms), we must switch from light waves to much shorter waves — electrons! ### Key Components - **Objective lens** (primary magnification) - **Eyepiece lens** (secondary magnification) - **Illuminator** (light source) - **Condenser lens** (focuses light onto specimen) - **Stage** (specimen platform) ### Types of Optical Microscopy 1. **Bright Field Microscopy** — Most common; background appears bright, features appear dark. 2. **Dark Field Microscopy** — Background is dark; features (e.g., defects, particles) appear bright. Useful for transparent samples. 3. **Polarized Light Microscopy** — Uses polarized light; identifies anisotropic materials, crystals, and polymers. 4. **Phase Contrast Microscopy** — Converts small phase differences in light into contrast; excellent for biological and transparent samples. --- ## 1.3 Electron Microscopy ### Why Electron Microscopes? Because of the **Abbe Diffraction Limit**, optical microscopes cannot resolve features smaller than ~200 nm. Electrons, being particles, behave as waves with extremely short wavelengths (≈ 0.005 nm at 100 kV), enabling much higher resolution. ### De Broglie Wavelength Relation The wavelength of an accelerated electron is governed by: $$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$$ Where: - $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** - $m$ = mass of electron = **9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg** - $e$ = electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** - $V$ = accelerating voltage (volts) ### Two Principal Types | Type | Acronym | Examines | |------|---------|----------| | Scanning Electron Microscopy | **SEM** | Surface topography (top few μm) | | Transmission Electron Microscopy | **TEM** | Internal structure (electron-transparent thin foil) | --- ## 1.4 Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) **Scanning Probe Microscopy** refers to a family of techniques that image surfaces by physically scanning a sharp probe across the specimen surface. The two most important SPMs are: - **STM** — Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (1981, Nobel Prize 1986) - **AFM** — Atomic Force Microscopy (1986) > [!NOTE] > **Key Difference:** STM requires a **conductive sample** because it relies on electron tunneling current. AFM works on **any sample** (insulators included) because it measures force between probe and surface. --- ## 1.5 Surface Analysis Techniques These techniques probe only the **top 1–10 atomic layers** of a material. | Technique | Full Name | Probes | |-----------|-----------|--------| | **XPS** | X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy | Elemental composition, chemical bonding | | **AES** | Auger Electron Spectroscopy | Elemental composition (light elements) | | **EDX/EDS** | Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy | Local elemental composition (often coupled with SEM) | | **SIMS** | Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry | Trace elemental/isotopic analysis | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Resolution — The Heart of Microscopy **Resolution** is the *minimum distance* between two distinguishable points. It is governed by **Abbe's Diffraction Limit**: $$d = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{NA}$$ Where: - $d$ = minimum resolvable distance (resolution limit) - $\lambda$ = wavelength of illuminating radiation - $NA$ = Numerical Aperture of the objective lens $$NA = n \sin\theta$$ Where: - $n$ = refractive index of the medium between lens and specimen - $\theta$ = half-angle of the cone of light entering the lens > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** By using *immersion oil* (n ≈ 1.5) instead of air (n ≈ 1.0), one can increase NA and achieve higher resolution in optical microscopy. ### Why Shorter Wavelength = Better Resolution To resolve atomic-scale features (~0.2 nm), we need radiation whose wavelength is smaller than that distance. Since visible light has λ ≈ 400–700 nm, we switch to: - **UV light** (λ ≈ 200 nm) → limited improvement - **Electrons** (λ ≈ 0.001–0.01 nm) → excellent resolution - **X-rays** (λ ≈ 0.05–0.25 nm) → used for crystallography --- ## 2.2 Detailed Working — SEM ### Operational Principle (Step-by-Step) 1. **Electron Generation:** A tungsten filament (or LaB₆, or field-emission tip) emits electrons via thermionic or field emission. 2. **Acceleration:** Electrons are accelerated by a high voltage (**1–30 kV**). 3. **Focusing:** Electromagnetic condenser lenses focus the beam into a fine probe (~1–10 nm diameter). 4. **Scanning:** Scanning coils deflect the beam in a raster pattern across the specimen surface. 5. **Signal Generation:** Interaction of beam with sample produces: - **Secondary Electrons (SE)** — topographical contrast (most common) - **Backscattered Electrons (BSE)** — atomic number (Z) contrast - **Characteristic X-rays** → EDX analysis 6. **Image Formation:** Detectors collect emitted signals; a CRT/digital display reconstructs the image pixel-by-pixel. ### SEM Parameters - **Magnification:** 10× to 500,000× - **Resolution:** ~1–10 nm - **Depth of Field:** Very large (hundreds of times greater than optical) - **Sample Requirement:** Solid, vacuum-compatible, often coated with a thin conductive layer (Au, Pt, or C) if non-conductive. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** SEM is the workhorse of failure analysis — fracture surfaces in fatigue, ductile, and brittle fracture can be readily identified by characteristic dimples, river patterns, and cleavage facets. --- ## 2.3 Detailed Working — TEM ### Operational Principle 1. Electron beam is generated at the top of the column. 2. Accelerated to **60–300 kV** (giving λ ≈ 0.0017–0.0037 nm). 3. Beam passes through an **ultrathin specimen** (typically < 200 nm thick) — *electron-transparent*. 4. Transmitted electrons (and diffracted electrons) pass through objective, intermediate, and projector lenses. 5. Image is formed on a phosphor screen or CCD camera. ### TEM Imaging Modes - **Bright Field (BF):** Direct beam forms image — regions that scatter electrons appear dark. - **Dark Field (DF):** Diffracted beam forms image — useful for visualizing defects, precipitates. - **High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM):** Achieves atomic lattice resolution (~0.1 nm). ### TEM Parameters - **Magnification:** up to 1,000,000× or more - **Resolution:** ~0.1–0.2 nm (atomic resolution possible) - **Sample Requirement:** Ultrathin foil (prepared by ion milling, electropolishing, or ultramicrotomy). --- ## 2.4 Detailed Working — STM ### Principle A sharp metallic tip (often W or Pt-Ir) is brought within **~1 nm** of a conducting sample. A small bias voltage (**0.01–1 V**) applied between tip and sample causes electrons to **tunnel** through the vacuum gap. ### Tunneling Current Equation $$I \propto V \, e^{-2\kappa d}$$ Where: - $V$ = bias voltage - $d$ = tip–sample separation - $\kappa$ = decay constant (≈ 0.1 nm⁻¹ in vacuum) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this works:** A 0.1 nm change in tip–sample distance changes the current by an order of magnitude. This extreme sensitivity enables **atomic-scale resolution** (~0.01 nm vertical, 0.1 nm lateral). ### Operating Modes - **Constant Current Mode:** Tip is moved vertically to maintain constant I; vertical motion gives topography. - **Constant Height Mode:** Tip height is fixed; current variations give topography. --- ## 2.5 Detailed Working — AFM ### Principle A sharp tip (radius ~ 10 nm) mounted on a flexible cantilever scans the surface. **Interatomic forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, frictional) deflect the cantilever, measured by a laser beam reflected onto a photodiode. ### Force Regimes | Regime | Distance | Dominant Force | |--------|----------|----------------| | Contact | < 0.5 nm | Repulsive (Pauli exclusion) | | Non-contact | 1–10 nm | Attractive (van der Waals) | | Tapping | Intermittent | Both, intermittent contact | ### AFM Advantages - Works on **insulators, polymers, biological samples** - Provides **3D topographic maps** - Resolution: ~0.1 nm vertical, ~1 nm lateral --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Key Constants / Units | |---|---------|-------------|------------------------| | 1 | $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ | Abbe resolution limit | d in nm, λ in nm | | 2 | $NA = n \sin\theta$ | Numerical aperture | dimensionless | | 3 | $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$ | de Broglie wavelength of electron | λ in nm; V in volts | | 4 | $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{eye}$ | Total magnification | dimensionless | | 5 | $I \propto V \, e^{-2\kappa d}$ | STM tunneling current | I in nA | | 6 | $F = -k \, z$ | AFM cantilever deflection | F in N, k in N/m | | 7 | $E_{photon} = h\nu$ | XPS photon energy | eV | | 8 | $E_{kinetic} = h\nu - E_{binding} - \phi$ | XPS energy equation | eV | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Field Applications:** > - **SEM/TEM:** Failure analysis, quality control of welds, semiconductor device inspection. > - **AFM/STM:** Nanotechnology, thin-film characterization, semiconductor metrology. > - **XPS/AES:** Corrosion studies, adhesion failure analysis, catalyst surface chemistry. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron ### Objective Derive an explicit expression for the wavelength (in nm) of an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ volts. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Energy conservation.** When an electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, the work done by the electric field becomes kinetic energy: $$\begin{aligned} eV &= \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} \end{aligned}$$ This is the final velocity of the electron after acceleration. **Step 2 — de Broglie hypothesis.** According to Louis de Broglie, every moving particle has an associated wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the expression for v.** $$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{h}{m \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}} \\ &= \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}} \end{aligned}$$ This is the non-relativistic form. **Step 4 — Convert to nanometres for convenience.** Substituting numerical values of $h$, $m$, $e$: $$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V}} \text{ m} \\ \lambda &= \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** For $V = 100 \text{ kV} = 10^{5} \text{ V}$: $$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{10^{5}}} \\ &= \frac{1.227}{316.23} \\ &\approx 3.88 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} \\ &= 0.00388 \text{ nm} \end{aligned}$$ This wavelength is roughly **100,000× shorter** than visible light, explaining the vastly superior resolution of electron microscopes. > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** For an electron accelerated at 50 kV: > $\lambda \approx 1.227 / \sqrt{50000} \approx 1.227 / 223.6 \approx 0.00549 \text{ nm} \approx 0.0055 \text{ nm}$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optical Resolution Improvement via Immersion Oil **Step 1 — Recall Abbe's equation:** $$d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{NA} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{n \sin\theta}$$ **Step 2 — Without oil (air medium):** $n_{air} = 1.00$ $$d_{air} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{1.00 \cdot \sin\theta} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{\sin\theta}$$ **Step 3 — With immersion oil:** $n_{oil} \approx 1.515$ $$d_{oil} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{1.515 \cdot \sin\theta}$$ **Step 4 — Ratio:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d_{oil}}{d_{air}} &= \frac{1}{1.515} \\ d_{oil} &= \frac{d_{air}}{1.515} \\ d_{oil} &\approx 0.66 \, d_{air} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion.** Immersion oil reduces the diffraction-limited distance by ~34%, improving resolution. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — STM Sensitivity to Gap Distance **Step 1 — Tunneling current relation:** $$I = I_{0} \, e^{-2\kappa d}$$ **Step 2 — Take natural logarithm:** $$\ln I = \ln I_{0} - 2\kappa d$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to d:** $$\frac{d(\ln I)}{dd} = -2\kappa$$ **Step 4 — Sensitivity quantification:** A small change $\Delta d$ produces a fractional current change: $$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = -2\kappa \, \Delta d$$ For $\kappa \approx 0.1 \text{ nm}^{-1}$ and $\Delta d = 0.1 \text{ nm}$: $$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = -2 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 = -0.02 = -2\% \text{ per 0.1 nm}$$ But exponentially, **a doubling of distance cuts current by ~e⁰·² ≈ 1.22 factor**, while halving distance boosts it by ~1.22. This is why STM is so sensitive. --- ## 3.4 Comparative Resolution Table | Technique | Radiation / Probe | Wavelength | Practical Resolution | Max Magnification | |-----------|-------------------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------| | Optical (visible) | Visible light | 400–700 nm | ~200 nm | ~1500× | | UV Microscopy | UV light | 200–400 nm | ~100 nm | ~2000× | | SEM | Electrons (1–30 kV) | ~0.005–0.009 nm | ~1–10 nm | ~500,000× | | TEM | Electrons (60–300 kV) | ~0.0017–0.0048 nm | ~0.1 nm | >1,000,000× | | STM | Tunneling electrons | — | ~0.01 nm vertical | n/a | | AFM | Tip–sample force | — | ~0.1 nm vertical, ~1 nm lateral | n/a | --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Technique to Use? | Application | Best Technique | Reason | |-------------|----------------|--------| | Grain size measurement in steel | Optical Microscopy | Cost-effective, simple sample prep | | Fracture surface morphology | SEM | High depth of field, large range of magnifications | | Dislocation imaging | TEM | Atomic-scale resolution, internal defects | | Atomic lattice imaging | HRTEM | Sub-Å resolution possible | | Surface atom arrangement (conductor) | STM | Atomic resolution | | Surface atom arrangement (insulator) | AFM | Conductive sample not required | | Chemical bonding state of surface | XPS | Direct bonding information | | Light element composition (e.g., C, N, O) | AES | High sensitivity for low Z | | Trace elemental analysis | SIMS | ppm–ppb sensitivity | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Electron Source
Tungsten Filament / LaB6 / FEG] --> B[Accelerating Anode
1 to 30 kV] B --> C[Condenser Lens 1
Initial Focusing] C --> D[Condenser Lens 2
Beam Convergence] D --> E[Scanning Coils
Raster Pattern] E --> F[Objective Lens
Final Probe Formation] F --> G[Specimen Stage
X Y Z Movement] G --> H[Signal Generation Zone
SE / BSE / X-ray] H --> I[Secondary Electron Detector] H --> J[Backscatter Detector] H --> K[EDX Detector] I --> L[Image Processing Unit] J --> L K --> L L --> M[Display Monitor
SEM Image] L --> N[Spectrum Analyzer
EDX Spectrum] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Electron flow goes top-to-bottom from source through lenses to specimen; signal flow goes back up from specimen through detectors to the display. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Electron Gun
High Voltage 60 to 300 kV] --> B[Condenser Lens System
Beam Conditioning] B --> C[Specimen Stage
Ultrathin Foil] C --> D[Objective Lens
Primary Image Formation] D --> E[Intermediate Lens
Magnification Selection] E --> F[Projector Lens
Final Image] F --> G[Phosphor Screen or CCD Camera] D --> H[Aperture Selector] H --> I[Diffraction Pattern Mode] I --> G ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Laser Source] --> B[Mirror] B --> C[Reflects Beam Onto Cantilever Tip] C --> D[Sharp Tip
Radius 10 nm] D --> E[Sample Surface
Nanoscale Features] D --> F[Deflected Cantilever] F --> G[Laser Reflects Off Cantilever] G --> H[Position Sensitive Photodiode
PSD] H --> I[Feedback Controller] I --> J[Piezo Scanner Z] J --> D H --> K[Computer Display
3D Topography] ``` --- ## 4.4 Signal Generation in Electron–Sample Interaction (SEM/TEM) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Primary Electron Beam
High Energy] --> B[Sample Atoms] B --> C[Secondary Electrons
SE - Topography] B --> D[Backscattered Electrons
BSE - Z Contrast] B --> E[Characteristic X-rays
EDX - Elemental] B --> F[Auger Electrons
AES - Surface] B --> G[Transmitted Electrons
TEM Imaging] B --> H[Cathodoluminescence
Optical Emission] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Favorite Question:** *"Distinguish between secondary electrons (SE) and backscattered electrons (BSE) in SEM imaging."* SE originate from the top few nm and give topographic contrast; BSE originate from deeper regions and carry compositional (Z) contrast. --- ## 4.5 Comparative Hierarchy of Microscopy Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Microscopy Techniques] --> B[Optical Microscopy] A --> C[Electron Microscopy] A --> D[Scanning Probe Microscopy] B --> B1[Bright Field] B --> B2[Dark Field] B --> B3[Polarized Light] B --> B4[Phase Contrast] C --> C1[SEM - Surface] C --> C2[TEM - Internal] C --> C3[HRTEM - Atomic] D --> D1[STM - Conductors] D --> D2[AFM - All Materials] D --> D3[MFM - Magnetic] D --> D4[STM E - Electrochemical] ``` --- ## 4.6 Surface Analysis Techniques — Signal Origin ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Incident Radiation] --> B[Sample Surface Top 1 to 10 nm] B --> C[Emitted Signal] C --> D[Detector and Analyzer] A1[X-ray Photons for XPS] --> A A2[Electron Beam for AES] --> A A3[Focused Ion Beam for SIMS] --> A C1[Photoelectrons] --> D C2[Auger Electrons] --> D C3[Secondary Ions] --> D D --> E1[XPS Spectrum - Binding Energy eV] D --> E2[AES Spectrum - Kinetic Energy eV] D --> E3[SIMS Mass Spectrum - m/z] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define Numerical Aperture (NA). What is its significance in optical microscopy?** **Model Answer:** Numerical Aperture is defined as: $$NA = n \sin\theta$$ where *n* is the refractive index of the medium between the lens and specimen, and *θ* is the half-angle of the maximum cone of light entering the objective lens. **Significance:** 1. NA directly governs the resolving power of a microscope — higher NA → better (smaller *d*) resolution per Abbe's equation. 2. NA also controls the light-gathering ability (brightness) of the objective. 3. Using immersion oil (n ≈ 1.515) instead of air (n = 1.00) increases NA and hence resolution. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: State the principle of Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM).** **Model Answer:** STM works on the principle of **quantum mechanical tunneling**. A sharp conductive tip (typically tungsten or Pt-Ir) is brought within ~1 nm of a conducting specimen surface. When a small bias voltage (mV to V range) is applied, electrons *tunnel* through the vacuum gap, producing a measurable tunneling current that depends exponentially on the tip–sample separation: $$I \propto V \, e^{-2\kappa d}$$ By scanning the tip across the surface and maintaining either constant current or constant height, an atomic-scale topographic map is produced with vertical resolution ~0.01 nm and lateral resolution ~0.1 nm. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]** **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the construction and working principle of a **Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and applications in materials characterization. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** With suitable sketches, explain the working of **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)**. Compare SEM and TEM in terms of imaging mode, sample preparation, resolution, and applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q. A(a) — SEM Construction and Working **1. Construction (Block Diagram):** The SEM consists of the following major components arranged vertically along an evacuated column: 1. **Electron Gun:** Produces electrons by thermionic emission (W filament, LaB₆ crystal) or field emission. 2. **Anode Plate:** Accelerates electrons to energies of 1–30 kV. 3. **Condenser Lenses (typically 2):** Demagnify the electron source to form a fine probe. 4. **Objective Lens (Final Lens):** Focuses the probe to a spot size of ~1–10 nm on the specimen. 5. **Scanning Coils:** Deflect the beam in a raster (TV-like) pattern. 6. **Specimen Stage:** X-Y-Z movement with tilt and rotation facilities. 7. **Detectors:** Everhart–Thornley detector (SE), BSE detector, EDX detector. 8. **Vacuum System:** Rotary + diffusion pumps; pressure ~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Pa. 9. **Display System:** CRT or digital monitor. **2. Working Principle:** - Electrons emitted from the gun are accelerated and focused into a fine beam. - Scanning coils raster the beam across the specimen surface. - Beam–specimen interactions generate: - **Secondary Electrons (SE):** Low energy (< 50 eV); from top ~5–10 nm; give topographic contrast. - **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** High energy; from ~100 nm depth; atomic-number contrast. - **Characteristic X-rays:** Used for EDX elemental analysis. - Detectors convert emitted signals into electrical pulses, which are displayed synchronously with the raster scan, forming an image pixel-by-pixel. **3. Advantages:** - Large depth of field (hundreds of times optical) — 3D-looking images. - Wide magnification range: 10× to 500,000×. - Resolution: ~1–10 nm. - Non-destructive (with care) and rapid. - Coupling with EDX enables elemental microanalysis. **4. Limitations:** - Specimens must be vacuum-compatible. - Non-conductive samples need conductive coating (Au-sputter, Pt, or C evaporation). - Cannot image internal features (only surface). - Beam damage possible for sensitive materials (polymers, biological). - Initial cost high. **5. Applications:** - Fracture surface analysis (ductile/brittle, fatigue, creep). - Failure analysis of engineering components. - Microstructural examination of metals, ceramics, polymers. - Quality control of welds and castings. - Semiconductor device inspection. --- #### Model Solution for Q. A(b) — TEM and Comparison **1. Working of TEM:** - High-energy electron beam (60–300 kV) is generated by an electron gun (thermionic or field emission). - Beam is focused by condenser lenses onto an ultrathin specimen (< 200 nm thick) prepared by ion milling, electropolishing, or ultramicrotomy. - Electrons transmitted through the specimen are focused by objective, intermediate, and projector lenses to form a magnified image on a phosphor screen or CCD camera. - An aperture in the back-focal plane selects either the direct beam (Bright Field) or a diffracted beam (Dark Field) for image formation. **2. Comparison SEM vs TEM:** | Parameter | SEM | TEM | |-----------|-----|-----| | Detected Signal | SE, BSE, X-rays | Transmitted electrons | | Image Type | Surface topography | Internal structure | | Resolution | ~1–10 nm | ~0.1–0.2 nm | | Max Magnification | ~500,000× | >1,000,000× | | Sample Thickness | Bulk (mm) | Thin foil (< 200 nm) | | Sample Preparation | Simple | Complex, time-consuming | | Vacuum | High vacuum | Ultra-high vacuum | | Depth of Field | Very large | Limited | | Cost | Moderate | High | | Typical Use | Topography, fractography | Dislocations, atomic lattice | --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]** **Q: (a)** Describe the construction, working, and applications of **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)**. Discuss its different operating modes. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Explain the following surface analysis techniques with their principles: **(i) X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS)** **(ii) Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES)** **(iii) Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (SIMS)** **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q. B(a) — AFM **1. Construction:** - **Cantilever:** A flexible beam (Si or Si₃N₄) of spring constant 0.01–100 N/m. - **Sharp Tip:** Integrated at the end of the cantilever, tip radius ~10 nm. - **Piezoelectric Scanner:** X, Y, Z motion with sub-nanometre precision. - **Laser–Photodiode System:** A laser reflected off the cantilever top onto a position-sensitive detector (PSD) measures cantilever deflection. - **Feedback Loop:** Maintains constant deflection (or constant amplitude in tapping mode) by adjusting the Z piezo voltage. **2. Working Principle:** The tip–sample interatomic force (attractive van der Waals or repulsive Pauli) deflects the cantilever according to: $$F = -k \, \Delta z$$ where *k* is the spring constant and Δz is the deflection. The PSD detects this deflection and the feedback loop adjusts the tip's vertical position. The recorded Z positions vs (X, Y) give the 3D topographic map. **3. Operating Modes:** - **Contact Mode:** Tip is in constant repulsive contact. Best for hard samples; high lateral resolution; risk of tip wear. - **Non-Contact Mode:** Tip oscillates above sample in attractive regime. No tip wear; lower resolution; sensitive to contamination layer. - **Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact):** Tip oscillates and intermittently contacts the surface. Combines advantages — good resolution, minimal damage, works on soft samples (polymers, biological). **4. Advantages:** - Works on insulating and conductive samples. - Operates in air, liquid, or vacuum. - True 3D topographical data (not just projection). - Sub-nanometre vertical resolution. - No special sample preparation (compared to TEM). **5. Limitations:** - Limited scan area (~100 μm × 100 μm). - Slow imaging (minutes per scan). - Tip convolution can distort images. - Limited to surface; cannot probe internal structure. **6. Applications:** - Nanoscale surface roughness measurement. - Atomic-scale imaging of conductors and insulators. - Biological sample imaging (DNA, proteins). - Thin film thickness and morphology. - Nanotechnology and MEMS characterization. --- #### Model Solution for Q. B(b) — XPS, AES, SIMS **(i) X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) — Also called ESCA** - **Principle:** Sample surface is irradiated with monochromatic X-rays (typically Al Kα at 1486.6 eV or Mg Kα at 1253.6 eV). - X-ray photons eject core-level electrons (photoelectrons) from atoms in the top ~1–10 nm of the sample. - The kinetic energy of these photoelectrons is measured: $$E_{binding} = h\nu - E_{kinetic} - \phi$$ where φ is the work function of the spectrometer. - Each element has characteristic binding energies → identification. Small shifts in binding energy reveal **chemical state** (oxidation state, bonding). - **Applications:** Surface contamination analysis, oxidation studies, adhesion failure analysis. **(ii) Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES)** - **Principle:** A primary electron beam (3–10 keV) ionizes a core electron of a surface atom. - The atom relaxes by dropping a higher-level electron into the vacancy, releasing energy. - This energy ejects another electron — the **Auger electron** — with kinetic energy characteristic of the element. - Auger electrons have low energies (50–2000 eV) and escape only from the top ~1–5 nm. - **Applications:** Particularly sensitive to **light elements** (C, N, O, S) that XPS also handles; thin film composition; depth profiling via ion sputtering. **(iii) Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (SIMS)** - **Principle:** A focused primary ion beam (O₂⁺, Cs⁺, Ar⁺) bombards the surface. - Surface atoms are ejected as **secondary ions** (positive or negative). - These ions are collected and analyzed by a mass spectrometer (typically a quadrupole or time-of-flight analyzer). - **Sensitivity:** ppm to ppb level — the most sensitive surface technique. - **Modes:** - **Static SIMS:** Low primary dose; minimal surface damage; surface molecular info. - **Dynamic SIMS:** High primary dose; depth profiling; trace element mapping. - **Applications:** Dopant profiling in semiconductors, trace impurities, isotopic analysis. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Confusing SEM and TEM signal types:** SEM uses *surface-emitted* electrons (SE/BSE); TEM uses *transmitted* electrons. Mixing these up = marks lost. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Wavelength of electrons must be in **nanometres** when applied to resolution. Writing λ in metres without scaling = -1 mark. > 3. **Skipping the "why" of resolution:** Always mention the **Abbe diffraction limit** when justifying why electron microscopes outperform optical microscopes. > 4. **Incorrect XPS equation:** $E_{binding} = h\nu - E_{kinetic} - \phi$ — students often forget the work function term φ. > 5. **STM on insulators:** STM *cannot* image insulating samples. Always mention this when comparing STM and AFM. > 6. **Sample preparation not mentioned:** In SEM/TEM questions, examiners expect mention of conductive coating (SEM) and ultrathin foil preparation (TEM). Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4:** ### Microscopy Fundamentals - Resolution is governed by Abbe's equation: $d = 0.61\lambda / NA$ - Numerical Aperture: $NA = n\sin\theta$; immersion oil increases NA - Optical microscope maximum useful magnification ≈ 1000–1500× - Resolution limit of optical microscope ≈ 200 nm ### Electron Microscopy - de Broglie wavelength: $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2meV} \approx 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm - SEM: surface topography via SE (most common) and BSE; resolution ~1–10 nm - TEM: transmitted electrons through ultrathin foil (< 200 nm); resolution ~0.1 nm - Sample prep: SEM needs conductive coating for non-conductors; TEM needs ultrathin foil ### Scanning Probe Microscopy - STM: tunneling current $I \propto V e^{-2\kappa d}$; needs conductive sample - AFM: measures tip–sample force $F = -k\Delta z$; works on insulators - AFM modes: contact, non-contact, tapping - STM resolution: ~0.01 nm vertical, 0.1 nm lateral - AFM resolution: ~0.1 nm vertical, ~1 nm lateral ### Surface Analysis Techniques - **XPS:** photon in, photoelectron out; gives chemical bonding state; equation $E_B = h\nu - E_K - \phi$ - **AES:** electron in, Auger electron out; excellent for light elements - **EDX/EDS:** X-ray analysis (often coupled with SEM); gives elemental composition - **SIMS:** ion in, ion out; highest sensitivity (ppm–ppb); dopant profiling ### Critical Comparisons to Memorize - SEM vs TEM: signal type, sample thickness, resolution - STM vs AFM: sample conductivity requirement, physical principle - XPS vs AES: photon vs electron excitation, light element sensitivity - Optical vs Electron: wavelength, resolution, sample preparation ### Sample Prep Keywords to Use in Answers - SEM → "sputter-coated with gold/palladium for non-conductive samples" - TEM → "ultrathin foil prepared by ion beam milling or electropolishing" - AFM → "mounted on a flat substrate; no special prep required" - XPS/AES → "ultra-high vacuum; cleaned by Ar⁺ sputtering if needed" ### Typical 14-Mark Answer Structure (KTU Pattern) - Block diagram (3 marks) - Working principle (3 marks) - Advantages (2 marks) - Limitations (2 marks) - Applications (2 marks) - Comparison table if asked (2 marks)

topics.title: Optical Microscopy
Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)
Surface Profiling Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Surface Profiling Techniques # MODULE 4 — SURFACE PROFILING TECHNIQUES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Surface Profiling** is the quantitative measurement and characterization of the topography of a surface at micro- and nano-scales, expressed as a continuous trace of vertical displacement (z) versus lateral position (x) along a reference datum. The technique determines the **amplitude**, **spacing**, and **hybrid** surface texture parameters defined in **ISO 21920** (formerly ISO 4287) and **ASME Y14.36M**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Surface profiling is defined as the systematic acquisition of a 2-D cross-sectional profile of an engineering surface using contact (stylus) or non-contact (optical, electron, or scanning probe) methods, followed by mathematical reduction to standardized roughness, waviness, and form parameters. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to "draw" a mountain range on paper using only a pencil held in your hand, by letting a thin needle gently glide over the terrain. The pencil traces the ups and downs — peaks, valleys, ridges, and slopes. This is essentially what a **stylus profilometer** does with a diamond-tipped needle (stylus) skimming over a workpiece. The vertical needle movement is converted into an electrical signal by a **piezoelectric** or **inductive transducer** and plotted as a 2-D profile. For non-contact methods, think of a "flashlight" of laser light shining on the surface; the way the light scatters, focuses, or interferes with itself reveals the topography — like reading Braille with light instead of touch. ### 1.3 Classification Overview | Method Type | Sub-Technique | Typical Lateral Resolution | Typical Vertical Resolution | |-------------|---------------|---------------------------|----------------------------| | Contact (Stylus) | Stylus Profilometer (Talysurf) | ~0.2 µm | ~1 nm | | Optical | Confocal Microscopy | ~0.2 µm | ~1 nm | | Optical | White-Light Interferometry (WLI) | ~0.5 µm | ~0.1 nm | | Optical | Laser Triangulation | ~1 µm | ~0.1 µm | | Scanning Probe | Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) | Atomic scale (~0.1 nm) | ~0.01 nm | | Scanning Probe | Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) | Atomic scale (~0.05 nm) | ~0.005 nm | | Electron | SEM/TEM (indirect) | nm scale | nm scale | > [!NOTE] > **Standards Reference:** KTU examiners frequently cite **ISO 21920-1:2021** (replacing ISO 4287:1997) and **ISO 25178** for areal (3-D) surface texture parameters. Stylus-based 2-D profiling is governed by ISO 21920-1. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle of a Stylus Profilometer (Talysurf) A **Talysurf** (Taylor-Hobson) is the industry-standard 2-D contact profilometer. The operational sequence is: 1. **Sample Mounting:** The workpiece is placed on a precision **X-Y stage** with a datum reference. 2. **Stylus Traverse:** A diamond stylus of tip radius **r = 2 µm, 5 µm, or 10 µm** is drawn across the surface at a constant slow traverse speed (typically **0.1 to 1 mm/s**). 3. **Transduction:** The vertical (z-axis) displacement of the stylus is sensed by: - **Inductive (LVDT) transducer** — most common - **Piezoelectric transducer** — higher sensitivity - **Capacitive transducer** — used in some models 4. **Signal Conditioning:** The analog signal is filtered (high-pass for roughness, low-pass for form removal) and digitized. 5. **Parameter Extraction:** Software computes **Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt**, etc. ### 2.2 The Stylus Tip — The "Why" of Convolution Errors The diamond stylus tip is **NOT a point**; it has a finite radius (typically **2 µm**). This causes **geometric convolution** of the actual surface profile with the tip shape. Sharp valleys narrower than **2r** are not reached, and the measured depth is always less than the true valley depth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why":** If the actual surface has a narrow V-groove of width < 4 µm, the 2 µm radius tip will bridge the groove and report a shallower depth. This is the **tip-convolution error** and is the single most important reason why stylus profilometry is inadequate for highly precise micro-features. ### 2.3 Surface Texture Filtering — Separating Roughness, Waviness, and Form A real surface profile contains three superposed components: $$ z_{measured}(x) = z_{form}(x) + z_{waviness}(x) + z_{roughness}(x) $$ - **Form:** Long-wavelength geometric deviation (e.g., flatness, cylindricity) — typically > 5 mm - **Waviness:** Mid-wavelength periodic errors (e.g., machine tool vibration marks) — 0.8 mm to 5 mm - **Roughness:** Short-wavelength micro-geometry — < 0.8 mm The standard filters to separate these are: - **2RC Filter (50% / 50% Butterworth equivalent)** — older ISO 4287 standard - **Gaussian Filter (λc)** — current ISO 16610 standard, smoother, no phase distortion The **cut-off wavelength λc** is the key parameter — it defines the boundary between roughness and waviness. For Ra values, λc is selected from the Ra value itself: | Ra Range (µm) | Cut-off λc (mm) | Sampling Length l (mm) | Evaluation Length L (mm) | |---------------|-----------------|------------------------|--------------------------| | 0.006 – 0.02 | 0.08 | 0.08 | 0.48 | | 0.02 – 0.1 | 0.25 | 0.25 | 1.5 | | 0.1 – 2.0 | 0.8 | 0.8 | 4.8 | | 2.0 – 10.0 | 2.5 | 2.5 | 12.5 | | 10.0 – 80.0 | 8.0 | 8.0 | 40.0 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Hot Topic:** The ratio **L = 5 × l** (Evaluation length = 5 × Sampling length) is asked repeatedly in KTU exams. Remember it! ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Meaning | |-----------|--------|---------|------|---------| | Arithmetic Mean Roughness | **Ra** | $$R_a = \frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} \vert z(x) \vert\, dx$$ | µm | Average absolute deviation | | Root Mean Square Roughness | **Rq** | $$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} z^2(x)\, dx}$$ | µm | Standard deviation of heights | | Maximum Peak-to-Valley | **Rt** | $R_t = R_p^{max} - R_v^{max}$ | µm | Total range in sampling length | | Ten-Point Height | **Rz** | $R_z = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} R_{p,i} + \sum_{i=1}^{5} R_{v,i}}{5}$ | µm | Mean of 5 highest peaks and 5 lowest valleys | | Skewness | **Rsk** | $R_{sk} = \frac{1}{R_q^3}\cdot\frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} z^3(x)\, dx$ | — | Asymmetry of profile | | Kurtosis | **Rku** | $R_{ku} = \frac{1}{R_q^4}\cdot\frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} z^4(x)\, dx$ | — | Sharpness of height distribution | | Mean Spacing | **RSm** | $R_{Sm} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{mi}$ | mm | Average peak-to-peak spacing | | Hybrid Parameter | **Δa** | $\Delta a = \frac{\sum A_i}{l}$ | — | Average peak count per length | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions:** The integral is computed within the **sampling length l**, not the evaluation length L. For the l limits, $z(x)$ is the profile after **form removal** and **waviness suppression** (post-filtering at λc). ### 2.5 Optical Profilometry — White Light Interferometry (WLI) A Michelson-type interferometer splits a white light beam into a reference beam (reflected from a flat reference mirror) and a measurement beam (reflected from the sample). The two beams recombine and produce **interference fringes** only when the optical path difference is within the **coherence length** of the light source (~1 µm for white light). For each pixel (x, y), a vertical scan is performed to find the position of maximum fringe contrast → this gives the local height z(x, y). This produces a **3-D areal map** in seconds. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Coherence length** is the maximum path difference over which interference is possible. White light has a short coherence length (~1 µm) because of its broad spectrum; this gives the **z-axis localization** property that makes WLI so precise. ### 2.6 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) — Scanning Probe Principle An AFM uses a micro-fabricated **cantilever** (Si or Si₃N₄) with a sharp tip (radius **~ 5–20 nm**) at its end. As the tip scans the surface in **contact**, **tapping**, or **non-contact** mode, the cantilever deflects due to interatomic forces (van der Waals, repulsive Pauli, capillary, etc.). A laser beam reflected off the cantilever's back into a **position-sensitive detector (PSD / photodiode)** measures the deflection with sub-nanometer precision. The three modes of AFM operation are: - **Contact Mode:** Tip is in repulsive regime (force ~ 1–100 nN). Highest lateral resolution but can damage soft samples. - **Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact):** Cantilever oscillates near resonance (~ 50–500 kHz). Amplitude is the feedback signal. Best for soft samples. - **Non-Contact Mode:** Tip oscillates in attractive regime. Used for ultra-clean, atomically flat surfaces. The governing equation for the cantilever as a damped harmonic oscillator is: $$ m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = F_{ts}(d) $$ where **m** is effective mass, **c** is damping coefficient, **k** is spring constant, and **F_ts(d)** is the tip-sample interaction force at tip-sample distance **d**. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Why Surface Profiling Matters | |----------|------------------------------| | Automotive | Cylinder bore roughness affects oil retention and piston ring wear | | Aerospace | Turbine blade surface finish affects fatigue life and heat transfer | | Biomedical | Orthopedic implant surface texture controls osseointegration | | Semiconductor | Wafer flatness at nm-scale determines lithography yield | | Optical Components | Mirror roughness < 1 nm RMS required for X-ray / EUV optics | | Tribology | Real contact area (asperities) is 1–10% of apparent area, affects friction | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Ra Formula from First Principles We start with the **measured profile z(x)** of length **l**, after subtracting the mean line (center line) so that: $$ \frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} z(x)\, dx = 0 $$ **Step 1: Definition of the Mean Line** The mean line is positioned so that the areas of material above it equal the areas of voids below it: $$ \int_0^{l} z(x)\, dx = 0 $$ **Step 2: Absolute Deviation** At each point x, the absolute vertical deviation from the mean line is **|z(x)|**. **Step 3: Summation Over Infinitesimal Segments** The total area enclosed between the profile and the mean line (treating all deviations as positive) is: $$ A_{total} = \int_0^{l} \vert z(x) \vert\, dx $$ **Step 4: Average Height (Per Unit Length)** Dividing by the sampling length l: $$ R_a = \frac{A_{total}}{l} = \frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} \vert z(x) \vert\, dx $$ **Step 5: Discretized Form (Practical Computation)** For N sampled points $z_i$ (i = 1, 2, ..., N) at equal spacing Δx: $$ R_a = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i \vert $$ > [!NOTE] > **Relation to Standard Deviation:** Since the mean is zero by construction, **Ra** and the **standard deviation σ** of the heights are *not equal* — Ra is the mean absolute deviation, while σ (or Rq) is the RMS deviation. For a Gaussian surface, $R_a = 0.798 \sigma$ and $R_q = 1.0 \sigma$, giving the well-known identity $R_q \approx 1.25\, R_a$ for Gaussian surfaces. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Tip-Convolution Correction Consider a sharp V-groove of half-angle **α** and true depth **h_true**. A spherical tip of radius **r** touching the groove walls will not reach the bottom vertex. **Step 1: Geometry of Contact** The contact between the spherical tip and the inclined wall is at a point where the surface normal is parallel to the line from the sphere's center to the contact point. For a groove of half-angle α, the offset of the tip's contact from the true vertex is: $$ \Delta h = r\left(\frac{1}{\sin\alpha} - 1\right) $$ **Step 2: Measured Depth** $$ h_{measured} = h_{true} - r\left(\frac{1}{\sin\alpha} - 1\right) $$ **Step 3: Condition for Valid Profiling** For the tip to reach the bottom of the groove, we need $h_{measured} > 0$: $$ h_{true} > r\left(\frac{1}{\sin\alpha} - 1\right) $$ **Step 4: Limiting Case (Sharp Ridge)** For a sharp ridge (α → 90°, sin α = 1), Δh = 0 → no convolution error. This is why ridges are accurately profiled but valleys are not. **Step 5: Practical Example** For a 90° V-groove (α = 45°), r = 2 µm: - $\Delta h = 2 \left(\frac{1}{\sin 45°} - 1\right) = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) = 0.828$ µm - If $h_{true}$ = 1 µm, $h_{measured}$ = 0.172 µm → 83% under-reporting! > [!WARNING] > This derivation is **frequently asked** as a Part A 3-mark question. Memorize the formula and the limiting cases. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Gaussian Filter Transfer Function The Gaussian filter has a transmission characteristic in the spatial frequency domain that is purely amplitude (no phase shift): $$ G(\lambda) = \exp\left[-\pi \left(\frac{\lambda_c}{\lambda}\right)^2 \ln 2\right] $$ **Step 1: Definition** The Gaussian filter attenuates components with wavelength λ < λc by 50% at λ = λc (i.e., at the cut-off wavelength, the transmission is exactly 50% by amplitude). **Step 2: Derivation from the 50% Criterion** At λ = λc, we require G(λc) = 0.5 (in amplitude): $$ 0.5 = \exp\left[-\pi \cdot 1^2 \cdot C\right] \implies C = \frac{\ln 2}{\pi} $$ where **C** is the constant in the filter expression. **Step 3: Generalization** $$ G(\lambda) = \exp\left[-\frac{\pi \ln 2}{\lambda_c^2} \lambda^2 \cdot \text{(correction for spatial vs frequency domain)}\right] $$ The final standard form (in the spatial frequency domain, where **f = 1/λ**) is: $$ G(f) = \exp\left[-\pi \ln 2 \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2\right] = \exp\left[-0.693 \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2\right] $$ **Step 4: Why Gaussian?** Unlike the 2RC filter, the Gaussian filter has **zero phase shift**, meaning peak positions are preserved. This makes the **mean line computation more robust** and is why ISO 16610 adopted it. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Ra Calculation **Problem:** A stylus profilometer yields the following height readings (in µm) at 10 points over a 0.8 mm sampling length on an aluminum surface: `z = [+0.4, -0.6, +0.2, -0.3, +0.5, -0.2, +0.7, -0.4, +0.3, -0.6]` **Step 1: Verify Mean Line (Should be ≈ 0)** $$ \bar{z} = \frac{1}{10}(0.4 - 0.6 + 0.2 - 0.3 + 0.5 - 0.2 + 0.7 - 0.4 + 0.3 - 0.6) = 0 $$ **Step 2: Sum of Absolute Values** $$ \sum \vert z_i \vert = 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.7 + 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.6 = 4.2 $$ **Step 3: Compute Ra** $$ R_a = \frac{4.2}{10} = 0.42 \text{ µm} $$ **Step 4: Compute Rq (RMS)** $$ R_q = \sqrt{\frac{0.16 + 0.36 + 0.04 + 0.09 + 0.25 + 0.04 + 0.49 + 0.16 + 0.09 + 0.36}{10}} = \sqrt{0.204} = 0.452 \text{ µm} $$ **Step 5: Verification (Gaussian Check)** $$ \frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{0.452}{0.42} = 1.076 $$ Since the expected ratio for Gaussian surface is **1.25**, our surface deviates slightly from Gaussian (skewed/periodic features). This is diagnostically useful. ### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Stylus vs Optical vs AFM | Feature | Stylus (Talysurf) | Optical (WLI / Confocal) | AFM | |---------|-------------------|--------------------------|-----| | Contact / Non-contact | Contact | Non-contact | Contact (or tapping) | | Tip Size | 2–10 µm | Diffraction-limited (~0.5 µm) | 5–20 nm | | Vertical Resolution | 1–10 nm | 0.1–1 nm | 0.01–0.05 nm | | Lateral Resolution | 0.2–1 µm | 0.2–0.5 µm | 0.1–5 nm | | Damage Risk | Possible on soft surfaces | None | Possible in contact mode | | Sample Prep | Minimal | Minimal to none | Requires flat, clean sample | | Speed | Slow (mm/s) | Fast (video rate possible) | Slow (µm/s) | | Maximum Scan Area | 100 mm | 100 mm | 100 µm | | Best Application | Engineering surfaces, bearings | Optical components, MEMS | Nano-structures, thin films | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Stylus Profilometer (Talysurf) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Workpiece on X-Y Stage] -->|Contact| B[Diamond Stylus
tip radius 2 to 10 um] B -->|Vertical displacement z| C[Transducer
LVDT or Piezo] C -->|Analog signal| D[Amplifier and Filter] D -->|Conditioned signal| E[ADC Converter] E -->|Digital profile data| F[Microprocessor] F -->|Ra, Rz, Rq values| G[Display and Printer] F -->|2D profile trace| H[Profile Plotter] I[Reference Datum Block] --> A ``` ### 4.2 Working Principle of White-Light Interferometer ```mermaid flowchart TB A[White Light Source] -->|Broadband beam| B[Beam Splitter] B -->|Reference beam| C[Reference Mirror
on PZT scanner] B -->|Measurement beam| D[Sample Surface] C -->|Reflected| B D -->|Reflected| B B -->|Combined beams| E[CCD Camera Array] E -->|Fringe contrast data| F[Vertical Scan Controller
PZT moves reference mirror] F -->|Height z at peak contrast| G[3D Topography Map] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram of an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AFM Tip
5 to 20 nm radius] -->|Tip-Sample Force| B[Microcantilever
Si or Si3N4] B -->|Deflection| C[Laser Diode] C -->|Reflected beam| D[Position Sensitive Detector PSD] D -->|Photodiode signal| E[Differential Amplifier] E -->|Error signal| F[Feedback Controller] F -->|Z correction| G[Piezo Scanner Z-axis] F -->|Height data z x y| H[Computer Display 3D Image] I[Sample on Piezo XY stage] -->|Raster scan| A G -->|Maintains constant force| A ``` ### 4.4 Surface Texture Decomposition — Form, Waviness, Roughness ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Measured Profile z_measured x] --> B[Form Removal
Least squares fit] A --> C[Waviness Filter
Low pass at lambda c] A --> D[Roughness Filter
High pass at lambda c] B --> E[Form Component z_form] C --> F[Waviness Component z_wav] D --> G[Roughness Component z_rough] E --> H[Final z = z_rough + z_wav + z_form] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Ra, Rq, Rz Computation] ``` ### 4.5 Stylus Tip — Geometric Convolution Effect ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Actual V-Groove
Depth h_true] --> B[Stylus tip radius r] B --> C[Contact point offset
Delta h = r times 1 over sin alpha minus 1] C --> D[Measured Depth h_measured
less than h_true] D --> E[Underestimation Error
for sharp valleys] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *Define the term "Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra)" of a surface. State the standard sampling length for a surface with Ra value between 0.1 µm and 2.0 µm.* **Model Answer:** The **Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra)**, also called the **Center Line Average (CLA)**, is defined as the **arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the ordinates of the profile** (z) measured from the mean line, over a specified sampling length (l). Mathematically: $$ R_a = \frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} \vert z(x) \vert\, dx $$ For a surface whose Ra value lies in the range **0.1 µm to 2.0 µm**, the standard sampling length (as per ISO 4288) is **l = 0.8 mm**, and the corresponding evaluation length is **L = 5 × l = 4.0 mm**. The cut-off wavelength λc for the roughness filter in this range is also **0.8 mm**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Point:** Ra is the most universally quoted surface finish parameter because it gives a stable average value insensitive to localized scratches or anomalies. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *What is the tip-convolution effect in stylus profilometry? How does it affect the measurement of sharp valleys?* **Model Answer:** The **tip-convolution effect** arises because the diamond stylus in a profilometer is not a point but a **sphere of finite radius (typically 2 µm, 5 µm, or 10 µm)**. When the tip encounters a **sharp valley narrower than 4 × r**, the spherical tip bridges the valley and cannot reach its true bottom. **Effect on measurement of sharp valleys:** - The measured valley depth is always **less than the true depth**. - The error is quantified by: $\Delta h = r\left(\frac{1}{\sin\alpha} - 1\right)$, where **α** is the half-angle of the valley. - For a 90° V-groove with r = 2 µm, the under-reporting is ~ 0.83 µm, which can be 80% or more of the true depth. - Sharper valleys (small α) produce **larger errors**. **Remedies:** 1. Use a smaller tip radius (down to 0.5 µm — special order). 2. Use a non-contact method (AFM or WLI) for sub-micron features. 3. Apply **tip-deconvolution algorithms** computationally. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write that "the stylus scratches the surface" — that is **not** tip-convolution. Tip-convolution is a **purely geometric/shadowing** effect, not a damage effect. --- ### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **working principle of a stylus profilometer (Talysurf)**. List the key components and state the function of each. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the relationship between **Ra, Rq, and the standard deviation σ** of heights for a **Gaussian (normally distributed) surface**. Show that **Rq ≈ 1.25 × Ra** for such surfaces. (7 marks) **Model Solution for (a):** A Talysurf is a high-precision 2-D contact profilometer for measuring surface roughness. The block diagram (refer Section 4.1) consists of the following components: | # | Component | Function | |---|-----------|----------| | 1 | **Workpiece holder / X-Y stage** | Holds the specimen; provides precise lateral positioning with micrometer accuracy | | 2 | **Diamond Stylus** (tip radius 2 µm) | Conforms to the surface micro-contour; converts geometric features into vertical motion | | 3 | **Pickup (Transducer)** — LVDT / Piezo / Capacitive | Converts vertical stylus motion into an electrical signal (mV/µm sensitivity) | | 4 | **Amplifier and Filter Unit** | Conditions the signal; applies Gaussian or 2RC filter for form/waviness removal | | 5 | **ADC and Microprocessor** | Digitizes the analog signal; computes Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt, etc. | | 6 | **Display / Recorder** | Shows numerical parameters and 2-D profile trace | | 7 | **Datum Reference Block** | Provides a calibrated reference for the mean line | **Working Principle:** The stylus is drawn across the surface at a slow traverse speed (typically 0.5 mm/s). As it follows the micro-contour, the vertical displacement is sensed by the LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer). The signal is amplified, filtered to remove form and waviness, and digitized. The microprocessor computes the standardized roughness parameters from the digital profile. The 2-D trace is plotted on a chart recorder or displayed on a monitor. **Model Solution for (b):** For a surface with **normally distributed (Gaussian) heights** $z(x)$ with mean $\bar{z} = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the probability density function is: $$ p(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) $$ **Step 1: Definition of Rq** By definition: $$ R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} z^2(x)\, dx} = \sigma $$ So for a Gaussian surface, **Rq = σ** (the standard deviation). **Step 2: Definition of Ra (using expectation)** $$ R_a = \frac{1}{l}\int_0^{l} \vert z(x) \vert\, dx = E[\vert z \vert] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert z \vert\, p(z)\, dz $$ **Step 3: Evaluate the Integral** Using the symmetry of the Gaussian distribution: $$ E[\vert z \vert] = 2\int_0^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dz $$ Substitute $u = z^2 / (2\sigma^2) \Rightarrow du = z\, dz / \sigma^2$: $$ E[\vert z \vert] = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} \sigma^2 e^{-u}\, du = \frac{2\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot 1 = \sigma\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} $$ **Step 4: Numerical Coefficient** $$ \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.6366} = 0.7979 $$ Therefore: $R_a = 0.7979 \sigma \approx 0.798 \sigma$ **Step 5: Derive the Rq/Ra Ratio** $$ \frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{\sigma}{0.798\sigma} = 1.253 $$ Hence, for a Gaussian surface, $R_q \approx 1.25 \times R_a$, **as required**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse Ra and Rq. Remember: **Ra = mean of absolute values; Rq = root mean square**. Also, the **1.25 ratio is valid only for Gaussian surfaces** — for periodic (e.g., turned) surfaces, the ratio can be as high as 1.6 or as low as 1.05. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **principle of Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **contact, tapping, and non-contact modes**. (7 marks) **(b)** A surface profile of length 4 mm yields the following 8 sampled heights (in µm) at 0.5 mm intervals after form removal: $z = [+1.0, -0.5, +1.5, -1.0, +0.5, -0.5, +1.0, -0.5]$. Compute **Ra, Rq, and Rt**. (7 marks) **Model Solution for (a):** **AFM Working Principle (refer Section 4.3 block diagram):** A sharp tip (radius 5–20 nm) mounted on a flexible micro-cantilever is scanned across the sample surface using a piezoelectric XYZ scanner. As the tip interacts with the surface, interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, repulsive Pauli) cause the cantilever to **deflect** by sub-nanometer amounts. A **laser beam** focused on the back of the cantilever reflects into a **position-sensitive detector (PSD)**, producing a voltage proportional to the deflection. A **feedback loop** adjusts the Z-piezo to maintain a **constant tip-sample force** (or constant amplitude in tapping mode), and the Z-piezo voltage is recorded as a function of (x, y) position → this yields the **3-D topography**. **Comparison of AFM Modes:** | Feature | Contact Mode | Tapping Mode | Non-Contact Mode | |---------|--------------|--------------|-------------------| | Tip-Sample Force | Repulsive (1–100 nN) | Intermittent (oscillating) | Attractive (sub-nN) | | Tip Oscillation | None | Near resonance (50–500 kHz) | Above resonance | | Feedback Signal | Deflection | Amplitude | Frequency / Amplitude | | Lateral Resolution | Highest (atomic) | High (~ 1 nm) | Lower (~ 5 nm) | | Sample Damage | High risk | Minimal | None | | Best for | Hard, rigid samples | Soft / loosely bound samples | Ultra-clean, atomically flat surfaces | | Imaging Medium | Air or liquid | Air or liquid | Ultra-high vacuum or controlled | > [!NOTE] > **Tapping mode** is the **default in most modern AFMs** for biological and polymer samples because it eliminates lateral (shear) forces that would otherwise drag the tip across the sample. **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $z = [+1.0, -0.5, +1.5, -1.0, +0.5, -0.5, +1.0, -0.5]$ µm; N = 8. **Step 1: Verify Mean Line** $$ \bar{z} = \frac{1.0 - 0.5 + 1.5 - 1.0 + 0.5 - 0.5 + 1.0 - 0.5}{8} = \frac{1.5}{8} = 0.1875 $$ The mean is **not zero** — this is because the data is "as measured" (form removed by software, but the residual mean may be nonzero). However, for Ra and Rq, the mean is **always subtracted** before computing. Let's recompute by shifting: $z'_i = z_i - 0.1875$: `[+0.8125, -0.6875, +1.3125, -1.1875, +0.3125, -0.6875, +0.8125, -0.6875]` Sum of shifts = 1.5 - 1.5 = 0 ✓ **Step 2: Compute Ra** $$ R_a = \frac{1}{8}\left(0.8125 + 0.6875 + 1.3125 + 1.1875 + 0.3125 + 0.6875 + 0.8125 + 0.6875\right) $$ $$ R_a = \frac{6.5}{8} = 0.8125 \text{ µm} $$ **Step 3: Compute Rq** $$ R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{8}\left(0.6602 + 0.4727 + 1.7227 + 1.4102 + 0.0977 + 0.4727 + 0.6602 + 0.4727\right)} $$ $$ R_q = \sqrt{\frac{5.9691}{8}} = \sqrt{0.7461} = 0.8638 \text{ µm} $$ **Step 4: Compute Rt** Maximum positive value: **+1.3125 µm** (after mean correction) Maximum negative value: **-1.1875 µm** (after mean correction) $$ R_t = 1.3125 - (-1.1875) = 2.5 \text{ µm} $$ **Step 5: Verify Gaussian Check** $$ \frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{0.8638}{0.8125} = 1.063 $$ This ratio is **much less than 1.25**, indicating the surface is **not Gaussian** — it has a strong periodic (machined/turned) character, which is consistent with the alternating sign pattern. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to **subtract the mean** before computing Ra and Rq. This is a 1-mark deduction each. Also, ensure the **sampling length l** is correctly identified (here l = 4 mm spans all 8 points). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in Surface Profiling questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Ra with Rq:** Ra is the *arithmetic mean of absolute values*; Rq is the *root mean square*. They are equal **only for specific distributions** — generally, Rq > Ra. **(Loss: 1–2 marks)** > > 2. **Forgetting the L = 5l rule:** The evaluation length is **5 times** the sampling length. Stating l = 4.8 mm instead of L = 4.8 mm is a common error. **(Loss: 1 mark)** > > 3. **Wrong filter:** The 2RC filter is *obsolete* per ISO 16610; current standard is the **Gaussian filter**. Examiners expect "Gaussian filter" in current-scheme answers. **(Loss: 1 mark)** > > 4. **Tip-convolution formula sign error:** $\Delta h = r\left(\frac{1}{\sin\alpha} - 1\right)$ is **positive** (under-reporting). Writing it negative is a 1-mark error. > > 5. **Units mismatch:** Ra in nm vs µm vs mm is a frequent slip. Always quote units explicitly. > > 6. **AFM mode confusion:** "Tapping mode" is *not* "contact mode with vibration" — it operates in the *attractive regime* with intermittent tip-sample contact at each oscillation cycle. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Surface Profiling Techniques** - [x] **Stylus profilometer (Talysurf)** is the standard 2-D contact method; tip radius typically **2 µm**; transducer usually **LVDT**. - [x] **Optical (WLI)** uses white-light **coherence length** (~1 µm) for vertical localization; produces 3-D areal maps. - [x] **AFM** uses cantilever with tip radius **5–20 nm**; works via **interatomic forces**; modes are contact, tapping, non-contact. - [x] **STM** uses **tunneling current** between a conductive tip and a conductive sample — requires conducting samples. - [x] **Ra** = mean absolute deviation; **Rq** = root mean square; for Gaussian surface, **Rq = 1.25 × Ra**. - [x] **Sampling length (l)** vs **Evaluation length (L = 5l)** — distinguish clearly. - [x] **λc** (cut-off wavelength) depends on Ra range; for 0.1 < Ra < 2 µm, **λc = 0.8 mm**. - [x] **Gaussian filter** is preferred over **2RC** filter (no phase shift; ISO 16610). - [x] **Tip-convolution** causes under-reporting of sharp valleys: $\Delta h = r\left(\frac{1}{\sin\alpha} - 1\right)$. - [x] **Skewness (Rsk)**: positive = peaks (good for bearing surfaces); negative = valleys (good for lubricated surfaces). - [x] **Kurtosis (Rku)** < 3 = bumpy; = 3 = Gaussian; > 3 = spiky (sharp peaks/valleys). - [x] ISO standards: **ISO 21920-1** (linear profile parameters) and **ISO 25178** (areal/3-D parameters). - [x] Lateral resolution order: **STM < AFM < Stylus < Optical** (best to worst for nano-scale). - [x] **Contact techniques** risk sample damage; **optical methods** are non-destructive; **AFM in tapping mode** is the best compromise for soft samples. - [x] For a 2 µm tip radius, a 90° V-groove of true depth 1 µm is measured as **0.17 µm** (83% error) — always quote tip radius in any stylus analysis.

topics.title: Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Techniques: Ultrasonic Testing, Radiographic Testing, Magnetic Particle Testing
Material Behavior and Failure Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Material Behavior and Failure Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Behavior** refers to the response of engineering materials to applied loads, encompassing **elastic deformation**, **plastic deformation**, **viscoelastic flow**, and eventual **fracture**. **Failure Analysis** is the systematic investigation of the causes, mechanisms, and progression of material degradation to prevent recurrence in service.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic forms the analytical backbone of **Module 4** in **OEMET721 – Engineering Materials**, bridging **microstructure-property relationships** with **macroscopic service performance**.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Material Response

| Pillar | Definition | Governing Equation (Conceptual) |
|---|---|---|
| **Elasticity** | Reversible, time-independent deformation | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ |
| **Plasticity** | Permanent, irreversible deformation | Governed by **yield criteria** (von Mises, Tresca) |
| **Fracture** | Complete loss of load-bearing capacity | **Griffith-Orowan** energy balance |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Rubber Band (Elastic Behavior):**
> Imagine stretching a rubber band. When released, it returns to its original length. This is **elastic deformation** — governed by **Hooke's Law**. The rubber band "remembers" its shape because atomic bonds stretch but do **not** break.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Copper Wire (Plastic Behavior):**
> Take a soft copper wire and bend it repeatedly. It stays bent. This is **plastic deformation** — atoms have slipped along crystallographic planes (**dislocation motion**) and **cannot return** to their original positions. The wire has a "new memory."

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Chalk Snap (Brittle Fracture):**
> Snap a piece of chalk — it breaks cleanly with no warning deformation. This is **brittle fracture**, characteristic of ceramics and cast iron at low temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 4 — The Paperclip Fatigue:**
> Bend a paperclip back and forth 10 times — it suddenly snaps. This is **fatigue failure**, where cyclic loading below the yield strength eventually causes crack initiation and propagation. About **80% of all engineering failures** are fatigue-related.

### 1.4 Fundamental Metrics (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary)

- **Engineering Stress ($\sigma$)** = Load / Original Cross-sectional Area
- **Engineering Strain ($\varepsilon$)** = Change in Length / Original Length
- **True Stress ($\sigma_t$)** = Load / Instantaneous Cross-sectional Area
- **True Strain ($\varepsilon_t$)** = Integral of incremental length change
- **Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)** in **GPa** — stiffness indicator
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** — dimensionless, typically **0.3** for metals
- **Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$)** in **MPa** — fatigue threshold for steels
- **Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$)** in **MPa$\sqrt{m}$** — crack resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stress-Strain Curve — A Complete Walkthrough

The **engineering stress-strain diagram** is the single most important graph in mechanical metallurgy. Every KTU examiner expects a labeled diagram.

**Region 1 — Elastic Region (Linear)**
- Stress is proportional to strain via **Young's Modulus ($E$)**.
- Atomic bonds stretch elastically; full recovery on unloading.
- Slope = $E$ (steepness indicates stiffness).

**Region 2 — Proportional Limit**
- End of linear elastic behavior. Beyond this point, the curve deviates from Hooke's Law.

**Region 3 — Elastic Limit**
- Maximum stress the material can withstand **without permanent deformation**.
- Often difficult to distinguish from the proportional limit in practice.

**Region 4 — Yielding**
- **Upper Yield Point ($\sigma_{uy}$)**: stress at which plastic deformation begins under decreasing load.
- **Lower Yield Point ($\sigma_{ly}$)**: nearly constant stress during continued yielding (notable in mild steel).
- **0.2% Offset Yield Strength ($\sigma_{0.2}$)**: stress producing 0.2% permanent strain (used for non-ferrous metals).

**Region 5 — Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)**
- Beyond yielding, additional stress is required for further plastic deformation.
- Mechanism: dislocation multiplication and entanglement.

**Region 6 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_{uts}$)**
- Maximum engineering stress the material can sustain.
- Necking begins **after** this point.

**Region 7 — Necking and Fracture**
- Localized cross-sectional reduction → stress concentration → void coalescence → fracture.
- Ductile materials show cup-and-cone fracture surfaces.

### 2.2 Failure Modes — The Master Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard Classification of Failure:**
> 1. **Ductile Fracture** — significant plastic deformation precedes fracture
> 2. **Brittle Fracture** — minimal plastic deformation, sudden failure
> 3. **Fatigue Failure** — progressive damage under cyclic loading
> 4. **Creep Failure** — time-dependent deformation at elevated temperatures
> 5. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — synergistic action of stress and corrosive environment

### 2.3 Ductile vs. Brittle — The Critical Comparison

| Property | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Plastic Deformation** | Extensive | Negligible |
| **Fracture Surface** | Dull, fibrous (dimples) | Bright, crystalline (cleavage) |
| **Energy Absorbed** | High | Low |
| **Warning Before Failure** | Yes (necking) | No (sudden) |
| **Examples** | Mild steel, copper, aluminum | Cast iron, glass, ceramics |
| **Temperature Behavior** | Becomes brittle below Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT) | — |

### 2.4 Fracture Mechanics — The Griffith-Orowan Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a crack propagate?** Because the **release of elastic strain energy** when the crack extends exceeds the **energy required to create new crack surfaces**.

The critical condition for crack propagation:

$$G \geq 2\gamma_s + \gamma_p$$

Where:
- $G$ = elastic strain energy release rate (J/m²)
- $\gamma_s$ = surface energy per unit area
- $\gamma_p$ = plastic work per unit area

**Orowan's modification** accounts for plasticity (important for metals):

$$G_c = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$$

Where $G_c$ is the **critical strain energy release rate**.

The relationship to **stress intensity factor**:

$$K_{IC} = \sqrt{G_c \cdot E}$$

### 2.5 Fatigue Analysis — Basquin's Law and S-N Curves

For **high-cycle fatigue** (number of cycles $N > 10^3$), the **Basquin Equation** governs:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$

Where:
- $\sigma_a$ = stress amplitude (MPa)
- $\sigma'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ true fracture strength)
- $N_f$ = cycles to failure
- $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels)

For **low-cycle fatigue** ($N < 10^3$), the **Coffin-Manson Relation** applies:

$$\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$$

Where $\Delta \varepsilon_p$ is the plastic strain range and $c$ is the fatigue ductility exponent.

### 2.6 Creep — The Time-Dependent Menace

**Creep** is progressive deformation under sustained load at elevated temperatures (typically $T > 0.4 T_m$, where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin).

**Three Stages of Creep:**
1. **Primary Creep** — decelerating strain rate (work hardening dominates)
2. **Secondary Creep** — steady-state, minimum strain rate (balance between hardening and recovery)
3. **Tertiary Creep** — accelerating strain rate leading to rupture (microvoid coalescence)

The **Norton-Bailey Power Law** for steady-state creep:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \sigma^n \exp\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $\dot{\varepsilon}_s$ = steady-state creep rate (s⁻¹)
- $A$ = material constant
- $\sigma$ = applied stress (MPa)
- $n$ = stress exponent (typically 3–8)
- $Q_c$ = activation energy for creep (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (elastic region) | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_t = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$ | True stress from engineering stress | MPa |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$ | True strain from engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor (mode I) | MPa$\sqrt{m}$ |
| 5 | $K_{IC} = \sqrt{G_c E}$ | Fracture toughness from energy release rate | MPa$\sqrt{m}$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's high-cycle fatigue law | MPa |
| 7 | $\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | Coffin-Manson low-cycle fatigue law | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | Norton-Bailey steady-state creep law | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Poisson's ratio definition | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G_c = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$ | Griffith-Orowan fracture criterion | J/m² |
| 11 | $\sigma_{fat} = \sigma'_f - \log(N)$ | Empirical Goodman approximation | MPa |
| 12 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Elastic moduli relation | GPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **stress amplitude** with **stress range**. Remember:
> - **Amplitude** = half the range
> - **Range** = $\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$
> - **Mean** = $(\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between True and Engineering Stress

**Starting Point:** Consider a tensile specimen under load $F$ with:
- Original length = $L_0$
- Original cross-sectional area = $A_0$
- Instantaneous length = $L_i$
- Instantaneous cross-sectional area = $A_i$

**Step 1 — Definition of Engineering Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of True Stress**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i}$$

**Step 3 — Assumption of Constant Volume (Plastic Deformation)**

During plastic deformation, volume remains constant:

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A_i$**

$$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}$$

**Step 5 — Express Engineering Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Volume Conservation**

$$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_0(1 + \varepsilon)} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon}$$

**Step 7 — Derive True Stress in Terms of Engineering Stress**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1+\varepsilon)} = \frac{F}{A_0}(1+\varepsilon)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \sigma(1 + \varepsilon)}$$

**Step 8 — Derive True Strain**

True strain is defined as the integral of incremental strain:

$$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Crack Length from Fracture Mechanics

**Given:**
- Applied stress $\sigma$
- Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$
- Geometry factor $Y$ (for a center-cracked plate, $Y \approx 1$)

**Step 1 — Write the Stress Intensity Factor Expression**

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Failure Criterion**

Fracture occurs when $K_I = K_{IC}$:

$$K_{IC} = Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a_c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Critical Crack Length $a_c$**

$$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma_c}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{a_c = \frac{K_{IC}^2}{\pi Y^2 \sigma_c^2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Endurance Life Using Basquin's Law (Worked Example)

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A steel component is subjected to a fully reversed cyclic stress. The fatigue strength coefficient is $\sigma'_f = 1000$ MPa and the fatigue strength exponent is $b = -0.10$. Calculate the number of cycles to failure when the stress amplitude is $\sigma_a = 400$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Write Basquin's Equation**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**

$$400 = 1000 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.10}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the Bracketed Term**

$$(2N_f)^{-0.10} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4$$

**Step 4 — Take the Reciprocal and Raise Both Sides to Power $1/(-0.10)$**

$$2N_f = (0.4)^{1/(-0.10)} = (0.4)^{-10}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Right Side**

$$(0.4)^{-10} = \frac{1}{(0.4)^{10}} = \frac{1}{1.0486 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9536.74$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N_f$**

$$N_f = \frac{9536.74}{2} \approx 4768$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 4768 \text{ cycles}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Selection Guide

| Loading Condition | Temperature | Loading Rate | Likely Failure Mode | Key Material Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static, uniaxial | Low ($<0.4 T_m$) | Slow | Ductile or Brittle (based on material) | Yield strength, fracture toughness |
| Static, uniaxial | High ($>0.4 T_m$) | Slow | Creep rupture | Creep rate, rupture life |
| Cyclic (high $N$) | Low | Any | Fatigue (high-cycle) | Endurance limit |
| Cyclic (low $N$) | Low | Any | Fatigue (low-cycle, plastic) | Fatigue ductility coefficient |
| Impact | Low | Very fast | Brittle fracture (if below DBTT) | Impact energy (Charpy) |
| Sustained + corrosive | Any | Static | Stress Corrosion Cracking | $K_{ISCC}$ threshold |
| Multiaxial | Any | Any | Mixed-mode fracture | Combined toughness |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Strain Energy Release Rate in Terms of Stress Intensity

**Starting Point — Energy Approach:**

For a crack extending by $da$ in a plate of thickness $B$, under fixed grip conditions:

$$G = -\frac{1}{B}\frac{dU}{da} = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}$$

(For plane stress; for plane strain, replace $E$ with $E/(1-\nu^2)$.)

**Step 1 — Square the Stress Intensity Factor Expression**

$$K_I^2 = Y^2 \sigma^2 \pi a$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate $\sigma^2 a$**

$$\sigma^2 a = \frac{K_I^2}{\pi Y^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $G$ Expression (plane stress, $Y=1$)**

$$G = \frac{\pi}{E} \cdot \frac{K_I^2}{\pi} = \frac{K_I^2}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize (plane strain)**

$$G = \frac{K_I^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Condition**

At fracture, $G = G_c$ and $K_I = K_{IC}$:

$$\boxed{K_{IC}^2 = \frac{G_c E}{1 - \nu^2}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
