## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish clearly between **ductile fracture** and **brittle fracture** based on (i) plastic deformation, (ii) fracture surface appearance, and (iii) energy absorbed.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded as 1+1+1):**

| Criterion | Ductile Fracture | Brittle Fracture |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Plastic Deformation** | Extensive plastic deformation precedes fracture; visible necking occurs | Negligible plastic deformation; failure occurs abruptly |
| **(ii) Fracture Surface** | Dull, fibrous appearance with characteristic **cup-and-cone** morphology; dimples indicate microvoid coalescence | Bright, shiny, granular appearance with **cleavage facets**; often shows **river patterns** |
| **(iii) Energy Absorbed** | High fracture energy (large area under stress-strain curve) | Low fracture energy (small area under stress-strain curve) |

**Additional Point for Full Marks:** Ductile fracture is **preferred** in engineering design because it provides **warning** before catastrophic failure, allowing for inspection and replacement.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **creep** and name the **three stages of creep** with a brief description of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Creep is the **time-dependent permanent deformation** of materials subjected to a **constant load or stress** at **elevated temperatures**, typically when $T > 0.4 T_m$ (where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature).

**Three Stages (2 Marks):**

1. **Primary Creep (Transient Creep):** Strain rate **decreases** with time. Work hardening dominates over recovery. Dislocation density increases, impeding further motion.

2. **Secondary Creep (Steady-State Creep):** Strain rate is **constant** and at its **minimum value**. A dynamic balance exists between **work hardening** and **recovery (annealing)** processes. This stage dominates the service life of components.

3. **Tertiary Creep:** Strain rate **accelerates** rapidly, leading to **rupture**. Internal damage mechanisms dominate — microvoid formation, grain boundary separation, and necking.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Students must answer **ONE** full question from the choice provided. Each question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neatly labeled engineering stress-strain diagram**, explain the salient features including proportional limit, elastic limit, yield point (with 0.2% offset), ultimate tensile strength, necking, and fracture point. (**7 Marks**)

**(b)** A mild steel specimen of diameter **12 mm** and gauge length **50 mm** was tested in tension. The maximum load recorded was **45 kN**, and the load at fracture was **32 kN**. The specimen's final diameter at the fracture was **8.5 mm** and final length was **58 mm**. Calculate: (i) Engineering stress at maximum load, (ii) True stress at fracture, (iii) Engineering strain at fracture, (iv) True strain at fracture. (**7 Marks**)

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — Stress-Strain Diagram (7 Marks)**

The engineering stress-strain diagram is plotted with **engineering stress ($\sigma$)** on the **Y-axis** (in MPa) and **engineering strain ($\varepsilon$)** on the **X-axis** (dimensionless, often in %).

**Salient Features (7 points × 1 mark each):**

1. **Proportional Limit ($\sigma_p$):** The maximum stress up to which stress remains **directly proportional** to strain (Hooke's Law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ valid). Beyond this point, the curve deviates from linearity.

2. **Elastic Limit:** The maximum stress the material can withstand **without any permanent deformation** upon unloading. Practically, this is very close to the proportional limit.

3. **Yield Point ($\sigma_y$):** The stress at which material begins to **deform plastically** without any increase in load (characteristic of mild steel). It has an **upper yield point** (sudden drop) and a **lower yield point** (continued plastic flow at constant stress).

4. **0.2% Offset Yield Strength ($\sigma_{0.2}$):** For materials **without a sharp yield point** (e.g., aluminum alloys), the stress corresponding to **0.2% permanent plastic strain** is taken as the yield strength. A line parallel to the elastic slope is offset by $\varepsilon = 0.002$.

5. **Strain Hardening Region:** Beyond yielding, additional deformation requires **increasing stress**. The curve rises because of **dislocation multiplication and entanglement**, which impede further slip.

6. **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$):** The **maximum engineering stress** the material can sustain. After this point, **necking** begins — the cross-section reduces locally.

7. **Necking and Fracture:** Localized reduction in cross-section creates a **triaxial stress state**. Continued loading produces **void nucleation, growth, and coalescence**, leading to **cup-and-cone fracture** (in ductile metals).

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Original diameter $d_0 = 12$ mm $\Rightarrow A_0 = \pi(12)^2/4 = 113.10$ mm²
- Original length $L_0 = 50$ mm
- Maximum load $F_{max} = 45$ kN $= 45000$ N
- Fracture load $F_f = 32$ kN $= 32000$ N
- Final diameter at fracture $d_f = 8.5$ mm $\Rightarrow A_f = \pi(8.5)^2/4 = 56.745$ mm²
- Final length $L_f = 58$ mm

**(i) Engineering Stress at Maximum Load (2 Marks):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{45000 \text{ N}}{113.10 \text{ mm}^2} = 397.88 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 397.9 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(ii) True Stress at Fracture (2 Marks):**

$$\sigma_t^f = \frac{F_f}{A_f} = \frac{32000 \text{ N}}{56.745 \text{ mm}^2} = 563.93 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t^f \approx 563.9 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iii) Engineering Strain at Fracture (1.5 Marks):**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{58 - 50}{50} = \frac{8}{50} = 0.16$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_f = 0.16 \text{ or } 16\%}$$

**(iv) True Strain at Fracture (1.5 Marks):**

$$\varepsilon_t^f = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_f) = \ln(1 + 0.16) = \ln(1.16) = 0.1484$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_t^f \approx 0.1484}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Griffith-Orowan theory of brittle fracture**. State the governing equation and discuss how Orowan's modification accounts for metallic materials. (**7 Marks**)

**(b)** A center-cracked plate of a steel alloy has a half-crack length $a = 5$ mm and is subjected to a tensile stress $\sigma = 350$ MPa. Given $Y = 1.0$, $K_{IC} = 55$ MPa$\sqrt{m}$, determine whether the plate will fracture. If not, calculate the **critical crack length** at which fracture would occur. (**7 Marks**)

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Griffith-Orowan Theory (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (2 Marks):**
In 1921, **A.A. Griffith** proposed that brittle fracture occurs due to the **propagation of pre-existing microcracks**. He recognized that the **theoretical cohesive strength** calculated from atomic bond energy is **100 to 1000 times higher** than the observed fracture strength. This discrepancy arises from the presence of microscopic flaws.

**Energy Balance (3 Marks):**
Griffith proposed that a crack will propagate when the **decrease in elastic strain energy** (as the crack grows) is greater than or equal to the **increase in surface energy** required to create new crack surfaces.

$$\frac{dU}{da} \leq \frac{dW_s}{da}$$

For a plate of thickness $B$ containing a crack of length $2a$:

$$\frac{dU}{da} = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E} \quad \text{(per unit thickness)}$$

$$\frac{dW_s}{da} = 4\gamma_s \quad \text{(two new surfaces)}$$

The critical condition becomes:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

**Orowan's Modification (2 Marks):**
For **metallic materials**, Griffith's theory severely underestimates the actual fracture strength because it ignores **plastic deformation** at the crack tip. In 1948, **E. Orowan** modified the theory by adding a term $\gamma_p$ for **plastic work** done at the crack tip:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}$$

Since $\gamma_p \gg \gamma_s$ (typically by 3–4 orders of magnitude) for metals, the energy requirement is much higher, and the **critical crack length** before fast fracture is correspondingly **larger**. This explains why metals are more resistant to brittle fracture than predicted by pure Griffith theory.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $a = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m
- $\sigma = 350$ MPa
- $Y = 1.0$
- $K_{IC} = 55$ MPa$\sqrt{m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Stress Intensity Factor (3 Marks):**

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

$$K_I = (1.0)(350)\sqrt{\pi \times 0.005}$$

$$K_I = 350 \times \sqrt{0.01571} = 350 \times 0.1253 = 43.86 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Fracture Toughness (2 Marks):**

$$K_I = 43.86 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m} < K_{IC} = 55 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$$

$$\therefore \text{The plate will NOT fracture.}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Critical Crack Length (2 Marks):**

Using $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c}$:

$$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$$

$$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{55}{1.0 \times 350}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{\pi}(0.1571)^2$$

$$a_c = \frac{0.02468}{\pi} = 7.857 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{a_c \approx 7.86 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The plate is safe in its current state. The crack must grow from 5 mm to approximately **7.86 mm** before fast fracture occurs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m before computing $\sqrt{\pi a}$. The **fracture toughness formula strictly requires SI units** (MPa, m, MPa$\sqrt{m}$).
>
> 2. **Confusing $K_I$ with $K_{IC}$:** $K_I$ is the **applied** stress intensity factor; $K_{IC}$ is the **material property** (critical value). Fracture occurs **only when** $K_I \geq K_{IC}$.
>
> 3. **Neglecting the Geometry Factor $Y$:** Always include $Y$ in calculations. For an edge crack, $Y \approx 1.12$; for a center crack, $Y = 1.0$. KTU often tests this explicitly.
>
> 4. **Basquin Equation Misuse:** Students forget that Basquin's law applies only to **elastic (high-cycle) fatigue**, while **Coffin-Manson** is for plastic (low-cycle) fatigue. Mixing them yields wrong answers.
>
> 5. **Engineering vs True Strain:** Engineering strain at fracture may appear larger than true strain numerically — always use $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$.
>
> 6. **Creep Formula Constants:** Students often forget that $T$ in the Norton-Bailey equation must be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius.
>
> 7. **0.2% Offset Construction:** For non-ferrous metals, draw a line **parallel to the elastic portion** but starting from $\varepsilon = 0.002$ on the X-axis. The intersection with the stress-strain curve gives $\sigma_{0.2}$.

---

### **Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 High-Density Summary — Print This Section Before Exam**

- [ ] **Stress-strain diagram** has 7 key points: proportional limit, elastic limit, yield point, UTS, necking, fracture.
- [ ] **0.2% offset method** is used for materials without a sharp yield point (e.g., Al alloys).
- [ ] **True stress** = $\sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ — accounts for actual area reduction.
- [ ] **True strain** = $\ln(1+\varepsilon)$ — additive for multi-stage deformation.
- [ ] **Ductile fracture** = microvoid coalescence, cup-and-cone surface, dimples.
- [ ] **Brittle fracture** = cleavage, river patterns, sudden failure, low energy.
- [ ] **DBTT** (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature) is critical for **bcc metals** like ferritic steels.
- [ ] **Griffith equation** = $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s/(\pi a)}$ — for ideally brittle materials.
- [ ] **Orowan modification** adds $\gamma_p$ (plastic work) for metals.
- [ ] **Fracture toughness** $K_{IC}$ is the critical stress intensity factor.
- [ ] **Critical crack length** = $K_{IC}^2 / (\pi Y^2 \sigma^2)$.
- [ ] **Fatigue** = $80\%$ of engineering failures; cyclic loading below yield.
- [ ] **Basquin's law** for high-cycle fatigue: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$.
- [ ] **Coffin-Manson** for low-cycle fatigue: $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$.
- [ ] **Endurance limit** $\sigma_e$ exists for steels but **NOT** for aluminum alloys.
- [ ] **Goodman and Soderberg** are mean-stress correction criteria for fatigue.
- [ ] **Creep** occurs when $T > 0.4 T_m$ (homologous temperature).
- [ ] **Three creep stages**: Primary (decelerating), Secondary (steady), Tertiary (accelerating).
- [ ] **Norton-Bailey law**: $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$.
- [ ] **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** requires **three conditions**: susceptible alloy, specific environment, sustained tensile stress.
- [ ] **Charpy and Izod impact tests** measure toughness as a function of temperature.
- [ ] **Hardness tests** (Brinell, Rockwell, Vickers) correlate approximately with tensile strength.
- [ ] **Poisson's ratio** for most metals is around **0.3**.
- [ ] **Modulus of resilience** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ — elastic energy stored per unit volume.
- [ ] **Modulus of toughness** is the total area under the stress-strain curve.

---

**End of Notes — Material Behavior and Failure Analysis (Module 4)**

topics.title: Fracture Mechanics and Failure Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fracture Mechanics and Failure Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fracture Mechanics?

**Fracture Mechanics** is the field of solid mechanics that deals with the study of the propagation of cracks in materials. It is used to predict the structural integrity of engineering components by evaluating the effect of cracks and flaws on the load-bearing capacity of materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Fracture Mechanics is the quantitative analysis of the conditions under which a crack in a stressed material will propagate, leading to complete structural failure. It evaluates the stress, crack geometry, and material property combinations that govern fracture.

## 1.2 What is Failure Analysis?

**Failure Analysis** is the systematic process of investigating engineering component or material failures to determine the physical root causes. It uses principles of fracture mechanics, metallurgy, and non-destructive testing to identify *why* and *how* a component failed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Fracture Mechanics tells us *WHEN* a material will fail; Failure Analysis tells us *WHY* it actually failed in service.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

**Analogy 1: The Paper Tear (Brittle Fracture)**
Imagine trying to tear a sheet of paper. If there is already a small notch at the edge, the paper tears cleanly and suddenly with a sharp sound. This represents **brittle fracture** — a small flaw grows rapidly with little plastic deformation.

**Analogy 2: The Copper Wire Stretch (Ductile Fracture)**
If you pull a soft copper wire, it stretches, narrows in the middle (necking), and finally separates with a visible thinning. This represents **ductile fracture** — significant plastic deformation precedes failure.

**Analogy 3: The Glass Cutter**
A glass cutter creates a small scratch (flaw) on the glass surface. When stress is applied, the crack grows from this pre-existing flaw. The deeper the scratch, the lower the stress needed to break the glass — a perfect illustration of **Griffith's crack theory**.

## 1.4 Classification of Fracture

| Fracture Type | Energy Absorbed | Plastic Deformation | Crack Speed | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ductile** | High | Extensive (Necking visible) | Slow, stable | Mild steel, copper, aluminium |
| **Brittle** | Low | Negligible | Fast, unstable | Glass, ceramics, cast iron |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU):** Fracture toughness **K_IC** is measured in **MPa·√m** (megapascal-square-root-meter) and is the critical material property used to quantify resistance to brittle fracture.

## 1.5 Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)

**DBTT** is the temperature below which a material (typically BCC metals like steel) changes from ductile to brittle behaviour. It is a critical design parameter for structures operating in cold environments (e.g., ships in Arctic waters, pipelines in Siberia).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Charpy V-notch impact test is the standard experimental method for determining DBTT. Materials with FCC crystal structure (e.g., aluminium, copper, austenitic stainless steel) do **not** exhibit a sharp DBTT.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Fracture — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Ductile Fracture

Ductile fracture occurs by a process of **extensive plastic deformation** prior to final separation. It is the preferred mode of failure in most engineering applications because it provides visual warning before catastrophic collapse.

**Stages of Ductile Fracture (in tension):**
1. **Neck Formation:** After ultimate tensile strength, a localized reduction in cross-sectional area begins.
2. **Formation of Microvoids:** Internal microvoids nucleate at inclusions or second-phase particles within the necked region.
3. **Void Coalescence:** Microvoids grow and merge to form an elliptical internal crack oriented perpendicular to the applied stress.
4. **Final Shear Fracture:** The remaining cross-section ruptures by shear at approximately **45°** to the tensile axis, forming a characteristic **cup-and-cone** fracture surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The percentage reduction in area (%RA) and the total elongation (%EL) in a tensile test are quantitative measures of ductility. Higher values mean greater safety margin in design.

### 2.1.2 Brittle Fracture

Brittle fracture occurs with **little or no plastic deformation** and is characterised by rapid crack propagation. It is highly dangerous because it offers **no warning**.

**Mechanism of Brittle Fracture:**
- Fracture occurs by **cleavage** along specific crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} planes in BCC iron).
- Crack propagation speed can approach **1/3 the velocity of sound** in the material.
- The fracture surface appears **granular and shiny** (no necking).

**Conditions Favouring Brittle Fracture:**
- Low temperature (below DBTT for BCC metals)
- High strain rate (impact loading)
- Triaxial state of stress (notch effects)
- Thick section size (plane strain condition)
- Presence of flaws or stress concentrators

## 2.2 Stress Concentration

When a material contains a **hole, notch, or crack**, the applied stress is no longer uniform. The stress lines are forced to crowd around the discontinuity, creating a localized peak stress.

**Stress Concentration Factor (K_t):**

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_0}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{max}$ is the maximum stress at the discontinuity
- $\sigma_0$ is the nominal applied stress far from the discontinuity

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A small hole in a steel plate under tension does not weaken the plate by its cross-sectional area alone. The local stress at the hole edge can be **2 to 3 times** the applied stress, drastically reducing the load-bearing capacity.

## 2.3 Griffith's Theory of Brittle Fracture

**A.A. Griffith (1921)** proposed that brittle fracture occurs when the reduction in **elastic strain energy** released by crack extension exceeds the **increase in surface energy** required to create new crack surfaces.

**Assumptions of Griffith's Theory:**
- Material contains pre-existing microcracks (flaws).
- Cracks propagate when energy release rate ≥ energy required for new surface.
- Applicable to perfectly elastic (brittle) materials like glass.
- Plane stress condition assumed.

**The Griffith Equation:**

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_c$ = critical stress for crack propagation
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $\gamma_s$ = specific surface energy (energy per unit area)
- $a$ = half-length of internal crack (or full length of edge crack)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The Griffith equation proves that **smaller cracks require higher stress** to propagate, and **larger cracks propagate at lower stress**. This is the foundation of damage-tolerant design philosophy.

## 2.4 Stress Intensity Factor and Fracture Toughness

For real engineering materials, the stress field near a crack tip is described by the **Stress Intensity Factor (K)**, which combines the effects of applied stress, crack length, and geometry.

**General Form of Stress Intensity Factor:**

$$K = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$$

where:
- $Y$ = dimensionless geometry factor (typically 1 to 2)
- $\sigma$ = applied stress
- $a$ = crack length

**Three Modes of Crack Loading:**
- **Mode I (Opening mode):** Tensile stress perpendicular to crack plane — *most common and most critical*
- **Mode II (Sliding mode):** In-plane shear stress
- **Mode III (Tearing mode):** Out-of-plane shear stress

**Fracture Toughness (K_IC):**

Fracture toughness is the **critical value of Mode I stress intensity factor** at which a crack becomes unstable and propagates catastrophically. The subscript "C" denotes **Critical**, and "I" denotes Mode I.

**Fracture Criterion:**

$$K_I \geq K_{IC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Fracture occurs}$$

**Critical Stress for Fracture:**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$$

**Critical Crack Length:**

$$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma} \right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** You must be able to rearrange the fracture toughness equation to solve for any of the three variables: $\sigma_c$, $K_{IC}$, or $a_c$. These are the most frequently asked computational problems in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Fatigue Failure

**Fatigue** is the failure of a material under **cyclic or fluctuating stresses** at a magnitude below the static tensile strength. It is responsible for approximately **80–90%** of all mechanical engineering failures.

**The Three Stages of Fatigue Failure:**
1. **Crack Initiation:** Forms at a surface or at a stress concentrator (typically Stage I — shear-mode growth along slip planes).
2. **Crack Propagation:** Crack grows perpendicular to tensile stress (Stage II). Beach marks or striations may be visible.
3. **Final Fracture:** Occurs when remaining ligament cannot support the load (sudden, often brittle).

**Basquin's Equation (High-Cycle Fatigue):**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2N_f)^b$$

where $\sigma_a$ is stress amplitude, $N_f$ is cycles to failure, $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ and $b$ are fatigue strength coefficients.

## 2.6 Creep Failure

**Creep** is the time-dependent permanent deformation of materials under sustained load at elevated temperatures (typically $T > 0.4 T_m$, where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin).

**Three Stages of Creep:**
- **Stage I (Primary):** Decreasing creep rate — strain hardening
- **Stage II (Secondary/Steady-State):** Minimum, constant creep rate — most design-relevant
- **Stage III (Tertiary):** Accelerating creep rate leading to rupture

**Steady-State Creep Rate (Power Law):**

$$\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

where $A$, $n$ are material constants, $Q$ is activation energy, $R$ is gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_t = \sigma_{max} \big/ \sigma_0$ | Stress concentration factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2 E \gamma_s \big/ (\pi a)}$ | Griffith critical stress (plane stress) | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p) \big/ (\pi a)}$ | Modified Griffith (with plastic work) | Pa |
| 4 | $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor (Mode I) | MPa·√m |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = K_{IC} \big/ (Y \sqrt{\pi a})$ | Critical fracture stress | MPa |
| 6 | $a_c = (1 \big/ \pi)(K_{IC} \big/ (Y \sigma))^2$ | Critical crack length | m |
| 7 | $K_{IC} = \sqrt{2 E (\gamma_s + \gamma_p) \big/ (1 - \nu^2)}$ | Fracture toughness (plane strain) | MPa·√m |
| 8 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's fatigue equation | MPa |
| 9 | $\Delta \epsilon_p \big/ 2 = \epsilon_f^{\prime} (2N_f)^c$ | Coffin-Manson (low-cycle fatigue) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q \big/ RT)$ | Steady-state creep rate | s$^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $B = (2 \big/ 3) \ln(n \big/ 2)$ | Notch sensitivity (Neuber) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by design engineers to:
> - Set safe inspection intervals (using $\sigma_c$ and $a_c$)
> - Establish working stress limits for cracked components
> - Predict service life of components under cyclic loading
> - Select materials for high-temperature service (boilers, jet engines, nuclear reactors)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Griffith Equation

This derivation establishes the fundamental relationship between applied stress, crack size, and the energy balance that governs brittle fracture.

### Step 1: System Setup and Initial Energy

Consider an infinitely wide plate of unit thickness containing a centre crack of total length **2a**. The plate is subjected to a uniform tensile stress **σ** far from the crack.

The total **strain energy stored per unit thickness** in an uncracked elastic body of volume $V$ is:

$$U_0 = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2E}$$

### Step 2: Strain Energy Released by Crack Formation

When the crack forms, the material in the vicinity of the crack surfaces relaxes. Griffith showed that the **released elastic strain energy** (per unit thickness) is equivalent to the strain energy originally stored in a circular region of diameter equal to the crack length.

**Released strain energy per unit thickness:**

$$U_{released} = \frac{\sigma^2 (\pi a^2)}{2E}$$

**Reasoning:** The relaxed region is modelled as a circle of radius $a$ (half the crack length). The volume of this circular zone is $\pi a^2 \cdot t$ (with $t = 1$ for unit thickness), and the energy density removed is $\sigma^2 / (2E)$.

### Step 3: Surface Energy Required

Creating a crack of total length $2a$ on each of its **two surfaces** produces a new surface area of $2 \cdot (2a) \cdot 1 = 4a$ (per unit thickness).

**Required surface energy per unit thickness:**

$$U_{surface} = 4a \gamma_s$$

where $\gamma_s$ is the specific surface energy (energy per unit area).

### Step 4: Total Energy of the Cracked System

$$U_{total} = U_0 - U_{released} + U_{surface}$$

$$U_{total} = U_0 - \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{2E} + 4a\gamma_s$$

### Step 5: Apply Energy Criterion for Fracture

According to Griffith, the crack will propagate when the **total energy decreases** with increasing crack length, i.e., when $\partial U_{total} / \partial a < 0$. The critical condition occurs when the derivative equals zero:

$$\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial a} = 0$$

### Step 6: Differentiate with Respect to Crack Length

$$\frac{dU_{total}}{da} = -\frac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{2E} + 4\gamma_s = 0$$

$$-\frac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 4\gamma_s = 0$$

### Step 7: Solve for the Critical Stress

$$\frac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E} = 4\gamma_s$$

$$\sigma_c^2 = \frac{4\gamma_s E}{\pi a}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Variation Check:** Some textbooks present Griffith's equation with a factor of 2 inside the square root instead of outside, depending on whether the crack length is taken as the half-length or full length. The most common engineering form (used in KTU textbooks) is the form with factor $2$ as the leading coefficient.

### Step 8: Modified Griffith Equation (Orowan Modification)

For **ductile materials**, significant plastic work $\gamma_p$ accompanies crack extension. Orowan modified the Griffith equation to account for this:

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}}$$

**Why this modification matters:**
- For glass: $\gamma_s \approx 1$ J/m², $\gamma_p \approx 0$ → original Griffith applies.
- For metals: $\gamma_p \approx 10^3$ to $10^6$ J/m², which is **many orders of magnitude larger** than $\gamma_s$.
- Therefore, $\gamma_s + \gamma_p \approx \gamma_p$ in metals, and the modified equation explains the higher fracture strength observed.

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Stress from Fracture Toughness

Starting from the definition of Stress Intensity Factor:

$$K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$$

At the point of **fracture instability**, $K = K_{IC}$ and $\sigma = \sigma_c$:

$$K_{IC} = Y\sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a_c}$$

**Solving for critical stress $\sigma_c$:**

$$Y\sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{IC}$$

$$\sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a_c} = \frac{K_{IC}}{Y}$$

$$\sigma_c = \frac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a_c}}$$

**Solving for critical crack length $a_c$:**

$$K_{IC}^2 = Y^2 \sigma_c^2 \pi a_c$$

$$a_c = \frac{K_{IC}^2}{\pi Y^2 \sigma_c^2}$$

$$\boxed{a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma_c}\right)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The squared relationship in the formula for $a_c$ means a **doubling** of stress reduces the critical crack length by a factor of **4**. This has critical implications for design safety factors.

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Type)

**Problem:** A steel plate with fracture toughness $K_{IC} = 55$ MPa·√m contains an edge crack of length $a = 5$ mm. The geometry factor is $Y = 1.1$. Determine the critical stress that will cause fracture.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $K_{IC} = 55$ MPa·√m
- $a = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $Y = 1.1$

Applying the critical stress formula:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{55}{1.1 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

Compute the denominator step by step:

$$\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 15.708 \times 10^{-3} = 0.01571$$

$$\sqrt{0.01571} = 0.1253 \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

$$1.1 \times 0.1253 = 0.1379$$

$$\sigma_c = \frac{55}{0.1379} = 398.8 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 399 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The applied stress is significantly lower than the yield strength of typical steel (~250–500 MPa), demonstrating that even moderate stresses can fracture flawed components.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mode I vs Mode II vs Mode III

| Feature | Mode I (Opening) | Mode II (Sliding) | Mode III (Tearing) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loading Type** | Tensile | In-plane shear | Out-of-plane shear |
| **Crack Movement** | Opens perpendicular to crack plane | Slides in-plane | Slips out-of-plane |
| **Stress Intensity** | $K_I$ | $K_{II}$ | $K_{III}$ |
| **Toughness** | $K_{IC}$ | $K_{IIC}$ | $K_{IIIC}$ |
| **Engineering Relevance** | Most common, most critical | Less common | Least common |
| **Typical Use** | Pressure vessels, bridges | Friction stir welds | Gear teeth, shafts |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Fracture Toughness" and state its units. How is it different from "Toughness"?**

**Model Answer:**

**Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$):** It is the property of a material which describes its ability to resist fracture in the presence of a crack. It is the critical value of the Mode I stress intensity factor at which a pre-existing crack propagates catastrophically.

**Units:** $K_{IC}$ is measured in **MPa·√m** (megapascal-square-root-meter) or equivalently **MN/m$^{3/2}$** in SI units.

**Difference from Toughness:**

| Property | Toughness | Fracture Toughness |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Total energy absorbed before fracture (area under stress-strain curve) | Critical stress intensity factor for cracked body |
| Requires Crack? | No | Yes (explicitly considers pre-existing flaws) |
| Units | Joules (J) or J/m³ | MPa·√m |
| Application | General material selection | Damage-tolerant design, defect assessment |

**(3 Marks: 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the term "Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)" and state the factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

**DBTT Definition:** The Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature is the temperature below which a material (especially BCC metals like ferritic steel) exhibits brittle fracture behaviour and above which it fractures in a ductile manner. It is determined experimentally by the **Charpy V-notch impact test** as the temperature at which a specified amount of energy (commonly 20 J) is absorbed.

**Factors Affecting DBTT:**

1. **Alloying Elements:** Phosphorus and silicon **raise** DBTT; nickel **lowers** DBTT (used in cryogenic steels).
2. **Grain Size:** Finer grain size **lowers** DBTT (Hall-Petch strengthening effect).
3. **Strain Rate:** Higher strain rate **raises** DBTT (impact loading promotes brittleness).
4. **Heat Treatment:** Tempering **lowers** DBTT; quenching may **raise** it.
5. **Crystal Structure:** FCC metals have **no sharp** DBTT; BCC metals have a **pronounced** DBTT.

**(3 Marks: 1 + 2 split into 5 factors)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Derive the **Griffith equation** for the critical stress required for brittle fracture, clearly stating the underlying assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Discuss the **classification of fractures** with neat sketches of the characteristic fracture surfaces. Compare **ductile and brittle fracture** in terms of deformation, energy, and crack speed. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Griffith Equation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions (1.5 Marks):**
1. Material contains pre-existing elliptical microcracks of length $2a$.
2. The material is perfectly elastic and isotropic.
3. Loading is uniaxial tension, and plane stress condition exists.
4. The energy released from the stress field must equal the surface energy of newly created crack faces.

**Derivation Steps (5.5 Marks):**

Consider an infinite plate of unit thickness with a central crack of length $2a$, loaded by uniform stress $\sigma$ far from the crack.

**Step 1:** Elastic strain energy released per unit thickness upon crack formation:

$$U_r = \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{2E}$$

**Step 2:** Surface energy required to create two new crack faces (length $2a$ each):

$$U_s = 4a \gamma_s$$

**Step 3:** Total energy of the system:

$$U_T = -\frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{2E} + 4a \gamma_s$$

(Constant $U_0$ ignored as it does not depend on $a$.)

**Step 4:** Apply equilibrium condition for critical crack:

$$\frac{dU_T}{da} = -\frac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 4\gamma_s = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $\sigma_c$:

$$\pi a \sigma_c^2 = 4E \gamma_s$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{4E\gamma_s}{\pi a}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}}$$

This is the **Griffith Equation** for brittle fracture.

**Discussion (Additional marks):** The inverse square-root dependence on $a$ implies that as crack length increases, the critical stress drops sharply. For ductile materials, the equation is modified to:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}$$

where $\gamma_p$ is the plastic work per unit area of fracture surface, which dominates the energy term in metals.

---

#### Part (b) — Classification of Fractures (7 Marks)

**Classification (2 Marks):**

Fractures are classified into two principal categories:

**1. Ductile Fracture:** Occurs after considerable plastic deformation. The material yields and necks before separation.

**2. Brittle Fracture:** Occurs with negligible plastic deformation. Failure is sudden and catastrophic.

**Characteristic Fracture Surfaces (2 Marks):**

| Type | Description | Sketch |
|---|---|---|
| **Ductile (Cup-and-Cone)** | Central flat fibrous region (from microvoid coalescence) surrounded by an outer shear lip at ~45° | Two matching halves: a "cup" and a "cone" |
| **Brittle** | Flat, granular, and shiny surface perpendicular to the tensile axis. Often shows chevron marks pointing toward the crack origin | Single flat plane with river patterns |

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Property | Ductile Fracture | Brittle Fracture |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic Deformation | Extensive (visible necking) | Negligible |
| Energy Absorbed | High | Low |
| Crack Speed | Slow, stable propagation | Very fast (~1500 m/s in steel) |
| Warning | Yes (deformation visible) | No (sudden) |
| Fracture Surface | Dimpled, fibrous, matte | Granular, shiny, crystalline |
| Typical Temperature | Above DBTT | Below DBTT |
| Typical Strain Rate | Low | High (impact) |
| Examples | Mild steel, copper, aluminium | Glass, ceramics, cast iron |
| Failure Mode | Safe, predictable | Dangerous, catastrophic |

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** Define **Stress Intensity Factor (K)**. Explain the **three modes of crack loading** with neat diagrams. Derive the relationship between **fracture toughness, applied stress, and critical crack length**. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the **mechanism of fatigue failure** in metals. Describe the **S-N curve** and the **endurance limit** concept. List the four main factors that affect fatigue life. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Stress Intensity Factor and Fracture Toughness (7 Marks)

**Definition of Stress Intensity Factor (1 Mark):**

The Stress Intensity Factor (K) is a parameter that describes the magnitude of the stress field at the tip of a crack. It is a function of the applied stress, crack length, and the geometry of the component. Mathematically:

$$K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Three Modes of Crack Loading (3 Marks):**

- **Mode I — Opening Mode:** The crack surfaces move directly apart, perpendicular to the crack plane. This is the **most common and most critical** mode, caused by tensile stress. (Figure: Two arrows pulling the crack faces apart)

- **Mode II — Sliding Mode:** The crack surfaces slide over each other in a direction perpendicular to the leading edge of the crack (in-plane shear). (Figure: Two arrows pointing in opposite directions parallel to the crack plane)

- **Mode III — Tearing Mode:** The crack surfaces move relative to each other parallel to the leading edge of the crack (out-of-plane shear, anti-plane strain). (Figure: Two arrows pointing in opposite directions perpendicular to the crack plane, but anti-parallel)

**Derivation of Fracture Criterion (3 Marks):**

The condition for fracture is when the stress intensity factor $K$ reaches a critical value $K_{IC}$ (fracture toughness):

$$K_I = K_{IC}$$

Substituting the definition of $K$:

$$Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{IC}$$

**Solving for critical stress:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \frac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a_c}}}$$

**Solving for critical crack length:**

$$K_{IC}^2 = Y^2 \sigma^2 \pi a_c$$

$$\boxed{a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2}$$

**Significance:** The squared relationship in $a_c$ means that doubling the applied stress reduces the critical crack length to one-fourth. This forms the basis of **damage-tolerant design** and inspection scheduling.

---

#### Part (b) — Mechanism of Fatigue Failure (7 Marks)

**Mechanism of Fatigue (3 Marks):**

Fatigue failure occurs in three distinct stages:

1. **Crack Initiation:** A small crack nucleates at a free surface, typically at a stress concentrator such as a machining scratch, inclusion, or grain boundary. The crack forms along a slip band (Stage I, shear-mode growth).

2. **Crack Propagation:** Once initiated, the crack advances perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress. Each loading cycle produces a small increment of growth, often leaving visible **beach marks** or **striations** on the fracture surface (Stage II).

3. **Final Fracture:** When the crack reaches a critical size, the remaining cross-section can no longer support the load, and sudden, often brittle, fracture occurs.

**S-N Curve and Endurance Limit (2.5 Marks):**

The S-N curve (Wöhler curve) plots **stress amplitude (S)** on the y-axis against **number of cycles to failure (N)** on a logarithmic x-axis. For **ferrous metals** (e.g., steel), the curve becomes horizontal beyond a certain number of cycles (~10⁶ to 10⁷), defining the **endurance limit (σ_e)** or **fatigue limit** — the stress below which the material can theoretically endure an infinite number of cycles without failing.

For **non-ferrous metals** (e.g., aluminium, copper), the S-N curve never becomes horizontal; instead, a **fatigue strength** is defined at a specific number of cycles (commonly $10^8$).

**Factors Affecting Fatigue Life (1.5 Marks):**

1. **Mean Stress:** Higher mean tensile stress reduces fatigue life.
2. **Surface Quality:** Polished surfaces give higher fatigue life; rough surfaces, decarburization, or scratches reduce it.
3. **Stress Concentrations:** Notches, holes, and keyways drastically reduce fatigue life.
4. **Environment:** Corrosive media, high temperature, and residual stresses all alter fatigue behaviour.
5. **Grain Size and Microstructure:** Finer grains generally improve fatigue resistance.
6. **Loading Frequency and Waveform:** Higher frequencies and irregular waveforms affect crack growth rates.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert crack length to **metres (m)** before substituting into $\sqrt{\pi a}$ — a frequent source of mark deduction. If $K_{IC}$ is in MPa·√m, the resulting $\sigma_c$ will be in MPa directly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Geometry Factor Y:** Many students incorrectly set $Y = 1$ in numerical problems. For an edge crack in a finite plate, $Y \approx 1.12$ is typical; for a central crack in an infinite plate, $Y = 1$. Read the problem carefully.
> 3. **Mixing Up Plane Stress vs Plane Strain:** Use Griffith's original equation for **plane stress** (thin plates). For thick plates, the factor $1/(1 - \nu^2)$ must be included in the modified Griffith equation.
> 4. **Confusing Toughness with Fracture Toughness:** Toughness is **energy**; fracture toughness is a **stress intensity**. Do not interchange these in definitions.
> 5. **Missing Internal Choice Declaration:** In KTU 2024, Part B Module 4 offers a choice between two full questions. Always select the one you can answer most completely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4: Fracture Mechanics and Failure Analysis):**

- **Ductile Fracture:** Cup-and-cone, dimpled surface, microvoid coalescence, necking, **%RA** and **%EL** measure it. Slow crack growth. Safe failure mode.

- **Brittle Fracture:** Cleavage, flat shiny surface, no plastic deformation, fast crack propagation, **no warning**. Always catastrophic.

- **DBTT:** Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature. Determined by **Charpy V-notch test**. BCC metals (steel) have a clear DBTT; FCC metals (Al, Cu) do not. Lowering DBTT: finer grains, add nickel, temper, reduce strain rate.

- **Stress Concentration Factor:** $K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_0$. Always greater than 1 at a discontinuity.

- **Griffith Equation:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / (\pi a)}$. Applies to perfectly brittle (elastic) materials.

- **Modified Griffith (Orowan):** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p) / (\pi a)}$. Accounts for plastic work in metals.

- **Stress Intensity Factor:** $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$. Combines stress, crack length, and geometry.

- **Three Modes:** I (Opening, most critical) | II (Sliding) | III (Tearing).

- **Fracture Criterion:** $K_I \geq K_{IC}$ → fracture. $K_{IC}$ is a **material property**, units **MPa·√m**.

- **Critical Stress:** $\sigma_c = K_{IC} / (Y\sqrt{\pi a})$.

- **Critical Crack Length:** $a_c = (1/\pi)(K_{IC}/(Y\sigma))^2$. The squared relationship is **critical for design safety**.

- **Fatigue:** Failure under cyclic stress below yield. **3 stages:** Initiation → Propagation (beach marks) → Final fracture. **S-N curve** gives fatigue life. **Endurance limit** exists for steel (not for Al).

- **Creep:** Time-dependent deformation at $T > 0.4 T_m$. **3 stages:** Primary (decreasing rate) → Secondary (steady-state) → Tertiary (rupture). Power law: $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$.

- **Failure Analysis Procedure:** Visual inspection → NDT → Fracture surface analysis (SEM) → Identify type → Determine root cause → Recommend corrective action.

- **Engineering Design Philosophies:** *Safe-life* (no cracks allowed), *Damage-tolerant* (design assumes cracks; use fracture mechanics), *Fail-safe* (redundant load paths).

- **Killer Formulas to Memorize:** Griffith, Modified Griffith, $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$, $K_{IC}$ criterion, $a_c$ formula, Basquin, Power-law creep.

- **KTU 2024 Favourite Questions:** Derivation of Griffith equation; Numerical on $K_{IC}$ / $\sigma_c$ / $a_c$; DBTT with Charpy test; Three modes of fracture; S-N curve with endurance limit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies in Material Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET721 - ENGINEERING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies in Material Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Failure (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Material failure* is the loss of a material's load-bearing capacity or functional integrity due to fracture, excessive deformation, wear, corrosion, fatigue, or creep, occurring when the applied service stress exceeds the material's resistance under prevailing environmental and loading conditions.

A **failure analysis** is a systematic, multi-disciplinary forensic engineering investigation that determines the **root cause(s)** of in-service degradation. The objective is not only to assign blame, but to **redesign, retrofit, or reselect** materials so the same failure mode never recurs. According to KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic is anchored to:

- **CO4:** *Identify failure mechanisms and apply remedial strategies using appropriate engineering materials.*
- **CO5:** *Correlate microstructure–property–processing–performance relationships in real engineering systems.*

The standard taxonomy of material failure modes adopted by **ASM International** and **KTU 2024 syllabus** is:

1. **Fracture** (ductile, brittle, quasi-cleavage)
2. **Fatigue** (high-cycle, low-cycle, thermal)
3. **Creep** (dislocation, diffusional, grain-boundary sliding)
4. **Corrosion / Oxidation** (uniform, pitting, SCC, intergranular)
5. **Wear** (abrasive, adhesive, erosive, fretting)
6. **Thermal degradation** (phase change, melting, ablation)
7. **Radiation damage** (embrittlement, swelling, transmutation)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Autopsy of a Machine")

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Why study failed parts?**
> Think of a material failure as a **medical autopsy of a machine**. When a patient dies unexpectedly, the pathologist doesn't just say *"the heart stopped."* They dig deeper: was it a blocked artery, a virus, a congenital defect, or a medication side-effect? In the same way, an engineer examining a broken turbine blade asks: *Was the alloy wrong? Was the heat treatment skipped? Was the design cyclic? Was the environment hostile?*
> Every famous engineering disaster (Titanic, Hyatt Regency, Challenger) is a **textbook chapter** that taught humanity something textbooks couldn't. Studying these is **not history** — it is **the fastest way to internalize material science** because it is driven by tragedy, urgency, and undeniable physical evidence.

### 1.3 The KTU 2024 "Failure Pentagon" — Five Universal Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every catastrophic failure in engineering history can be traced to **one or more** of the following five root causes. Memorize this framework — it is a guaranteed 7–8 mark question in KTU Module 4.

| # | Driver | Engineering Meaning | Iconic Example |
|---|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Wrong Material** | Alloy/grade unsuitable for service conditions | Titanic hull plates (high-sulfur steel) |
| 2 | **Wrong Processing** | Improper heat treatment, welding, machining | Liberty Ships (as-welded brittle zones) |
| 3 | **Wrong Design** | Geometric stress concentration, no safety factor | Hyatt Regency walkway (double-loaded rod) |
| 4 | **Wrong Loading** | Unanticipated fatigue/cyclic/thermal load | Aloha Airlines (multiple fatigue cycles) |
| 5 | **Wrong Environment** | Corrosion, temperature, radiation neglected | Challenger O-rings (cold-stiffened rubber) |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Carries High Weightage

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **favor case-study questions** because they test **higher-order Bloom's levels** (Analyzing L4, Evaluating L5). You are not asked to *recall* Griffith's equation; you are asked to *apply* it to the Hyatt Regency collapse. This is why the 2024 scheme's Part B 14-mark questions almost always feature a **disaster + diagnosis + redesign** triplet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Failure Equation (Conceptual Backbone)

Every material failure, regardless of mode, can be expressed by a single conceptual equation that KTU examiners love to quote:

$$\text{Failure} \iff \sigma_{\text{applied}}(t) \;>\; \sigma_{\text{resist}}(t,\,T,\,\text{env})$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{\text{applied}}(t)$ is the **time-dependent service stress** (may be cyclic, thermal, or static).
- $\sigma_{\text{resist}}(t,\,T,\,\text{env})$ is the **time-, temperature-, and environment-dependent material strength**.

The right-hand side decays over time through **degradation mechanisms** (fatigue, corrosion, creep). The left-hand side fluctuates through **service loading**. The instant the two curves intersect — **failure is inevitable**.

---

## 2.2 The Eight Canonical Failure Mechanisms

### 2.2.1 Ductile Fracture
- High **% elongation** and **reduction in area** before rupture.
- **Micro-void coalescence**: voids nucleate at inclusions, grow, and merge.
- **Cup-and-cone** fracture surface (characteristic 45° shear lips).

### 2.2.2 Brittle Fracture
- **No plastic deformation**; sudden, catastrophic cleavage.
- Transgranular cleavage (bcc metals) or **intergranular** (hcp, ceramics).
- Triggered by **low temperature**, **high strain rate**, **triaxial stress state**.

### 2.2.3 Fatigue (High-Cycle & Low-Cycle)
- Failure under **cyclic stress** below the yield strength.
- Initiation at **persistent slip bands** → stage-I micro-crack → stage-II propagation (striations) → final fracture.
- **Basquin's law** governs HCF, **Coffin-Manson** governs LCF.

### 2.2.4 Creep
- Time-dependent **permanent deformation** at $T > 0.4\,T_m$.
- Three stages: primary (decelerating), secondary (steady-state), tertiary (runaway to rupture).
- **Norton's law** for steady-state creep rate.

### 2.2.5 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Synergistic** action of **tensile stress + corrosive environment + susceptible alloy** (e.g., Cl⁻ on austenitic SS, caustic on carbon steel).
- Branching "river-pattern" cracks.

### 2.2.6 Wear (Adhesive, Abrasive, Erosive, Fretting)
- Material loss from **surface interaction**.
- Fretting: small-amplitude oscillatory motion at contact surfaces (e.g., bolted joints, rivets).

### 2.2.7 Thermal Fatigue & Shock
- Stresses induced by **constrained thermal expansion**; spalling, cracking.

### 2.2.8 Radiation Damage
- Displacement cascades, helium embrittlement, void swelling (nuclear reactor internals).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Equations in One Place

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every formula here has appeared in KTU 2022/2023 University Examinations. The "Pitfall" column is your examiner's secret — lose marks on sign convention or units, and your answer is half-credited.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Units | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Nominal Stress | $\sigma = F/A$ | $F$ = force, $A$ = area | MPa | Use **engineering** stress, not true |
| 2 | Stress Concentration Factor | $K_t = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{nom}}$ | Geometric ratio | Dimensionless | Always $> 1$ for sharp notch |
| 3 | Safety Factor | $n = \sigma_{\text{yield}}/\sigma_{\text{working}}$ | Yield / working | Dimensionless | Use **ultimate** for brittle materials |
| 4 | Basquin's Law (HCF) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f\,(2N_f)^{b}$ | $\sigma'_f$ fatigue strength coef. | MPa | $b$ is **negative** ($-0.05$ to $-0.12$) |
| 5 | Coffin-Manson (LCF) | $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^{c}$ | $\varepsilon'_f$ ductility coef. | — | $c \approx -0.5$ to $-0.7$ |
| 6 | Paris Law (Crack Growth) | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^{m}$ | $C$, $m$ material constants | m/cycle | $m \approx 2$ to $4$ for metals |
| 7 | Stress Intensity Range | $\Delta K = Y\,\Delta\sigma\,\sqrt{\pi a}$ | $Y$ geometry factor | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | Crack length $a$ in meters |
| 8 | Norton's Creep Law | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A\,\sigma^{n}\,\exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | $A$, $n$, $Q_c$ constants | 1/s | $Q_c$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ |
| 9 | Monkman-Grant | $t_r \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}^{m'} = C_{MG}$ | Empirical constants | h, 1/s | Predicts rupture time |
| 10 | Goodman Fatigue | $\sigma_a/\sigma'_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_u = 1$ | $e$ endurance, $u$ ultimate | MPa | Conservatively safe |
| 11 | Griffith-Orowan | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s/(\pi a)}$ | $\gamma_s$ surface energy | J/m² | Brittle solids only |
| 12 | Pitting Resistance | $PRE = \%Cr + 3.3\%Mo + 16\%N$ | Composition | wt% | $> 32$ = "super" austenitic |

---

## 2.4 The Failure Analysis Workflow (ASM Protocol)

> [!NOTE]
> This **8-step protocol** is what professional failure analysts (and KTU examiners) expect. Memorize the order — it is worth 3–5 marks whenever a case study is asked.

1. **Background & Service History** — gather drawings, logs, operating conditions.
2. **Visual Examination** — naked eye + low-power stereo microscope.
3. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — dye-pen, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, radiography.
4. **Macroscopic Fractography** — locate origin, identify fracture mode.
5. **Microscopic Fractography (SEM)** — striations, dimples, cleavage facets.
6. **Chemical Analysis** — composition, inclusions, contaminants.
7. **Mechanical Testing** — hardness, tensile, impact, fatigue on adjacent material.
8. **Synthesis & Recommendation** — root cause, corrective action, redesign.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Failure analysis is the **cornerstone of safety-critical industries**:
- **Aerospace** (FAA mandates failure reporting for every turbine blade).
- **Nuclear** (post-Fukushima material re-qualification).
- **Pressure vessels & piping** (ASME Section XI).
- **Automotive** (NHTSA recalls driven by fatigue analysis).
- **Biomedical implants** (corrosion-fatigue of Ti-6Al-4V hip stems).

> [!TIP]
> The phrase **"Lessons Learned Report (LLR)"** is the deliverable. In KTU answers, you can earn easy marks by **always ending with a one-line corrective recommendation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stress Concentration Factor for an Elliptical Hole

This is the **single most-examined derivation** in KTU Module 4, and it underpins the Hyatt Regency and Liberty Ship cases.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider an infinite plate of width $W$ containing an elliptical hole of semi-major axis $a$ (perpendicular to load) and semi-minor axis $b$ (parallel to load). The remote stress is $\sigma_0$.

**Goal:** Derive the maximum stress at the tip of the ellipse.

### 3.1.2 Inglis's Classical Solution (1913)

Inglis solved the 2D elastic stress field using the **Airy stress function** $\phi$ for an elliptical hole. The result is exact in the limit $W \to \infty$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{2a}{b} \right)$$

**Step-by-step exposition:**

**Step 1** — Parametrize the elliptical boundary in curvilinear coordinates:
$$x = c \cosh\xi \cos\eta, \quad y = c \sinh\xi \sin\eta$$
where $c = \sqrt{a^2 - b^2}$ is the focal half-distance.

**Step 2** — The Airy function for a uniform far-field $\sigma_0$ acting on an ellipse is:
$$\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{4} \left( x^2 + y^2 \right) + \dfrac{\sigma_0 a^2 b^2}{2(x^2 + y^2 + c^2)} \cdot \text{cosh terms}$$

**Step 3** — Apply the **traction-free boundary condition** at the hole surface ($\sigma_{rr} = \tau_{r\theta} = 0$) and solve the biharmonic equation $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$.

**Step 4** — Evaluate $\sigma_{yy}$ at the point of maximum curvature, i.e., the **end of the major axis** ($\xi = 0$, $\eta = 0$).

**Step 5** — The dominant term gives:
$$\sigma_{yy}(\text{tip}) = \sigma_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{2a}{b} \right)$$

**Step 6** — Therefore, the **elastic stress concentration factor** is:
$$K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_0} = 1 + \dfrac{2a}{b}$$

### 3.1.3 Limiting Cases (Critical for KTU Numericals)

> [!NOTE]
> These two limits are asked in nearly every KTU paper:

**Case A: Circular hole** ($a = b = r$)
$$K_t = 1 + \dfrac{2r}{r} = 3$$

This is the famous result: a circular hole **triples** the local stress.

**Case B: Sharp crack** ($b \to 0$, fixed $a$)
$$K_t \to \infty$$

This means **a perfect crack has theoretically infinite stress** — engineering materials cannot tolerate sharp cracks. This is precisely why **fracture toughness $K_{IC}$** is used instead of stress for cracked bodies.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 10 mm wide steel plate contains a central elliptical hole with $a = 5$ mm, $b = 1$ mm. The plate is loaded in tension with $F = 50$ kN. Find the maximum local stress.

**Solution:**

Nominal stress (gross section):
$$\sigma_0 = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^3}{(0.010 - 0.002) \times t} \text{ (assume } t = 10 \text{ mm)}$$

$$\sigma_0 = \dfrac{50\,000}{0.008 \times 0.010} = 6.25 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 625 \text{ MPa}$$

Stress concentration factor:
$$K_t = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 5}{1} = 11$$

Maximum local stress:
$$\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_0 = 11 \times 625 = 6875 \text{ MPa} = 6.875 \text{ GPa}$$

This **vastly exceeds** the yield strength of any steel ($\sim 250$–$1500$ MPa) → **brittle fracture** guaranteed. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Basquin's High-Cycle Fatigue Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basquin's law** explains the Liberty Ship and Aloha Airlines cases. It connects cyclic stress amplitude to cycles-to-failure.

### 3.2.1 Empirical Origin

Plotting $\log \sigma_a$ vs. $\log N_f$ on the S-N (Wöhler) curve gives a **straight line** in the HCF regime ($N_f > 10^3$).

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Statement

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^{b}$$

**Step-by-step derivation from first principles:**

**Step 1** — Take logarithm of both sides:
$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \log(2N_f)$$

**Step 2** — Let $X = \log(2N_f)$ and $Y = \log \sigma_a$. The equation becomes $Y = bX + \log \sigma'_f$, a **linear equation** with slope $b$ and intercept $\log \sigma'_f$.

**Step 3** — Slope of S-N curve (negative):
$$b = \dfrac{\log \sigma_{a2} - \log \sigma_{a1}}{\log N_2 - \log N_1}$$

**Step 4** — For most metals: $b \in [-0.05, -0.12]$.

**Step 5** — When $2N_f = 1$ (i.e., one reversal), $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f$ → $\sigma'_f$ is the **fatigue strength coefficient**, approximately equal to the true fracture stress $\sigma_f$.

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical (Basquin)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 7075-T6 aluminum alloy has $\sigma'_f = 1500$ MPa and $b = -0.10$. Find the cycles to failure at a stress amplitude of $\sigma_a = 250$ MPa.

**Solution:**

$$250 = 1500 (2N_f)^{-0.10}$$

$$\left(\dfrac{1500}{250}\right) = (2N_f)^{0.10}$$

$$6 = (2N_f)^{0.10}$$

$$\ln 6 = 0.10 \ln(2N_f)$$

$$\ln(2N_f) = \dfrac{\ln 6}{0.10} = \dfrac{1.7918}{0.10} = 17.918$$

$$2N_f = e^{17.918} = 6.04 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 3.02 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles}}$$

This is well into the **endurance regime** — confirming that 7075-T6 can survive $\sim$30 million cycles at 250 MPa. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Paris Law for Fatigue Crack Propagation

This explains the Aloha Airlines, Silver Bridge, and Bhopal case studies where **initial defects grew subcritically** before catastrophic fast fracture.

### 3.3.1 Governing Equation

$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^{m}$$

where $\Delta K = K_{\max} - K_{\min} = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Integration (Useful for KTU Numericals)

**Step 1** — Separate variables:
$$da = C\,(Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^{m} dN = C\,Y^{m}(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\pi^{m/2}\,a^{m/2}\,dN$$

**Step 2** — Assume $Y$ and $\Delta\sigma$ are constant (long-crack, constant-amplitude loading):
$$a^{-m/2}\,da = C\,Y^{m}(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\pi^{m/2}\,dN$$

**Step 3** — Integrate from initial crack $a_i$ to critical crack $a_c$:
$$\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2}\,da = C\,Y^{m}(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\pi^{m/2}\int_{0}^{N_f} dN$$

**Step 4** — For $m \neq 2$:
$$\left[\dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}\right]_{a_i}^{a_c} = C\,Y^{m}(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\pi^{m/2}\,N_f$$

**Step 5** — Solve for cycles-to-failure:
$$N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{(m/2 - 1)\,C\,Y^{m}(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\pi^{m/2}}$$

**Step 6** — Critical crack length from fracture mechanics:
$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma_{\max}}\right)^{2}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical (Paris Law)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 2024-T3 aluminum fuselage skin has an initial crack $a_i = 1$ mm. Material constants: $K_{IC} = 30$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, $Y = 1$, $C = 2 \times 10^{-11}$, $m = 3$, $\Delta\sigma = 80$ MPa. Find cycles to reach $a_c$.

**Step 1 — Critical crack length:**
$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{30}{1 \times 80}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}(0.375)^{2} = \dfrac{0.1406}{\pi} = 0.0448 \text{ m} = 44.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Plug into Paris solution (with $m/2 = 1.5$, so $m/2 - 1 = 0.5$):**
$$N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{-0.5} - a_c^{-0.5}}{0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-11} \times 1 \times 80^3 \times \pi^{1.5}}$$

Numerator:
$$(0.001)^{-0.5} - (0.0448)^{-0.5} = 31.62 - 4.72 = 26.90 \text{ m}^{-0.5}$$

Denominator:
$$0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-11} \times 512000 \times 5.568 = 2.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{-0.5}/\text{cycle}$$

$$N_f = \dfrac{26.90}{2.85 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 9.44 \times 10^{5} \text{ cycles}$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 944\,000 \text{ cycles} \approx 9.4 \times 10^5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *exact* analytical approach used by NTSB investigators after the Aloha Airlines incident — they back-calculated the probable initial crack size from the measured striation spacing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Case Studies to Failure Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your **KTU exam cheat-sheet**. For each disaster, identify the **mode**, **driver**, and **one-line fix**. Examiners award full marks when all three are present.

| Case Study | Year | Material | Dominant Failure Mode | Root Cause | Corrective Fix |
|------------|------|----------|----------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **RMS Titanic** | 1912 | High-S hull steel | Brittle fracture (DBTT) | High S, P content; cold seawater | Modern normalized low-S steel |
| **Liberty Ships** | 1942–53 | ASTM A-7 ship plate | Brittle fracture | As-welded, no stress relief | Preheat, PWHT, notch-tough steel |
| **Silver Bridge** | 1967 | Carbon steel eyebar | Brittle fracture + SCC | Stress corrosion + low toughness | Periodic NDT, redundancy |
| **Hyatt Regency** | 1981 | A36 carbon steel rod | Tensile overload at thread | Design change doubled the load | Single continuous hanger rod |
| **Aloha 243** | 1988 | 2024-T3 Al skin | Corrosion-fatigue | Multi-site fatigue at rivets | Improved sealant, NDT program |
| **Challenger** | 1986 | Viton O-ring | Loss of elastomeric seal | Cold stiffening at 2 °C | Redesign joint, T constraints |
| **Piper Alpha** | 1988 | Carbon steel pipe | Crack + explosion | Wrong material for H₂S | Use of Cr-Mo alloy, gas detection |
| **Fukushima** | 2011 | Zr-alloy cladding | Steam-Zr reaction | High-T oxidation | Diverse cooling, fail-safe design |
| **Bhopal** | 1984 | Carbon steel tank | Corrosion + runaway | Water ingress into MIC | Inert atmosphere, scrubber |

---

## 3.5 The Five "KTU-Examiner Traps" in Case-Study Answers

> [!WARNING]
> These are the most common **mark-deduction** errors. Avoid them to secure full 14/14:

1. **Calling every crack "brittle fracture"** — distinguish **cleavage** from **intergranular** vs. **dimpled (ductile)**.
2. **Forgetting units** — Paris Law $da/dN$ is in m/cycle, not mm/cycle.
3. **Confusing $K_t$ (geometric) with $K_{IC}$ (material)** — they are not the same.
4. **Missing the corrective recommendation** — KTU marking scheme allots ~2 marks for the "lessons learned" line.
5. **Ignoring the environment** — Titanic + Challenger are useless without mentioning temperature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Universal Failure-Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Failed Component Received] --> B{Visual & Macroscopic<br/>Examination}
    B --> C{Fracture Surface<br/>Type?}
    C -->|Dimpled, cup-cone| D[Ductile Fracture<br/>- Check overload<br/>- Check %RA]
    C -->|Cleavage facets,<br/>river patterns| E[Brittle Fracture<br/>- Check DBTT<br/>- Check notch]
    C -->|Beach marks,<br/>striations| F[Fatigue Failure<br/>- S-N analysis<br/>- Paris Law]
    C -->|Intergranular,<br/>branching cracks| G[Stress Corrosion<br/>Cracking]
    C -->|Bulging, oxidation,<br/>multi-cavity| H[Creep Failure<br/>- Norton plot]

    D --> I[Root Cause Synthesis]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J{Mitigation Possible?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Corrective Action<br/>- Redesign<br/>- Reselect material<br/>- Improve process]
    J -->|No| L[Accept & Monitor<br/>with NDT]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Five Failure Pentagon Drivers and Their Real Cases

```mermaid
graph TD
    Pentagon[Five Drivers of Material Failure] --> D1[Wrong Material]
    Pentagon --> D2[Wrong Processing]
    Pentagon --> D3[Wrong Design]
    Pentagon --> D4[Wrong Loading]
    Pentagon --> D5[Wrong Environment]

    D1 --> C1[Case: Titanic<br/>High-S steel hull]
    D2 --> C2[Case: Liberty Ships<br/>As-welded plates]
    D3 --> C3[Case: Hyatt Regency<br/>Double-load design]
    D4 --> C4[Case: Aloha Airlines<br/>Cyclic pressurization]
    D5 --> C5[Case: Challenger<br/>Cold stiffening]

    C1 --> Lesson[Universal Lesson<br/>Match Material to Service]
    C2 --> Lesson
    C3 --> Lesson
    C4 --> Lesson
    C5 --> Lesson

    style Pentagon fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style Lesson fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Fatigue Crack Propagation Lifecycle (3-Stage)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Stage I: Crack Initiation<br/>at persistent slip bands<br/>Length: 1-5 grains] --> S2
    S2[Stage II: Stable Propagation<br/>Paris Law regime<br/>Visible striations] --> S3
    S3[Stage III: Unstable Fracture<br/>da/dN diverges<br/>K approaches K_IC] --> S4[Final Catastrophic<br/>Separation]

    style S1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    style S2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    style S3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    style S4 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBTT) Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BCC[Body-Centered Cubic Metals]
        H[High Temperature<br/>Austenitic-like ductility<br/>Charpy Energy HIGH] --> T[Transition Region<br/>Energy falls sharply<br/>Mixed mode] --> L[Low Temperature<br/>Cleavage fracture<br/>Charpy Energy LOW]
    end

    T -.->|Favors brittle| Titanic[Case: Titanic at -2 C]
    T -.->|Favors brittle| Liberty[Case: Liberty Ships in N. Atlantic]
    L -.->|Most susceptible| Titanic
    L -.->|Most susceptible| Liberty

    H -.->|Safe regime| Safe[Engineering Design<br/>Keep T > DBTT + safety margin]

    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    style T fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    style L fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px

```

## 4.5 The KTU "Cause-Cure" Cross-Mapping Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Fracture] --> M1{By Mode?}
    M1 -->|Ductile| R1[Reduce stress<br/>OR use tougher alloy]
    M1 -->|Brittle| R2[Raise temp<br/>OR notch-tough steel<br/>OR reduce stress triaxiality]
    M1 -->|Fatigue| R3[Lower cyclic stress<br/>OR shot peen<br/>OR NDT schedule]
    M1 -->|Creep| R4[Lower T OR use<br/>superalloy]
    M1 -->|SCC| R5[Remove aggressive species<br/>OR compressive shot peen]

    style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams in an exam:** If the question provides a stress value and a temperature, trace the DBTT map to know if you're in the **safe (green)**, **transition (yellow)**, or **brittle (red)** zone. This is the fastest way to earn the "diagnosis" marks.
