# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**Differentiate between ductile and brittle fracture on the basis of (i) plastic deformation, (ii) fracture surface appearance, and (iii) energy absorption.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Ductile Fracture | Brittle Fracture |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **(i) Plastic deformation** | Significant (elongation > 5%) | Negligible (elongation < 2%) |
| **(ii) Fracture surface** | Dimpled, fibrous, cup-and-cone | Cleavage facets, river patterns, shiny |
| **(iii) Energy absorbed** | High (Charpy energy > 100 J) | Low (Charpy energy < 20 J) |

Additionally, ductile fracture is **temperature-insensitive** in the upper shelf region, while brittle fracture is highly sensitive to **low temperatures, high strain rates, and triaxial stress states**. **Micro-mechanism:** ductile = micro-void coalescence; brittle = cleavage along specific crystallographic planes {100} in bcc. ✅

---

### Q2. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**What is a ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)? Why is it critical for ships operating in the North Atlantic?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is the temperature below which a body-centered cubic (bcc) material — typically ferritic steel — fractures in a **brittle (cleavage) manner** rather than a **ductile (fibrous) manner**. It is identified on a Charpy V-notch impact energy vs. temperature plot, conventionally at **20 J** (or 27 J for offshore standards).

The DBTT is the single most important parameter for **North Atlantic ship design** because:

- Seawater temperatures drop to **$-2$°C** in winter.
- The hull steel must remain **above its DBTT** to avoid catastrophic brittle fracture.
- A high DBTT combined with **sulfur embrittlement** and **stress concentrations** (rivet holes, weld defects) is the precise reason the **Titanic and Liberty Ships** failed.

Modern ASTM A36 / A131 normalized ship plate is specified with **Charpy impact tested at $-40$°C ≥ 27 J** to guarantee the transition is well below service temperature. ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule (2024 Scheme):** You will be given **one Part B question (14 marks)** with two alternative choices. **Attempt either Option A or Option B** in full. Both are provided below for your reference.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the ductile-to-brittle transition in bcc metals. How did the failure of the Liberty Ships in the 1940s highlight the importance of this phenomenon?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A thick steel plate contains a central elliptical hole of semi-major axis $a = 8$ mm and semi-minor axis $b = 2$ mm. The plate is 30 mm wide, 10 mm thick, and loaded in tension with $F = 120$ kN. Calculate: (i) the nominal stress, (ii) the stress concentration factor, and (iii) the maximum stress at the hole tip. If the steel has a yield strength of 350 MPa, will the plate yield locally?* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Sketch (3 marks):**

A Charpy impact energy vs. temperature plot showing the three zones:

- **Lower shelf** (brittle, low energy)
- **Transition region** (steep drop)
- **Upper shelf** (ductile, high energy)

DBTT is marked at a standard energy level (e.g., 20 J).

**Step 2 — Explanation of mechanism (3 marks):**

In **bcc** metals (e.g., ferritic steel), the **Peierls-Nabarro stress** that moves dislocations is highly **temperature-sensitive**. Above DBTT, dislocation slip is easy → plastic flow. Below DBTT, the yield stress exceeds the cleavage stress, and the material fractures by **cleavage along {100} planes** with virtually no plasticity.

**Step 3 — Liberty Ships connection (1 mark):**

In WWII, 2,710 Liberty Ships were built using ASTM A-7 steel plates joined by **welds (a new technology at the time)**. Three problems combined:

1. **High sulfur content** (0.04–0.07%) raised DBTT.
2. **Weld-induced HAZ** had coarse grains → low notch toughness.
3. **No preheating or post-weld heat treatment** → high residual stress.

Ships like the **SS Schenectady** (1943) **split in half at the dock** in $-6$°C weather — the most famous example of brittle fracture in history. The U.S. Navy and TWI subsequently mandated **preheating, PWHT, and notch-tough steel grades**, which is why modern ships do not fail this way.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $a = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m
- $b = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m
- $W = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m, $t = 10$ mm $= 0.010$ m
- $F = 120$ kN $= 120\,000$ N
- $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa

**Step 1 — Nominal (gross) stress [2 marks]:**

$$\sigma_0 = \dfrac{F}{W \cdot t} = \dfrac{120\,000}{0.030 \times 0.010} = 4.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 400 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Stress concentration factor [2 marks]:**

$$K_t = 1 + \dfrac{2a}{b} = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 8}{2} = 1 + 8 = 9$$

**Step 3 — Maximum stress [2 marks]:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_0 = 9 \times 400 = 3600 \text{ MPa} = 3.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion [1 mark]:**

Since $\sigma_{\max} = 3600 \text{ MPa} \gg \sigma_y = 350 \text{ MPa}$, the plate **will definitely yield locally** at the hole tip. In a real bcc steel at low temperature, it will **cleave (brittle fracture)** because the local stress vastly exceeds the cleavage stress.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Q-A):** Many students write $K_t = 1 + a/b$ (forgetting the factor of 2). The correct Inglis formula is $\boxed{K_t = 1 + 2a/b}$. Also, use **gross section** for $\sigma_0$, not net section. Net section is for **average** post-notch stress, not for $K_t$ definition.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]*

**(a)** *Discuss the Hyatt Regency walkway collapse of 1981 as a case study in design-induced material failure. What were the material and design deficiencies? What changes in the design would have prevented the disaster?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 2024-T3 aluminum component is subjected to a fully-reversed cyclic stress of amplitude $\sigma_a = 200$ MPa. The material follows Basquin's law with $\sigma'_f = 1200$ MPa and $b = -0.09$. Estimate the number of cycles to failure. If a compressive residual stress of $-80$ MPa is introduced by shot peening, recalculate the cycles to failure. Comment on the improvement.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) [7 marks]

**Background [2 marks]:**

On **17 July 1981**, two suspended walkways in the Hyatt Regency Hotel, Kansas City, collapsed during a tea dance, killing **114 people and injuring 216**. The failure originated at the **suspension rod-to-beam connection** on the fourth floor.

**Original design (correct) [2 marks]:**

A single continuous rod passed through the ceiling and supported the fourth-floor walkway, with the second-floor walkway suspended from the fourth-floor beam via a **separate short rod** (Figure). Each rod carried the load of **only one walkway** ≈ 30 kN.

**Changed design (fatal) [2 marks]:**

To simplify construction, the contractor (with structural engineer approval) replaced the two-rod system with a **single continuous rod that supported BOTH walkways at the fourth-floor ceiling connection**. This **doubled the tensile load on the upper connection** to ≈ 60 kN.

The original design used a **single C-shaped nut and washer** capable of ≈ 30 kN. The new design required the connection to carry **60 kN on a single nut-and-thread system with a 45° threaded rod that was only 1 inch in diameter**.

The combined effect of:
- Doubling the load,
- A non-standard threaded connection,
- Carbon steel A36 with limited ductility at the notch (thread root), and
- Geometric stress concentration at the threads ($K_t \approx 3$–$4$)

…led to **brittle overload fracture** at the connection.

**Lessons and corrections [1 mark]:**

- **Never change a load path** without recalculation and re-certification.
- Use **continuous rod of adequate cross-section** ($A \geq F/\sigma_{\text{allow}}$).
- Specify **forged eye-bars or redundant systems** for human-occupied structures.
- The Hyatt collapse led to **mandatory peer-review** in most U.S. states.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Cycles to failure without shot peening [3 marks]:**

Basquin's law:
$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^{b}$$

$$200 = 1200 (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

$$\left(\dfrac{200}{1200}\right) = (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{6} = (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

$$6 = (2N_f)^{0.09}$$

$$\ln 6 = 0.09 \ln(2N_f)$$

$$\ln(2N_f) = \dfrac{\ln 6}{0.09} = \dfrac{1.7918}{0.09} = 19.909$$

$$2N_f = e^{19.909} = 4.46 \times 10^{8}$$

$$N_f \approx 2.23 \times 10^{8} \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Cycles to failure with shot peening [3 marks]:**

Shot peening introduces a **compressive residual stress** $\sigma_r = -80$ MPa. This shifts the **mean stress** to a more compressive value. The effective stress amplitude (using Goodman/Soderberg mean-stress correction, simplified):

$$\sigma_{a,\text{eff}} = \sigma_a - \vert\sigma_r\vert = 200 - 80 = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

$$120 = 1200 (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

$$\dfrac{120}{1200} = (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{10} = (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

$$10 = (2N_f)^{0.09}$$

$$\ln(2N_f) = \dfrac{\ln 10}{0.09} = \dfrac{2.3026}{0.09} = 25.585$$

$$2N_f = e^{25.585} = 1.33 \times 10^{11}$$

$$N_f \approx 6.65 \times 10^{10} \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Comment [1 mark]:**

Shot peening increases fatigue life by a factor of $\dfrac{6.65 \times 10^{10}}{2.23 \times 10^{8}} \approx 298\times$, i.e., nearly **three orders of magnitude**. This is precisely why shot peening is standard for **aircraft landing gear, turbine blades, and welded steel bridges** — it is the most cost-effective surface treatment for HCF resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Q-B):**
> 1. Don't forget to halve the equation — Basquin uses **$2N_f$** (reversals), not $N_f$ (cycles). This is the **single most common error** in Basquin problems.
> 2. For shot peening, the **residual stress is compressive**, so it **reduces** the effective cyclic amplitude. Do not add it as if it were tensile.
> 3. Always quote the **factor of improvement**, not just the new $N_f$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — The "One-Page Revision" Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-week revision sheet for KTU Module 4. Read this the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Material failure** = loss of load-bearing capacity via fracture, fatigue, creep, corrosion, or wear.
- [x] **Ductile fracture** = micro-void coalescence, dimples, cup-and-cone; high energy.
- [x] **Brittle fracture** = cleavage, river patterns, low energy, no plasticity.
- [x] **DBTT** = temperature below which bcc metals become brittle; critical for ships in cold seas.
- [x] **Stress concentration**: $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$ (ellipse), $K_t = 3$ (circular hole).
- [x] **Basquin's law** (HCF): $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$, with $b \in [-0.05, -0.12]$.
- [x] **Coffin-Manson** (LCF): $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$.
- [x] **Paris Law** (crack growth): $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$, with $m \approx 2$–$4$.
- [x] **Creep law** (Norton): $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$.
- [x] **Goodman**: $\sigma_a/\sigma'_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_u = 1$ — for finite-life design with mean stress.
- [x] **PRE** = %Cr + 3.3%Mo + 16%N — ranks stainless steel pitting resistance.
- [x] **SCC** requires three conditions: susceptible alloy + tensile stress + corrosive environment.
- [x] **Titanic** — high-S steel, cold seawater, brittle fracture.
- [x] **Liberty Ships** — as-welded A-7, no preheat, brittle fracture.
- [x] **Hyatt Regency** — design change doubled load, threaded rod fracture.
- [x] **Aloha 243** — multi-site fatigue, corrosion-fatigue, 2024-T3.
- [x] **Silver Bridge** — eyebar SCC, low-toughness carbon steel.
- [x] **Challenger** — Viton O-ring lost elasticity at 2 °C cold soak.
- [x] **Piper Alpha** — wrong material for H₂S, no leak detection.
- [x] **Fukushima** — Zr-alloy steam reaction, loss of cooling.
- [x] **Bhopal** — MIC corrosion, water ingress, runaway reaction.
- [x] **Always end** your case-study answer with a **one-line "Lessons Learned"** to grab the final 2 marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last tip:** KTU's 2024 marking scheme rewards **specific numerical values, units, and material designations** (e.g., "2024-T3," "ASTM A36," "$K_{IC} = 30$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$"). Vague answers lose marks. Quantify everything you can.
